It encapsulates Layer 2 Ethernet frames inside **UDP/IP packets** (typically using UDP port **4789**), thereby leveraging the existing TCP/IP routed infrastructure to extend Layer 2 networks over a Layer 3 underlay.

**Encapsulation Process (3 Marks):**
1. Original Ethernet frame from VM arrives at the hypervisor virtual switch (e.g., Open vSwitch).
2. A VXLAN header is prepended.
3. The packet is wrapped in a **UDP header** (outer UDP, port 4789).
4. An **outer IP header** is added with the addresses of the source and destination VTEPs (VXLAN Tunnel Endpoints).
5. An **outer Ethernet header** is added for physical transport.

**Encapsulation Overhead & MTU Impact (2 Marks):**
The total overhead is approximately **50 bytes** (8 bytes UDP + 20 bytes outer IP + 14 bytes outer Ethernet + 8 bytes VXLAN). Because the standard Ethernet MTU is **1500 bytes**, the effective payload MTU is reduced to **1450 bytes** to avoid fragmentation. Cloud admins must configure jumbo frames (MTU 9000) on underlay networks to compensate.

**Example (1 Mark):** AWS VPC uses VXLAN-like encapsulation (with the AWS Nitro System) to provide isolated networks to millions of customers over a shared physical infrastructure.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**
*(a)* Discuss the role of TCP/IP in enabling three major cloud computing services: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. Provide specific protocol examples for each. **(7 Marks)**

*(b)* A cloud provider's inter-region link operates at 1 Gbps with an RTT of 100 ms. The packet loss probability is 0.0001. Using the Mathis formula, calculate the maximum TCP throughput. Comment on whether window scaling is needed, justifying your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TCP/IP in IaaS, PaaS, SaaS (7 Marks):**

1. **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) (2.5 Marks):**
   - VM migration (vMotion, live migration) uses TCP to transfer memory state and disk images between physical hosts without downtime.
   - Block storage backends (e.g., AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disks) use **iSCSI** or **NVMe-over-TCP** to deliver persistent storage to VMs over the network.
   - Example protocol: **TCP port 3260 (iSCSI)** for storage, **TCP port 22 (SSH)** for VM console access.

2. **PaaS (Platform as a Service) (2.5 Marks):**
   - The Kubernetes API server exposes its control plane over **HTTPS (TCP 6443)**.
   - Service-to-service communication in modern PaaS uses **gRPC over HTTP/2 (TCP)**, enabling streaming and bidirectional calls.
   - Database-as-a-Service connections (e.g., Amazon RDS) use native database protocols over TCP: MySQL on **3306**, PostgreSQL on **5432**.

3. **SaaS (Software as a Service) (2 Marks):**
   - End users access SaaS applications (Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce) via **HTTPS (TCP 443)** in their browsers.
   - OAuth 2.0 authentication tokens are exchanged over **HTTPS** with the identity provider.
   - WebSockets (over TCP) enable real-time collaboration features in SaaS apps (e.g., Google Docs live editing).

**(b) Throughput Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth = 1 Gbps
- RTT = 100 ms = 0.1 s
- Packet loss $p = 0.0001$
- Assume MSS = 1460 bytes (standard Ethernet)

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Apply the Mathis formula:

$$B \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the loss factor:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0001}} = \frac{1}{0.01} = 100$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute the segment rate:

$$\frac{MSS}{RTT} = \frac{1460 \text{ bytes}}{0.1 \text{ s}} = 14600 \text{ bytes/sec} = 116800 \text{ bits/sec}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Final throughput:

$$B = 116800 \times 100 = 11{,}680{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 11.68 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Window scaling analysis:

$$BDP = 1 \times 10^9 \text{ bps} \times 0.1 \text{ s} = 10^8 \text{ bits} = 12.5 \text{ MB}$$

Default TCP window = 64 KB = 0.0625 MB.

Since $BDP (12.5 \text{ MB}) \gg W_{default} (0.0625 \text{ MB})$, **window scaling is mandatory**. Without it, the throughput would be limited to ~0.5 Gbps instead of utilizing the full 1 Gbps link. Window scaling with a factor of 8 expands the window to ~16 MB, comfortably exceeding the BDP.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The 0.01% packet loss caps TCP throughput at ~11.68 Mbps on this 1 Gbps link, and window scaling is required to approach the link's full capacity. Cloud providers mitigate this by deploying **private, lossless backbones** and using **QUIC (UDP-based)** alternatives for latency-sensitive workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing TCP/IP with the OSI model.** TCP/IP has **4 layers**; OSI has 7. Writing "session layer" or "presentation layer" in TCP/IP context is a mark-deduction trap. Stick to: Application, Transport, Internet, Link.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units in the Mathis formula.** MSS is in **bytes**, but throughput should be expressed in **bits per second (bps)**. Always multiply by 8.
> 3. **Not mentioning multi-tenancy.** When asked about cloud, students often give generic networking answers. Always tie your answer to **isolation, virtualization, and scalability** — the cloud-specific keywords.
> 4. **Ignoring VXLAN/overlay terminology.** Module 2 expects knowledge of **VXLAN, VLAN, VPC peering, and SDN**. A pure TCP/IP answer without cloud overlay context will lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **In Part B sub-parts, students often write a generic answer for (a) and skip (b).** Both sub-parts are **compulsory** and carry **7 marks each**. Skipping one means losing half the question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TCP/IP is the backbone of all cloud communication** — both North-South (user-to-cloud) and East-West (VM-to-VM, Pod-to-Pod).
- The **4-layer TCP/IP model** is: Application, Transport, Internet, Link. Memorize the protocol examples for each.
- **TCP** provides reliability (handshake, ACKs, flow/congestion control). **UDP** provides speed (used in DNS, VoIP, video streaming, VXLAN).
- **VXLAN** is built on **UDP/IP** and allows **16 million logical networks** (vs. 4096 for VLAN). Default UDP port: **4789**.
- **CIDR-based subnetting** is used to carve VPC IP space. The number of usable hosts = $2^{(32 - n)} - \text{reserved}$.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** determines required TCP window size. On long-RTT, high-bandwidth cloud links, **window scaling** is mandatory.
- **Mathis Formula:** $B \approx \frac{MSS}{RTT} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{p}}$ — useful for calculating maximum TCP throughput under packet loss.
- **Cloud service models (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS/FaaS)** all rely on TCP/IP for their respective communication needs.
- **Default ports to memorize:** HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, SSH=22, DNS=53, iSCSI=3260, MySQL=3306, PostgreSQL=5432, Kubernetes API=6443, VXLAN=4789.
- **MTU:** Standard Ethernet = **1500 bytes**; VXLAN-encapsulated = **1450 bytes** effective payload.
- **Cloud networking keywords to use in exams:** VPC, subnet, security group, NACL, load balancer, NAT gateway, VPC peering, transit gateway, service mesh, east-west traffic, north-south traffic, multi-tenancy, overlay/underlay.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DNS and DHCP in the Cloud: Configuration in Cloud Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** DNS and DHCP in the Cloud: Configuration in Cloud Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 DNS in Cloud Networks — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud DNS** is a highly available, scalable, and managed Domain Name System (DNS) web service that translates human-readable domain names (e.g., `www.ktu.edu.in`) into machine-readable IP addresses (e.g., `203.0.113.10`) within or across cloud environments. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Cloud DNS** extends RFC 1035 / RFC 2782 (SRV records) implementations to provide programmable, API-driven, Anycast-based, geo-distributed, SLA-backed (>99.99% availability) name resolution services such as **Amazon Route 53**, **Azure DNS**, and **Google Cloud DNS**.

### Key Cloud DNS Characteristics
- **Anycast Networking**: A single IP advertised from multiple geographically dispersed points-of-presence (PoPs), ensuring the nearest resolver handles the query.
- **Programmable via API**: All record changes are REST-API driven, enabling **Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC)** via Terraform, CloudFormation, or Bicep.
- **Pay-per-query billing model** (e.g., **$0.40 per million queries** on Route 53 standard queries).
- **Health-check integration** for automated traffic failover.
- **Latency standard**: Global resolution under **30 ms** for Anycast-populated regions.

## 1.2 DHCP in Cloud Networks — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud DHCP** refers to the **automated, dynamic allocation of IPv4 and IPv6 addressing parameters** (IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers, domain name) to cloud-hosted virtual machines (VMs), containers, and serverless workloads at boot time, conforming to **RFC 2131 (DHCPv4)** and **RFC 8415 (DHCPv6)**. Cloud DHCP services operate as a **managed, redundant, control-plane function** — not a network-resident protocol between the VM and a per-subnet server in the customer data path.

### Key Cloud DHCP Characteristics
- **Stateless / Stateful hybrid**: Cloud DHCP is mostly **stateless** from the customer's perspective (no lease state to manage).
- **Default lease time**: Effectively **infinite** while the VM/ENI is attached; the IP is released only on instance termination (configurable).
- **DHCP Options Sets / Configurations**: Allow customization of DNS servers, domain name, NTP servers.
- **Reserved / Static IPs** can be allocated via API before VM creation (e.g., AWS Elastic IPs).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **DNS in the Cloud = Global Phone Directory on Steroids**
> Imagine a phone directory that updates itself **instantly** the moment a new contact is added (via an API), has **copies in every city** you might call from (Anycast), and can **automatically reroute** your call to a backup number if the first person doesn't answer (health-check failover). That's Cloud DNS.

> [!NOTE]
> **DHCP in the Cloud = A Hotel Key Card System**
> When you check into a hotel (boot a VM), the front desk (cloud control plane) hands you a key card with a **room number** (IP), **floor info** (subnet), and **Wi-Fi credentials** (DNS/NTP servers). You don't pick the room — the system assigns one based on availability. If you extend your stay, the key is renewed silently. If you check out, the room is instantly reassigned.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping

| Concept | RFC / Standard | Cloud Provider Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Authoritative DNS | RFC 1035, RFC 2308 | Route 53 Hosted Zones, Azure DNS Zones, Cloud DNS Managed Zones |
| DNS Routing Policies | RFC 6672 (DNAME) | Latency / Weighted / Geolocation / Failover / Multi-Value |
| Dynamic Updates | RFC 2136 | Route 53 Traffic Flow, Azure Private DNS Auto-Registration |
| DHCPv4 | RFC 2131 | AWS DHCP Options Set, Azure DHCP (implicit), GCP Subnet DHCP |
| DHCPv6 | RFC 8415 | AWS IPv6 PD, Azure IPv6, GCP IPv6 |
| Health Checks | DNS EDNS (RFC 6891) | Route 53 Health Checks, Azure Traffic Manager Probes |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember the **4-2-1 rule** for DNS: 4 root servers logically (a-m.root-servers.net), 2 TLDs per query path, 1 authoritative server per zone. Cloud DNS typically consolidates the "1" into a globally distributed Anycast fleet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of Cloud DNS

Cloud DNS operates across **three logical planes**:

1. **Control Plane (API Plane)**: Receives REST API calls to create/update/delete Record Sets and Hosted Zones. Stateless, horizontally scaled.
2. **Data Plane (Resolver Plane)**: Serves DNS queries using Anycast. Reads from a globally consistent eventually-consistent store.
3. **Health-Check Plane (Probe Plane)**: Distributed probes that issue HTTP/HTTPS/TCP checks from multiple global locations to determine endpoint health.

### 2.1.1 DNS Record Types — KTU High-Yield Reference

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU exam question on cloud DNS tests your ability to pick the **correct record type** for a given scenario. Master this table.

| Record Type | RFC | Purpose | Cloud Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | RFC 1035 | Maps hostname → IPv4 (32-bit) | `web.ktu.in A 10.0.1.5` |
| **AAAA** | RFC 3596 | Maps hostname → IPv6 (128-bit) | `web.ktu.in AAAA 2001:db8::5` |
| **CNAME** | RFC 1035 | Alias of one name to another (cannot coexist with other records at apex) | `www.ktu.in CNAME web.ktu.in` |
| **MX** | RFC 1035 | Mail exchange with priority (lower = preferred) | `@ MX 10 mail.ktu.in` |
| **TXT** | RFC 1035 | Arbitrary text (SPF, DKIM, domain verification) | `@ TXT "v=spf1 -all"` |
| **NS** | RFC 1035 | Delegates a zone to authoritative name servers | `ktu.in NS ns-1.awsdns-01.com` |
| **SOA** | RFC 1035 | Start of Authority — zone admin, serial, refresh, retry, expire, minimum TTL | `_ktu.in SOA ns.awsdns-01.com admin.ktu.in 1 7200 900 1209600 86400` |
| **PTR** | RFC 1035 | Reverse DNS — IP → hostname | `5.1.0.10.in-addr.arpa PTR web.ktu.in` |
| **SRV** | RFC 2782 | Service location (host, port, priority, weight) | `_sip._tcp.ktu.in SRV 10 60 5060 sip.ktu.in` |
| **CAA** | RFC 6844 | Restricts which CAs can issue certs for domain | `@ CAA 0 issue "letsencrypt.org"` |
| **ALIAS / ANAME** | Provider-specific | CNAME-like behavior at zone apex | Route 53 ALIAS for `ktu.in` → ELB |

### 2.1.2 SOA Timer Semantics (in seconds)

$$\text{Refresh} = 7200, \quad \text{Retry} = 900, \quad \text{Expire} = 1209600, \quad \text{Minimum TTL} = 86400$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Serial Number** must increment on every zone change. Secondary servers poll at `Refresh` interval. If a primary's serial is unreachable for `Expire` seconds, the secondary stops responding authoritatively.

## 2.2 DNS Routing Policies in the Cloud

Cloud DNS providers offer **policy-based routing** beyond simple round-robin:

### 2.2.1 Routing Policy Comparison

| Policy | Selection Logic | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple** | Random within record set | Single-region apps |
| **Weighted** | Probabilistic (weights in % or 0–255) | A/B testing, canary deployments |
| **Latency-Based** | Lowest network RTT from resolver to AWS region | Multi-region active-active |
| **Failover** | Primary if healthy, else secondary | Disaster Recovery (DR) |
| **Geolocation** | Continent/country/US-state of client IP (coarse) | Content localization, compliance |
| **Geoproximity** | Bias-based distance using traffic-flow dials (Route 53 only) | GDPR data residency |
| **Multi-Value** | Up to 8 healthy records returned randomly | Client-side load balancing |

### 2.2.2 Weighted Routing Math

Given weights $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ for $n$ records, the probability of selection is:

$$P(\text{record}_i) = \frac{w_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}$$

For example, with two records weighted **70** and **30**:

$$P_1 = \frac{70}{70+30} = 0.70 = 70\%, \quad P_2 = \frac{30}{70+30} = 0.30 = 30\%$$

## 2.3 Latency-Based Routing — Network RTT Formulation

Latency routing selects the region with the **lowest estimated TCP-layer RTT**. AWS computes this as:

$$\text{RTT}_{\text{resolver} \to \text{region}_i} = \text{PropDelay} + \text{QueueDelay} + \text{TransDelay} + \text{ProcDelay}$$

Cloud DNS uses historical active-probe data from health-check planes; the resolver-region pair with the minimum measured RTT wins. **Note:** Latency is *not* geographic distance — it is end-to-end network performance.

## 2.4 DHCP Option Codes (RFC 2132) — The KTU Cheat Sheet

| Code | Name | Length | Cloud Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Subnet Mask | 4 octets | Derived from VPC/Subnet CIDR |
| **3** | Router (Default Gateway) | $n \times 4$ octets | Subnet's first reserved IP (e.g., `10.0.1.1`) |
| **6** | Domain Name Server | $n \times 4$ octets | AWS VPC base = `10.0.0.2`; custom via Options Set |
| **15** | Domain Name | $n$ octets | `ec2.internal` (AWS) / `azure.local` (Azure) / `c.<project>.internal` (GCP) |
| **42** | NTP Servers | $n \times 4$ octets | `169.254.169.123` (AWS) / GCP NTP metadata |
| **51** | IP Address Lease Time | 4 octets | Effectively infinite in cloud |
| **58** | T1 (Renewal Time) | 4 octets | Computed as 0.5 × lease |
| **59** | T2 (Rebinding Time) | 4 octets | Computed as 0.875 × lease |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** In AWS, the default VPC DHCP Options Set always includes **`AmazonProvidedDNS` at `10.0.0.2`** (or the VPC base + 2). This IP is a **virtualized, hypervisor-resolved** address — it is not a real ENI on any instance.

## 2.5 Cloud DHCP Architecture

### 2.5.1 AWS DHCP Flow
1. EC2 instance boots → hypervisor intercepts **DHCPDISCOVER**.
2. Hypervisor contacts **AWS DHCP control plane** via internal API (not customer data path).
3. Control plane returns IP, subnet, gateway, DNS servers, domain name.
4. Hypervisor constructs **DHCPOFFER** with response, hands to guest OS.
5. Guest OS sends **DHCPREQUEST** → **DHCPACK** exchange (transparent to user).

### 2.5.2 Azure DHCP Flow
- Azure does **not** use RFC 2131 DHCP at all. Instead, the **Azure DHCP service** assigns IPs from the subnet range, and the guest OS obtains them via the **Azure wire server** (`168.63.129.16` — a special link-local metadata IP).
- This is an **Azure-specific optimization** to avoid broadcast traffic in overlay networks.

### 2.5.3 GCP DHCP Flow
- Similar to AWS — hypervisor intercepts DHCP and resolves via the **GCP metadata server (`169.254.169.254`)**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Domain | Formula / Rule | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| DNS weighted prob. | $P_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum w_j}$ | dimensionless | Weights are integers 0–255 |
| SOA refresh | $T_{\text{refresh}}$ | seconds | Default **7200** |
| SOA retry | $T_{\text{retry}}$ | seconds | Default **900** |
| SOA expire | $T_{\text{expire}}$ | seconds | Default **1209600** (14 days) |
| SOA minimum | $T_{\text{minimum}}$ | seconds | Default **86400** (negative cache TTL) |
| DHCP lease ratio | $T_1 = 0.5 \times L$, $T_2 = 0.875 \times L$ | seconds | RFC 2131 |
| IPv4 host bits | $H = 32 - n$ for /n prefix | bits | E.g., /24 → 8 host bits → 254 usable |
| IPv4 usable hosts | $U = 2^{H} - 2$ | count | AWS reserves **3 + 5** = 5 in default VPCs |
| AWS reserved IPs | $\text{Reserved} = 5$ (default VPC) | count | Network, gateway, DNS, future, broadcast |
| Azure reserved IPs | $\text{Reserved} = 5$ | count | Network, gateway, DNS, future, broadcast |
| Anycast RTT (theoretical min) | $\text{RTT} = 2 \times \dfrac{d}{c} + \text{process}$ | seconds | $c$ = speed of light in fiber (~2×10⁸ m/s) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DHCP Lease Math for Subnet Capacity Planning

> **Problem:** A KTU cloud architect deploys a VPC with CIDR `10.0.0.0/22`. AWS reserves 5 IPs per subnet. If a subnet is `/24`, how many usable IPs exist per subnet, and across the /22 how many subnets exist?

### Step 1: Determine Host Bits per Subnet

The subnet mask is `/24`, meaning 24 bits are network, and the remaining bits are for hosts:

$$H_{\text{host}} = 32 - 24 = 8 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2: Compute Raw Host Addresses

Each bit position doubles the address space:

$$\text{Raw addresses} = 2^H = 2^8 = 256$$

### Step 3: Subtract AWS Reserved Addresses

AWS reserves **5 IPs** (network, VPC router, DNS, future, network broadcast):

$$U = 2^H - 5 = 256 - 5 = 251 \text{ usable IPs per subnet}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some sources cite "256 − 5" while older AWS docs say "256 − 3". The current default-VPC rule is **5 reserved**, giving **251 usable**.

### Step 4: Compute Number of Subnets Inside /22

The VPC is `/22` (22 network bits); the subnet is `/24` (24 network bits). The difference gives subnet bits:

$$B_{\text{subnet}} = 24 - 22 = 2 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Subnets} = 2^{B_{\text{subnet}}} = 2^2 = 4 \text{ subnets}$$

### Step 5: Total Usable IPs in the VPC

$$U_{\text{total}} = U \times \text{Subnets} = 251 \times 4 = 1004 \text{ usable IPs}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Weighted DNS Routing — Canary Deployment Math

> **Problem:** A KTU fintech startup is rolling out a new payment microservice. They use Route 53 weighted records: `v1.pay.ktu.in` weight = **90**, `v2.pay.ktu.in` weight = **10**. Over **1,000,000** DNS queries, how many reach v2?

### Step 1: Sum the Weights

$$W_{\text{total}} = w_1 + w_2 = 90 + 10 = 100$$

### Step 2: Compute Probability of Selecting v2

$$P(\text{v2}) = \frac{w_2}{W_{\text{total}}} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10$$

### Step 3: Compute Expected Queries to v2

$$Q_{\text{v2}} = P(\text{v2}) \times Q_{\text{total}} = 0.10 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ queries}$$

### Step 4: Stepwise Canary Promotion

After v2 passes monitoring at 10%:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1:} & \quad w_1 = 90,\ w_2 = 10 \Rightarrow 10\% \\
\text{Stage 2:} & \quad w_1 = 75,\ w_2 = 25 \Rightarrow 25\% \\
\text{Stage 3:} & \quad w_1 = 50,\ w_2 = 50 \Rightarrow 50\% \\
\text{Stage 4:} & \quad w_1 = 0,\ w_2 = 100 \Rightarrow 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: DNS TTL and Caching Window

> **Problem:** A record is set with TTL = **300 seconds**. A recursive resolver caches it at $t=0$. At what time does the cache expire, and what happens between $t=300$ and the next query response?

### Step 1: Define TTL Window

$$T_{\text{cache}} = [0, \text{TTL}) = [0, 300) \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 2: Expiry Point

$$t_{\text{expiry}} = 0 + 300 = 300 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 3: Behavior at Expiry

At $t = 300$, the TTL counter hits 0. The next query triggers a fresh iterative resolution. The next response sets a *new* TTL window beginning at the response-arrival time:

$$t_{\text{new-window}} = t_{\text{expiry}} + \epsilon$$

where $\epsilon$ is the recursive-resolution latency (typically **20–80 ms** for cached zones, **100–500 ms** for uncached).

### Step 4: Impact on Failover

For DNS-based failover to work, TTL **must** be lower than the **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)**:

$$\text{TTL} \ll \text{RTO}$$

For an RTO of 60 seconds, a TTL of 60s is the **upper bound**; in practice, 30s is recommended.

## 3.4 Derivation: SOA Serial Number and Zone Transfer Trigger

> **Problem:** A primary name server updates a record at 14:32:05 on 2024-11-15. The serial follows the YYYYMMDDNN convention. Compute the new serial.

### Step 1: Date Component

$$\text{Date} = 20241115$$

### Step 2: Counter Component (NN)

If multiple updates on the same day, increment NN. For the first update of the day:

$$\text{Counter} = 01$$

### Step 3: Concatenate

$$\text{Serial} = \text{Date} \times 100 + \text{Counter} = 2024111501$$

For the 2nd update of the day:

$$\text{Serial}_2 = 2024111501 + 1 = 2024111502$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Latency Routing — Sample RTT Matrix

> **Problem:** Health-check data from a resolver in **Kochi, India** shows the following TCP RTTs (in ms) to AWS regions: Mumbai=18, Singapore=42, Frankfurt=180, N. Virginia=240. Which region will Route 53 latency-based routing select?

### Step 1: Construct the RTT Vector

$$\vec{R} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{\text{mumbai}} \\ R_{\text{singapore}} \\ R_{\text{frankfurt}} \\ R_{\text{virginia}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 42 \\ 180 \\ 240 \end{bmatrix} \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2: Find the Argmin

$$\text{Selected region} = \arg\min_i R_i = \arg\min(18, 42, 180, 240) = \text{Mumbai}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Result

$$R_{\text{selected}} = 18 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Cloud DNS Provider Comparison

| Feature | AWS Route 53 | Azure DNS | Google Cloud DNS |
|---|---|---|---|
| SLA | **100%** | **100%** | **100%** |
| Anycast | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Latency Routing | ✅ | ❌ (use Traffic Manager) | ❌ (use Load Balancer) |
| Geo Routing | ✅ | ✅ (Traffic Manager) | ❌ |
| Health Checks | ✅ (HTTP/TCP/HTTPS) | ✅ (TM probes) | ✅ (LB health checks) |
| DNSSEC | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Private Zones | ✅ | ✅ (Private DNS) | ✅ |
| Query Price (per million) | **$0.40** | **$0.50** | **$0.20** |
| Apex Alias | ✅ (ALIAS) | ❌ (CNAME workaround) | ❌ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cloud DNS Resolution Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Browser] --> B[Local OS Resolver]
    B --> C{Is Record Cached?}
    C -- Yes, TTL valid --> D[Return Cached IP]
    C -- No or Expired --> E[ISP Recursive Resolver]
    E --> F[Root Server Anycast]
    F --> G[TLD Server .in]
    G --> H[Authoritative Route 53 Hosted Zone]
    H --> I[Apply Routing Policy]
    I --> J{Health Check Pass?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Return Selected Record A]
    J -- No --> L[Failover to Secondary Record B]
    K --> M[Resolver Caches with TTL]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Client Connects to Web Server]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The resolver always asks the authoritative server, not the root, for **cached** responses. The root is only consulted on a *cache miss* (cold start). Once the TLD `.in` is cached, the resolver skips root for subsequent queries.

## 4.2 Cloud DHCP Lifecycle (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant VM as EC2 Instance
    participant HV as Hypervisor
    participant CP as AWS Control Plane
    participant MD as Metadata Service

    VM->>HV: DHCPDISCOVER (broadcast)
    HV->>CP: Internal API call (tenant context)
    CP->>CP: Allocate IP from subnet pool
    CP-->>HV: IP, Subnet, Gateway, DNS, Domain
    HV-->>VM: DHCPOFFER
    VM->>HV: DHCPREQUEST
    HV-->>VM: DHCPACK
    VM->>MD: GET /latest/meta-data/local-ipv4
    MD-->>VM: 10.0.1.25
    Note over VM,MD: Lease is renewed silently on T1 = 0.5 × L
    VM->>HV: Instance Terminates
    HV->>CP: Release IP back to pool
    CP-->>HV: Pool updated
```

## 4.3 DNS Routing Policy Decision Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[DNS Query] --> P{Routing Policy}
    P -- Simple --> S[Random Pick from Set]
    P -- Weighted --> W[Probabilistic Selection]
    P -- Latency --> L[Min RTT Region]
    P -- Failover --> F{Health Check}
    F -- Primary OK --> F1[Return Primary]
    F -- Primary Down --> F2[Return Secondary]
    P -- Geolocation --> G[Match Client IP to Region]
    P -- Multi-Value --> M[Up to 8 Healthy Random]
    S --> R[Return IP]
    W --> R
    L --> R
    F1 --> R
    F2 --> R
    G --> R
    M --> R
```

## 4.4 Hybrid DNS Architecture — On-Premises + Cloud (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OnPrem[On-Premises Data Center]
        DC[Internal DNS BIND Server 10.20.0.10]
        CRM[CRM App Server]
    end
    subgraph CloudVPC[AWS VPC 10.0.0.0/16]
        RT[Route 53 Resolver Inbound Endpoint]
        RPO[Route 53 Resolver Outbound Endpoint]
        PHZ[Private Hosted Zone corp.ktu.in]
        EC2[EC2 Instance 10.0.1.50]
    end
    CRM --> DC
    DC --> RPO
    RPO --> PHZ
    PHZ --> EC2
    EC2 --> RT
    RT --> DC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most exam-relevant KTU hybrid DNS pattern**: a *forward* direction (on-prem → cloud) and a *reverse* direction (cloud → on-prem) using **Route 53 Resolver Endpoints** with conditional forwarding rules.

## 4.5 DHCP Options Set Configuration Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VPC 10.0.0.0/16] --> B[Subnet 10.0.1.0/24]
    B --> C{DHCP Options Set}
    C -- Default --> D[AmazonProvidedDNS at 10.0.0.2]
    C -- Custom --> E[User-defined DNS servers: 8.8.8.8, 8.8.4.4]
    C -- Custom Domain --> F[Domain: corp.ktu.in]
    C -- Custom NTP --> G[NTP: 169.254.169.123]
    D --> H[VM Boot]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[VM Receives Lease]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Cloud DNS. List any **four** DNS record types used in AWS Route 53 with one-line purposes.*

**Model Answer:**

**Cloud DNS** is a managed, globally distributed, Anycast-based Domain Name System service that translates human-readable domain names to IP addresses and provides programmable routing policies via REST APIs and Infrastructure-as-Code.

| Record Type | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **A** | Maps hostname to IPv4 address (32-bit) |
| **AAAA** | Maps hostname to IPv6 address (128-bit) |
| **CNAME** | Creates an alias from one name to another |
| **MX** | Specifies mail exchange servers with priority |

*(Any 4 of A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS, SOA, PTR, SRV, CAA accepted.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain why cloud DHCP is described as "effectively stateless" from a customer's perspective.*

**Model Answer:**

In a traditional on-premises DHCP setup, the customer must manage lease databases, DHCP relay agents, and IP conflict resolution. In contrast, **Cloud DHCP** is fully managed by the cloud provider's control plane. The customer does not:
- Configure or monitor lease timeouts,
- Run DHCP server software,
- Manually reserve IPs (except via explicit Elastic IP APIs).

The IP address is **retained** as long as the instance/ENI exists and is **automatically released** on termination. Hence, customers experience DHCP as *stateless* — they only need to declare the desired configuration, not operate the protocol.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, KTU Model Paper, Module 2 Choice A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Compare **Simple, Weighted, Latency-Based, and Failover** routing policies in AWS Route 53. Provide a real-world scenario for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Policy | Selection Logic | Real-World Scenario |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple** | Random pick from a record set (no health checks) | Single-region static website on EC2 |
| **Weighted** | Probabilistic by user-defined weights (0–255) | Canary release: 95% traffic to v1, 5% to v2 |
| **Latency-Based** | Region with lowest measured RTT from resolver | Multi-region app: users in India get Mumbai, EU users get Frankfurt |
| **Failover** | Primary if health check passes, else secondary | Active-passive DR: primary in Mumbai, standby in Singapore |

**Key Insight:** Failover routing is the *only* policy that uses health checks for primary-secondary selection. Latency routing does not check health by default — it can return an unhealthy endpoint.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A startup uses Route 53 weighted records: `api.ksp.gov.in` with two endpoints — `v1` weight **80**, `v2` weight **20**. Over **5,00,000** queries, calculate: (i) queries to v1, (ii) queries to v2, (iii) explain what happens if the health check for v1 fails.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Sum weights**
$$W_{\text{total}} = 80 + 20 = 100$$

**Step 2: Probability of v1**
$$P(v1) = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 3: Probability of v2**
$$P(v2) = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20$$

**Step 4: Query counts**
$$Q_{v1} = 0.80 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000$$
$$Q_{v2} = 0.20 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5: Health check failure of v1**
In **weighted routing**, if a health check fails, Route 53 stops returning that record — it does **not** redistribute traffic. Therefore, if v1 fails its health check, **all** 5,00,000 queries would go to v2, which may not have the capacity. The proper policy for graceful degradation is **Failover routing** with v2 as primary, not weighted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, KTU Model Paper, Module 2 Choice B]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Describe the architecture of **AWS DHCP** including the role of the hypervisor, the control plane, and the metadata service. Why does AWS intercept DHCP at the hypervisor level rather than running a customer-visible DHCP server?*

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Hypervisor Layer**: The Nitro / Xen hypervisor intercepts the VM's `DHCPDISCOVER` broadcast *before* it leaves the host. This is necessary because AWS uses an **overlay network (VPC)**, and broadcasts are confined to a single host — they cannot traverse the VPC fabric.

2. **AWS Control Plane**: Receives the intercepted request via internal API, looks up the VPC, subnet, and tenant context, and allocates an IP from the pool. It returns a structured lease object (IP, mask, gateway, DNS servers, domain).

3. **Metadata Service (`169.254.169.254`)**: The VM confirms its IP via a link-local HTTP call. This decouples the actual IP assignment from the in-guest network configuration.

**Why Hypervisor-Level Interception?**

- **Performance**: No L2 broadcast flooding across the VPC.
- **Security**: Tenants cannot sniff each other's DHCP traffic.
- **Scalability**: Eliminates the need for per-subnet DHCP servers.
- **Determinism**: Allocation is atomic and conflict-free at the control plane.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A VPC has CIDR `10.0.0.0/20` and subnets of size `/24`. A custom DHCP Options Set is configured with DNS servers `1.1.1.1` and `1.0.0.1` and domain `corp.ktu.in`. Calculate: (i) number of subnets possible, (ii) usable IPs per subnet, (iii) total usable IPs in the VPC.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Subnet bits**
$$B = 24 - 20 = 4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Number of subnets**
$$N_{\text{sub}} = 2^4 = 16 \text{ subnets}$$

**Step 3: Host bits per subnet**
$$H = 32 - 24 = 8 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Usable IPs per subnet** (AWS reserves 5)
$$U = 2^8 - 5 = 256 - 5 = 251 \text{ IPs/subnet}$$

**Step 5: Total usable IPs in VPC**
$$U_{\text{total}} = 16 \times 251 = 4016 \text{ IPs}$$

**Bonus Insight:** The custom DNS servers (`1.1.1.1`, `1.0.0.1`) override the default `AmazonProvidedDNS` for **all** instances in any VPC that uses this Options Set. A VPC can only have **one** DHCP Options Set at a time, but you can change it via API at any time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Reserved IPs"**: Students often write `2^H − 2` (classical network formula) when AWS reserves **5**, not 2. This costs **2 marks** in sub-parts.
> 2. **Forgetting the CNAME at apex rule**: Students say "use CNAME at the zone apex" — this is **illegal** per RFC 1035. Use ALIAS (Route 53) or ANAME.
> 3. **Mixing routing policies**: Latency routing is **not** health-checked by default. Students who claim "latency routing will fail over" lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring the broadcast domain**: Claiming "DHCP broadcasts traverse the VPC" is wrong — they are intercepted at the hypervisor.
> 5. **Arithmetic errors in weighted routing**: The total must be the **sum of all weights** in the record set, not the highest one.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

### DNS in the Cloud
- [ ] **Cloud DNS** = managed, Anycast, API-driven, SLA ≥ **99.99%** authoritative DNS.
- [ ] **Route 53 / Azure DNS / Cloud DNS** all support A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, NS, SOA, PTR, SRV, CAA.
- [ ] **ALIAS record** = Route 53-specific workaround for CNAME at zone apex.
- [ ] **Latency routing** uses historical RTT, *not* geographic distance; not health-checked by default.
- [ ] **Failover routing** = primary/secondary, requires health checks, used for DR.
- [ ] **Geolocation routing** is *coarse* (continent/country/state), not IP-level geofencing.
- [ ] **SOA timers**: Refresh 7200, Retry 900, Expire 1209600, Minimum 86400.
- [ ] **DNSSEC** is supported but optional; uses asymmetric crypto to sign zone data.
- [ ] **Hybrid DNS** uses **Route 53 Resolver Endpoints** (Inbound/Outbound) for on-prem ↔ cloud resolution.
- [ ] **Private Hosted Zones** are VPC-scoped; resolution is automatic from EC2 instances.

### DHCP in the Cloud
- [ ] **Cloud DHCP** is hypervisor-intercepted — broadcasts never leave the host.
- [ ] **AWS DHCP Options Set** = tenant-configurable, applies to entire VPC (only one per VPC).
- [ ] **Default DNS in AWS VPC** = `10.0.0.2` (or VPC base + 2), hypervisor-resolved, not a real ENI.
- [ ] **Azure uses `168.63.129.16`** (wire server) — not standard DHCP at all.
- [ ] **GCP uses `169.254.169.254`** metadata server for IP confirmation.
- [ ] **AWS reserves 5 IPs per subnet** (network, gateway, DNS, future, broadcast).
- [ ] **DHCP lease = effectively infinite** while VM/ENI is attached.
- [ ] **T1 (renew)** = 0.5 × lease, **T2 (rebind)** = 0.875 × lease (RFC 2131).
- [ ] **Elastic IPs / Reserved Public IPs** are *outside* DHCP — pre-allocated static addresses.
- [ ] **DHCP option code 6** = DNS servers, **code 15** = domain name, **code 42** = NTP.

### Quick-Recall Numerical Anchors
- [ ] `/24` subnet → 8 host bits → 256 raw → **251 usable** (AWS).
- [ ] `/22` VPC with `/24` subnets → **4 subnets**, 1004 usable IPs.
- [ ] Weighted 80:20 over 1,000,000 queries → **800,000 : 200,000**.
- [ ] Argmin RTT selects latency-routed region.
- [ ] TTL must be **< RTO** for effective DNS-based failover.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtual Private Networks (VPN): Implementation in Cloud and Securing VPNs for Cloud Connectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Virtual Private Networks (VPN): Implementation in Cloud and Securing VPNs for Cloud Connectivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Virtual Private Network (VPN)** is a network technology that creates a logically isolated, encrypted overlay tunnel across a public or shared network infrastructure (typically the Internet) in order to transport private traffic with the **confidentiality**, **integrity**, and **authentication** properties of a dedicated leased line. In the context of **cloud computing**, a VPN extends an enterprise's on-premises private network boundary into a Cloud Service Provider's (CSP) Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) by establishing cryptographically protected point-to-point or site-to-site tunnels between the customer gateway and the cloud VPN gateway.

The formal ITU-T X.800 and IETF RFC 4301 (IPsec) definition characterizes a VPN as a *confidentiality-controlled network constructed by using public network resources and providing the user with the perception that they are directly connected to a private network*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT521 – Module 2):**
> A VPN is *not* a separate physical network. It is a **virtual** (logical) construct built using **tunneling**, **encryption**, and **access control** to provide *equivalence to a private leased line* over *untrusted* public infrastructure (Internet).

### 1.2 Key Terminology the Examiner Expects

| Term | Standard Meaning (as per RFC 4301 / 4303) |
|---|---|
| **Tunnel** | A logical path that encapsulates the original packet inside a new outer header |
| **Encapsulation** | Wrapping an inner PDU with an outer header (e.g., GRE, IPsec ESP) |
| **Encryption** | Transformation of plaintext into ciphertext using a cryptographic key (e.g., **AES-256-GCM**) |
| **Authentication Header (AH)** | IPsec protocol that provides integrity + authentication (no confidentiality) |
| **Encapsulating Security Payload (ESP)** | IPsec protocol that provides confidentiality + integrity + authentication |
| **Security Association (SA)** | A simplex (one-way) logical connection identified by SPI, destination IP, and protocol |
| **IKE (Internet Key Exchange)** | Protocol (UDP 500/4500) used to negotiate SAs and exchange keys |
| **VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)** | A logically isolated virtual network inside a public cloud |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy – "The Armored Postal Pipeline":**
> Imagine the public Internet as a vast, open postal system where everyone can see and tamper with your letters. A VPN is like an **armored, sealed pipeline** that you lay across this open postal system. You drop your letter (the *plaintext packet*) into a **secure box** (the *encrypted payload*), seal it with a **tamper-proof lock** (the *ESP/AH trailer + MAC*), and attach a **private courier ID card** (the *outer IP header with tunnel endpoint IP*). Couriers (routers) along the way can only see the *outer address* of the pipeline's exit, never the contents. When the box reaches the cloud data center's gate, only the **authorized recipient** (the *cloud VPN gateway with the correct pre-shared key or certificate*) can open it.

For a cloud engineer, this analogy translates directly: the **enterprise firewall/CPE router** is the *sealing station*, the **cloud VPN gateway** (e.g., AWS Virtual Private Gateway, Azure VPN Gateway) is the *authorized recipient*, and the **IPsec tunnel** is the *armored pipeline*.

### 1.4 Why VPN Matters in the Cloud (KTU Perspective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Engineering Reasons Cloud VPNs are Critical:**
> 1. **Data-in-Transit Confidentiality** – Protects traffic from eavesdropping between on-premises data center ↔ cloud VPC.
> 2. **Network Extension** – Makes cloud resources appear as if they are on the same Layer-2/3 LAN as on-premises servers.
> 3. **Compliance** – Satisfies regulatory mandates (RBI, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR) that require encrypted channels for sensitive workloads.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture – The 5-Layer VPN Stack

A cloud VPN is best understood as a **layered protocol stack**. Each layer has a specific cryptographic or networking responsibility.

**Layer 5 – Application Payload**
The original user data (HTTP, SQL, SSH) generated by the source host.

**Layer 4 – Transport**
TCP/UDP segmentation and port multiplexing.

**Layer 3 – Original (Inner) IP Header**
The source/destination IP that the two endpoints wish to communicate with privately.

**Layer 2 – VPN Encryption (ESP Payload)**
The inner IP packet + TCP/UDP segment is encrypted using a symmetric algorithm (default in AWS/Azure = **AES-256-GCM**; legacy = AES-128-CBC). The encryption key length **must be at least 128 bits** to be FIPS 140-2 compliant.

**Layer 1 – Outer IP Header + Tunnel Header**
A new IP header (with the *public* IP of the cloud VPN gateway) is prepended. For IPsec, ESP trailer + ESP authentication (ICV) are appended. For SSL-VPN (e.g., OpenVPN), a UDP or TCP outer header carrying TLS wraps the payload.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Explain VPN operation", always draw both the *cleartext* packet and the *encrypted/encapsulated* packet side-by-side. The examiner awards 2 marks specifically for showing the **additional headers** (outer IP + ESP header + ESP trailer + ESP Auth) added to the original packet.

### 2.2 VPN Classification (KTU Mandated Taxonomy)

A. **By Deployment Topology**
- **Remote Access VPN** – A single user device tunnels into a corporate/cloud network. Uses client software (e.g., OpenVPN GUI, Cisco AnyConnect, AWS Client VPN).
- **Site-to-Site VPN** – Two whole networks (e.g., on-prem DC ↔ AWS VPC) are joined via dedicated VPN gateways on both sides. No per-user client.
- **Hub-and-Spoke VPN** – Multiple branch offices all tunnel to a central cloud VPN concentrator (often AWS Transit Gateway + VPN attachments).

B. **By Layer of Operation**
- **Layer 2 VPN** – e.g., L2TP, VXLAN. Carries Ethernet frames.
- **Layer 3 VPN** – e.g., IPsec, MPLS. Carries IP packets. *Default for cloud.*
- **Application Layer VPN** – e.g., SSL/TLS VPN. Runs in the browser, only proxies specific apps.

C. **By Trust Model**
- **Intranet VPN** – Connects only internal branches of one organization.
- **Extranet VPN** – Connects the organization to specific business partners using separate crypto domains.
- **Client/Remote-Access VPN** – Connects individual mobile users.

### 2.3 IPsec – The Workhorse of Cloud VPN

The Internet Protocol Security (IPsec) suite is the **de-facto standard** for cloud VPNs. Every major CSP (AWS, Azure, GCP, Oracle Cloud, IBM Cloud) uses IPsec with IKEv2 as the default site-to-site tunnel protocol.

IPsec has two core protocols and one supporting protocol:

1. **AH (Authentication Header – IP Protocol 51)**
   - Provides **integrity** and **authentication** of the IP packet.
   - Does **NOT** encrypt payload.
   - Authenticates *entire* packet including outer IP header (except mutable fields like TTL).

2. **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload – IP Protocol 50)**
   - Provides **confidentiality**, **integrity**, **authentication**.
   - Encrypts payload and ESP trailer.
   - Standard for cloud VPNs (chosen over AH in 99% of deployments).

3. **IKE (Internet Key Exchange – UDP 500/4500)**
   - Automates SA negotiation, key exchange, and peer authentication.
   - **IKEv1** (legacy) – 6 messages, 2 phases.
   - **IKEv2** (RFC 7296, modern) – 4 messages, 1 phase. *Preferred by all CSPs.*

### 2.4 IPsec Modes – Transport vs Tunnel

| Feature | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Outer Header** | Original IP header preserved | New outer IP header added (tunnel endpoints) |
| **Encrypts** | Payload only | Entire original IP packet |
| **Use Case** | Host-to-host within trusted network | Gateway-to-gateway (the *cloud* standard) |
| **Cloud VPN Usage** | Rare | **Always** (AWS VPG, Azure Local Network Gateway) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Transport" with "TCP/UDP". In IPsec, *Transport mode* means the **original IP header stays visible**, and only the payload is protected. In *Tunnel mode*, even the original IP header is hidden inside the encrypted payload. **Cloud VPNs ALWAYS use Tunnel Mode ESP.**

### 2.5 IKEv2 Phase-1 Exchange – Step-by-Step

The IKEv2 uses 4 messages (called *IKE_SA_INIT* and *IKE_AUTH*):

1. **HDR, SAi1, KEi, Ni** (Initiator → Responder)
   - Proposes cryptographic suite, sends Diffie-Hellman public key, sends nonce.

2. **HDR, SAr1, KEr, Nr, [CERTREQ]** (Responder → Initiator)
   - Accepts suite, sends DH public key, sends nonce, may request certificate.

3. **HDR, SK {IDi, [CERT], [CERTREQ], [IDr], AUTH, SAi2, TSi, TSr}** (Initiator → Responder)
   - Identity, authentication, and Child SA (IPsec SA) parameters.

4. **HDR, SK {IDr, [CERT], AUTH, SAr2, TSi, TSr}** (Responder → Initiator)
   - Authentication confirmed, Child SA created.

> **Result:** Two pairs of SAs are established: one IKE SA (for key management) and at least one Child SA (for actual data traffic).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table compiles the exact quantitative relationships that frequently appear in KTU Module-2 questions. Note: All pipe symbols have been escaped as `\vert` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Identity | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $A = g^{a} \bmod p$ ; $B = g^{b} \bmod p$ ; $K = B^{a} \bmod p = A^{b} \bmod p$ | $a, b$ are private; $g$ is generator; $p$ is prime modulus |
| 2 | DH Bit Strength (NIST SP 800-57) | Group 14 = **2048-bit MODP** ; Group 24 = **2048-bit MODP w/256-bit Prime Subgroup** | Cloud VPNs default to Group 14 or higher |
| 3 | HMAC Verification | $\text{tag} = \text{HMAC}_{K}(M)$ ; $\text{valid} \iff \text{tag}_{recv} = \text{tag}_{local}$ | $K$ is shared secret; $M$ is message |
| 4 | AES Key Sizes (FIPS 197) | $k \in \{128, 192, 256\}$ **bits** | Cloud default = 256-bit |
| 5 | Encryption Throughput (approx) | $T \approx \dfrac{8 \cdot \text{FileSize}}{\text{EncryptTime}}$ | Measured in **Mbps** |
| 6 | ESP Header Overhead | $\text{IPv4} (20) + \text{UDP}(8) + \text{ESP}(8) + \text{ESP-Trailer}(\geq 2) + \text{ESP-Auth}(12) + \text{IV}(8 \text{ for AES-GCM})$ | Total $\geq 58$ **bytes** added per packet |
| 7 | MTU Consideration | $\text{Effective MTU} = 1500 - \text{Overhead}$ | Cloud MTU often set to **1436 bytes** to avoid fragmentation |
| 8 | PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy) | New DH key generated per Child SA | Defeats retrospective decryption attacks |
| 9 | SPI (Security Parameter Index) | 32-bit identifier in ESP/AH header | Uniquely identifies an SA on the receiver |
| 10 | Replay Protection | Sliding window of size $W = 32$ or $64$ packets | Anti-replay using sequence numbers |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How VPN is Used |
|---|---|
| **Hybrid Cloud** | On-prem Oracle ERP ↔ AWS VPC via IPsec VPN |
| **Multi-Cloud** | AWS ↔ Azure connected using both CSPs' VPN gateways + BGP routing |
| **Remote Work** | Engineers use OpenVPN/WireGuard to access private S3 buckets in a VPC |
| **Compliance** | Banks encrypt inter-DC traffic using site-to-site IPsec as per **RBI Cyber Security Framework** |
| **DevOps** | Site-to-site VPN allows CI/CD runners to deploy into private subnets without exposing them to the Internet |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example – Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (Exhaustive Derivation)

This is the **most frequently asked numerical question** in KTU Module-2 VPN sections. We will derive the shared secret $K$ step by step.

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU 2-Mark Sub-Part):**
Alice and Bob agree on a prime $p = 23$ and a generator $g = 5$. Alice chooses private key $a = 6$ and Bob chooses private key $b = 15$. Compute the shared secret $K$.

**Step 1 – Compute Alice's Public Value $A$**

$$
A = g^{a} \bmod p
$$

$$
A = 5^{6} \bmod 23
$$

Compute $5^{6}$ by repeated squaring:

$$
5^{2} = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
5^{4} \equiv 2^{2} = 4 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
5^{6} = 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
\therefore A = 8
$$

**Step 2 – Compute Bob's Public Value $B$**

$$
B = g^{b} \bmod p
$$

$$
B = 5^{15} \bmod 23
$$

Decompose the exponent:

$$
5^{15} = 5^{8} \cdot 5^{4} \cdot 5^{2} \cdot 5^{1}
$$

Compute powers:

$$
5^{2} \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
5^{4} \equiv 4 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
5^{8} \equiv 4^{2} = 16 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
5^{15} \equiv 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
16 \cdot 4 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \cdot 23 = 64 - 46 = 18 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
18 \cdot 2 = 36 \equiv 36 - 23 = 13 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
13 \cdot 5 = 65 \equiv 65 - 2 \cdot 23 = 65 - 46 = 19 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
\therefore B = 19
$$

**Step 3 – Compute Shared Secret on Alice's Side**

$$
K_{A} = B^{a} \bmod p
$$

$$
K_{A} = 19^{6} \bmod 23
$$

$$
19 \equiv -4 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
(-4)^{2} = 16 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
(-4)^{4} \equiv 16^{2} = 256 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
256 = 11 \cdot 23 + 3 \implies 256 \equiv 3 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
(-4)^{6} = (-4)^{4} \cdot (-4)^{2} \equiv 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
48 = 2 \cdot 23 + 2 \implies 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
\therefore K_{A} = 2
$$

**Step 4 – Compute Shared Secret on Bob's Side (Verification)**

$$
K_{B} = A^{b} \bmod p
$$

$$
K_{B} = 8^{15} \bmod 23
$$

Decompose:

$$
8^{15} = 8^{8} \cdot 8^{4} \cdot 8^{2} \cdot 8^{1}
$$

$$
8^{2} = 64 \equiv 18 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
8^{4} \equiv 18^{2} = 324 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
324 = 14 \cdot 23 + 2 \implies 324 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
8^{8} \equiv 2^{2} = 4 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
8^{15} \equiv 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
4 \cdot 2 = 8
$$

$$
8 \cdot 18 = 144 \pmod{23} \implies 144 = 6 \cdot 23 + 6 \implies 144 \equiv 6 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
6 \cdot 8 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}
$$

$$
\therefore K_{B} = 2
$$

**Verification Step:**

$$
K_{A} = K_{B} = 2 \pmod{23}
$$

Hence both parties derive the **shared secret $K = 2$** from their respective private keys without ever transmitting it over the network. An eavesdropper seeing $A = 8$ and $B = 19$ would need to solve the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** to recover $a$ or $b$, which is computationally infeasible for properly chosen large primes (typically 2048+ bits in real cloud VPNs).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – VPN Protocol Comparison

This matrix is **gold for KTU Part-B (14-mark) questions** asking "Compare IPsec and SSL/TLS VPN" or "Discuss the security of various tunneling protocols."

| Property | IPsec | SSL/TLS VPN (OpenVPN) | L2TP/IPsec | PPTP (Legacy) | WireGuard (Modern) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 3 | Layer 4–5 (TLS over TCP/UDP) | Layer 2 (L2TP) + Layer 3 (IPsec) | Layer 2 | Layer 3 |
| **Encryption** | AES-256-GCM (via ESP) | AES-256-GCM (via TLS 1.3) | AES-256-GCM | RC4 (BROKEN) | ChaCha20-Poly1305 |
| **Authentication** | X.509 / Pre-Shared Key | X.509 / Username-Password | X.509 / PSK | MS-CHAPv2 (weak) | Curve25519 public keys |
| **Client Software Required** | Yes (heavyweight IKE daemon) | Browser-only or light client | Yes | Built into Windows (legacy) | Yes (lightweight) |
| **NAT Traversal** | Requires NAT-T (UDP 4500) | Native (uses TCP 443 – bypasses firewalls) | Requires NAT-T | Poor | Native |
| **Cloud Provider Support** | **Universal** (AWS, Azure, GCP) | Partial (Azure Point-to-Site, AWS Client VPN) | Limited | Not supported | Third-party only |
| **Speed** | Medium | Slow over TCP (overhead) | Medium | Fast but insecure | **Fastest** |
| **Recommended Use** | Site-to-Site (gateway ↔ gateway) | Remote user access via browser | Legacy compatibility | **Never use** | Modern remote access |
| **Security Rating (2024)** | Strong (with IKEv2 + AES-GCM) | Strong (TLS 1.3) | Strong | **Broken – Deprecated** | Strong (small attack surface) |
| **Code Size (audit risk)** | ~400,000 lines (IKE daemon) | OpenSSL (~500K lines) | Larger | Small but weak | **~4,000 lines (auditable)** |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step – Encapsulation Overhead Calculation

This derivation is **frequently asked as a 3-mark Part-A question**.

**Problem:** An enterprise uses an IPsec ESP tunnel (AES-128-CBC + HMAC-SHA-256). Calculate the total overhead added to a 1460-byte TCP payload sent over IPv4.

**Step 1 – Identify Required Headers/Trailers**

| Component | Size (bytes) | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| New (outer) IPv4 Header | 20 | Tunnel endpoint IP addressing |
| ESP Header (SPI + Seq) | 8 | Identifies the SA |
| ESP IV (for AES-CBC) | 16 | Initialization Vector |
| Padding (0–255) | up to 15 | Align to AES block (16 bytes) |
| Pad Length field | 1 | Required by RFC 4303 |
| Next Header field | 1 | Identifies original protocol |
| ESP Authentication (HMAC-SHA-256) | 16 | ICV for integrity |
| **Total ESP Overhead** | **61** | Sum of above |

**Step 2 – Total Outer Packet Size**

$$
\text{Packet}_{\text{outer}} = \text{Inner Payload} + \text{ESP Overhead} + \text{Outer Header}
$$

$$
\text{Packet}_{\text{outer}} = 1460 + 61 + 20 = 1541 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 – MTU Implication**

The standard Ethernet MTU is **1500 bytes**. The encrypted packet exceeds it by 41 bytes. Therefore, the path MTU must be reduced (typical cloud setting: MTU = **1436 bytes** at the customer gateway) to avoid IP fragmentation, which would break IPsec anti-replay protection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The effective inner TCP MSS that should be configured on cloud workloads is $1500 - 61 - 20 = 1419$ **bytes** to prevent fragmentation after ESP encapsulation.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step – Security Association (SA) Triplet Identification

In any KTU question asking to "list the parameters that identify an SA", write the **SA Triplet**:

$$
\text{SA} = \{ \text{Destination IP},\ \text{IPsec Protocol (AH/ESP)},\ \text{SPI (32-bit)} \}
$$

A single IPsec gateway may have **hundreds of SAs** simultaneously. For a bi-directional VPN tunnel, **two SAs** are required (one inbound, one outbound), because SA is *simplex* (one-way) by RFC 4301.

### 3.5 ESP Packet Format – Exhaustive Breakdown

```
+--------------------------------+ 0
|         Outer IP Header        | 20 bytes (IPv4) or 40 bytes (IPv6)
+--------------------------------+
|         ESP Header             | 8 bytes: SPI (4) + Sequence Number (4)
+--------------------------------+
|  IV (Initialization Vector)    | 8/12/16 bytes (depends on cipher)
+--------------------------------+
|                                |
|  Encrypted Payload             |  Inner IP packet + TCP/UDP + data
|  (variable length)             |
|                                |
+--------------------------------+
|  ESP Trailer                   |  Padding + Pad Length (1) + Next Hdr (1)
+--------------------------------+
|  ESP ICV (Authentication Data) | 12/16/32 bytes (HMAC-SHA-256/384/512)
+--------------------------------+
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Authentication (ICV)** covers the **ESP Header + Encrypted Payload + ESP Trailer** but does **NOT** cover the outer IP header (which changes at every hop). This is a crucial distinction that examiners use to differentiate between AH and ESP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – Site-to-Site IPsec VPN between On-Premises and AWS Cloud

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates a typical hybrid-cloud site-to-site VPN topology. It uses **safe alphanumeric node IDs** and contains **no markdown formatting inside node labels** to ensure clean rendering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OnPrem["ON PREMISES DATA CENTER"]
        AppServer1["App Server 10 10 0 10"]
        AppServer2["DB Server 10 10 0 11"]
        CustGW["Customer Gateway 198 51 100 1"]
        FW["Enterprise Firewall"]
    end

    subgraph Internet["PUBLIC INTERNET UNTRUSTED"]
        ISP1["ISP Router 1"]
        ISP2["ISP Router 2"]
        ISP3["ISP Router 3"]
    end

    subgraph AWSCloud["AWS CLOUD REGION us east 1"]
        VPG["Virtual Private Gateway 203 83 219 25"]
        TGW["Transit Gateway"]
        VPC1["VPC A 10 20 0 0 16"]
        VPC2["VPC B 10 30 0 0 16"]
        EC2A["EC2 Private Subnet 10 20 1 5"]
        EC2B["RDS Database 10 30 1 20"]
    end

    AppServer1 --> FW
    AppServer2 --> FW
    FW --> CustGW
    CustGW -- "IPsec ESP Tunnel Encrypted" --> ISP1
    ISP1 --> ISP2
    ISP2 --> ISP3
    ISP3 -- "Decrypted at VPG" --> VPG
    VPG --> TGW
    TGW --> VPC1
    TGW --> VPC2
    VPC1 --> EC2A
    VPC2 --> EC2B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The inner traffic `10.10.0.0/16 ↔ 10.20.0.0/16` is **encrypted at the Customer Gateway** and traverses the public Internet as opaque ESP packets. The **Virtual Private Gateway (VPG)** terminates the tunnel, and the **Transit Gateway (TGW)** routes the now-decrypted packets to multiple VPCs.

### 4.2 Block Diagram – Remote-Access SSL VPN Architecture (Client-to-Site)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RemoteUser["REMOTE USER LAPTOP"]
        VPNClient["OpenVPN Client SW"]
        Browser["Web Browser"]
    end

    subgraph CloudVPC["CLOUD VPC AZURE AWS"]
        EdgeFW["Cloud Firewall NSG"]
        VPNGW["VPN Gateway IKEv2 SSL"]
        AuthSvc["Auth Service RADIUS AAD"]
        AppTier["Internal App 10 40 0 0 24"]
        DataTier["Database Tier 10 40 1 0 24"]
    end

    VPNClient -- "TLS over UDP 1194" --> VPNGW
    Browser -- "HTTPS 443 Reverse Proxy" --> VPNGW
    VPNGW -- "Verify Certificate or PSK" --> AuthSvc
    AuthSvc -- "Issue Session Token" --> VPNGW
    VPNGW -- "Allowed by NSG Rules" --> EdgeFW
    EdgeFW --> AppTier
    EdgeFW --> DataTier
```

> **Engineering interpretation:** The VPN Gateway enforces **per-user authentication** via RADIUS/Azure AD, then dynamically injects a route into the client's OS routing table so that only `10.40.0.0/16` traffic is sent through the tunnel (split-tunneling). This conserves bandwidth and improves performance.

### 4.3 Block Diagram – VPN Protocol Stack Layering (Original vs Encapsulated Packet)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OriginalPacket["ORIGINAL CLEAR PACKET BEFORE VPN"]
        L5O["Application Data HTTP 800 bytes"]
        L4O["TCP Header 20 bytes"]
        L3O["Inner IP Header src 10 10 0 10 dst 10 20 1 5"]
    end

    subgraph EncryptedPacket["AFTER IPsec ESP ENCAPSULATION"]
        L3N["NEW Outer IP Header src 198 51 100 1 dst 203 83 219 25"]
        L2N["ESP Header SPI 0x1234ABCD SeqNum 0x00000001"]
        L1N["ESP IV 16 bytes random"]
        LEN["ENCRYPTED Inner IP plus TCP plus Data"]
        LTN["ESP Trailer Padding 1 to 255 bytes plus PadLen plus NextHdr"]
        LAN["ESP ICV HMAC SHA 256 16 bytes"]
    end

    OriginalPacket --> EncryptedPacket
```

> **Engineering interpretation:** A network sniffer on the Internet segment can only see the **outer IP** and the **ESP header**. The payload inside is encrypted ciphertext plus an integrity tag. Any tampering with the ciphertext will cause the HMAC to fail at the receiver, and the packet will be silently dropped.

### 4.4 Block Diagram – IKEv2 Message Exchange Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Initiator as Cloud VPN Gateway Init
    participant Responder as Customer CPE Router Resp

    Initiator->>Responder: HDR1 SAi1 KEi Ni
    Note over Initiator,Responder: IKE SA INIT Phase 1 of 2
    Responder->>Initiator: HDR2 SAr1 KEr Nr
    Note over Initiator,Responder: Both compute DH Shared Secret
    Initiator->>Responder: HDR3 SK IDi CERT AUTH SAi2 TSi TSr
    Note over Initiator,Responder: IKE AUTH Phase 2 of 2 + Child SA
    Responder->>Initiator: HDR4 SK IDr CERT AUTH SAr2 TSi TSr
    Note over Initiator,Responder: Tunnel Ready
```

> **Engineering interpretation:** Only **4 messages** are needed to establish both the IKE SA and the first Child SA. The `SK { ... }` notation means everything inside is encrypted using the keys derived from the DH exchange in messages 1 and 2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Virtual Private Network (VPN). List any TWO tunneling protocols used in cloud VPN implementations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **Virtual Private Network (VPN)** is a technology that creates a logically isolated, encrypted tunnel across a public network to securely transport private traffic with confidentiality, integrity, and authentication equivalent to a leased line. *(2 marks)*

Two tunneling protocols used in cloud VPN implementations:
1. **IPsec (Internet Protocol Security)** – The default for site-to-site cloud VPNs (AWS, Azure, GCP).
2. **SSL/TLS VPN (e.g., OpenVPN)** – Used for client-to-site remote access; traverses firewalls on TCP 443. *(1 mark for both)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between IPsec Transport Mode and Tunnel Mode. Which mode is used in cloud site-to-site VPNs?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
In **Transport Mode**, the original IP header is preserved and only the payload is encrypted/authenticated; used for host-to-host communication within a trusted network. *(1 mark)*

In **Tunnel Mode**, the entire original IP packet is encapsulated inside a new outer IP header and then encrypted; the original IP addresses are hidden from intermediate routers. *(1 mark)*

**Cloud site-to-site VPNs ALWAYS use Tunnel Mode ESP** because the endpoints are gateways (Customer Gateway ↔ Cloud VPN Gateway), and the inner IP addresses (private RFC 1918 ranges) must be hidden from the public Internet. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### 5.2.1 QUESTION A (14 Marks) – **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Site-to-Site IPsec VPN** used to connect an on-premises data center to an AWS VPC. Identify the roles of the Customer Gateway, Virtual Private Gateway, and Transit Gateway in the architecture.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **IKEv2 protocol** used in cloud VPNs. List the four messages exchanged during IKE_SA_INIT and IKE_AUTH phases and state TWO advantages of IKEv2 over IKEv1.

**Model Answer (a) – 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Site-to-Site IPsec VPN is a persistent, always-on encrypted tunnel established between two network gateways (an on-premises Customer Gateway and a cloud Virtual Private Gateway) that allows all hosts behind each gateway to communicate as if they were on the same private LAN.

**Architecture (5 marks – with block diagram):**

1. **Customer Gateway (CGW)** *(1 mark)* – A software or hardware device deployed at the on-premises data center perimeter. It holds the public IP of the enterprise router/firewall and the configuration (PSK or certificate) for tunnel establishment.

2. **Virtual Private Gateway (VPG)** *(1 mark)* – The AWS-side VPN concentrator. It is attached to a VPC and terminates the IPsec tunnel. It uses a redundant pair of endpoints (two public IPs) for high availability.

3. **Transit Gateway (TGW)** *(1 mark)* – A regional cloud router that interconnects multiple VPCs and on-premises networks. The VPG is attached to the TGW, which then routes traffic to one or more VPCs (VPC A, VPC B, etc.) across accounts or even AWS regions.

4. **Routing (1 mark)** – BGP is used to dynamically advertise the on-premises prefixes (`10.10.0.0/16`) to AWS and the cloud prefixes (`10.20.0.0/16`) back to the customer gateway. Static routes are an alternative for simpler setups.

5. **Encryption (1 mark)** – The IPsec ESP tunnel uses **AES-256-GCM**, **SHA-384** for HMAC, **DH Group 14** (2048-bit), and **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy)** enabled.

**Block Diagram (must accompany answer):**
```
On-Premises (10.10.0.0/16) → Customer Gateway → Internet → VPG (AWS) → Transit Gateway → VPC-A, VPC-B
```

---

**Model Answer (b) – 7 Marks:**

**IKEv2 (Internet Key Exchange version 2 – RFC 7296)** is the protocol used to dynamically negotiate Security Associations (SAs) and exchange keys for IPsec tunnels in cloud VPNs. It replaced IKEv1 in 2010 and is the **mandatory standard** for AWS, Azure, and GCP site-to-site VPN connections. *(1 mark)*

**Four IKEv2 Messages (4 marks – 1 mark each):**

1. **IKE_SA_INIT – Message 1 (Initiator → Responder):** Contains HDR, SAi1 (proposed cryptographic suite), KEi (Diffie-Hellman public value), and Ni (Initiator Nonce).
2. **IKE_SA_INIT – Message 2 (Responder → Initiator):** Contains HDR, SAr1 (selected suite), KEr (DH public value), Nr (Responder Nonce). Both sides now compute the DH shared secret.
3. **IKE_AUTH – Message 3 (Initiator → Responder):** Encrypted payload SK { IDi, [CERT], AUTH, SAi2, TSi, TSr } containing the identity, authentication signature, and parameters for the first **Child SA** (the actual data tunnel).
4. **IKE_AUTH – Message 4 (Responder → Initiator):** Encrypted payload SK { IDr, [CERT], AUTH, SAr2, TSi, TSr } confirming the Child SA is established. Tunnel is now ready to carry data.

**Two Advantages of IKEv2 over IKEv1 (2 marks):**

1. **Reduced message count** – IKEv2 uses 4 messages in a single phase, while IKEv1 required up to 9 messages in 2 phases. This means **faster tunnel establishment** (~0.3 s vs 2–3 s) and **lower susceptibility to DoS attacks** during setup.

2. **Built-in NAT Traversal (NAT-T) and MOBIKE** – IKEv2 natively supports traversal of NAT devices (essential for cloud deployments) and allows the client to **change IP addresses** (e.g., switching from Wi-Fi to 4G) without dropping the tunnel. IKEv1 required extensions and a separate DPD mechanism.

*(Other valid advantages: Built-in Dead Peer Detection (DPD), EAP support, anti-DoS cookie mechanism, PFS by default.)*

---

#### 5.2.2 QUESTION B (14 Marks) – **[Alternative Choice for Internal Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **security mechanisms** used in cloud VPNs. Discuss the role of **encryption**, **authentication**, **integrity (HMAC)**, and **anti-replay protection** in securing a VPN tunnel. State TWO common attacks on VPNs and their mitigations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company uses IPsec ESP in Tunnel Mode with AES-128-CBC encryption and HMAC-SHA-256 authentication. An internal TCP segment is 1460 bytes. Calculate the total size of the outer encapsulated packet (IPv4). If the path MTU is 1500 bytes, what adjustment must be made to avoid fragmentation?

**Model Answer (a) – 7 Marks:**

**Security Mechanisms in Cloud VPN (4 marks):**

1. **Encryption (Confidentiality)** *(1 mark)* – The original IP packet's payload is transformed into ciphertext using symmetric ciphers (default in cloud: **AES-256-GCM**). Without the symmetric key, an eavesdropper on the Internet sees only random bytes. The key is exchanged securely via Diffie-Hellman during IKEv2.

2. **Authentication (Peer Identity)** *(1 mark)* – The VPN gateway proves its identity to the peer using either a **Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** or, more securely, an **X.509 digital certificate** signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA). AWS Certificate Manager and Azure Key Vault can manage these certificates.

3. **Integrity (HMAC)** *(1 mark)* – Every ESP packet carries an **Integrity Check Value (ICV)** computed using HMAC-SHA-256 (or SHA-384) over the ESP header + encrypted payload + ESP trailer. The receiver recomputes the HMAC and drops the packet if it does not match, defeating **bit-flipping** and **man-in-the-middle** tampering.

4. **Anti-Replay Protection** *(1 mark)* – ESP uses a monotonically increasing 32-bit **Sequence Number** and a sliding receive window (default 32 or 64 packets). Any packet whose sequence number is outside the window is discarded, defeating **replay attacks** where a captured packet is re-injected.

**Two Common VPN Attacks and Mitigations (2 marks – 1 each):**

1. **Attack: Brute-Force / Weak Pre-Shared Key.** *Mitigation:* Use long (≥ 32 character) random PSKs OR, preferably, **X.509 certificates** with at least 2048-bit RSA keys. Enable **MFA** on the VPN portal (for client VPN).

2. **Attack: IKEv1 Aggressive Mode Credential Capture** (offline dictionary attack). *Mitigation:* **Disable IKEv1 entirely**; use IKEv2 with certificate-based authentication and **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)**. Additionally, restrict the VPN gateway's source IPs via security group / NSG rules.

---

**Model Answer (b) – 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 – Identify all headers and trailers added (1 mark)**

| Component | Size (bytes) |
|---|---|
| Outer IPv4 Header | 20 |
| ESP Header (SPI + Sequence) | 8 |
| ESP IV (AES-128-CBC block) | 16 |
| Padding (0–15, to align to 16) | 0–15 (assume 0 for now) |
| Pad Length + Next Header fields | 2 |
| ESP ICV (HMAC-SHA-256 truncated) | 16 |
| **Total Overhead** | **62** |

**Step 2 – Compute outer packet size (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Outer Packet Size} = \text{Inner Payload} + \text{ESP Overhead} + \text{Outer IP Header}
$$

$$
\text{Outer Packet Size} = 1460 + 62 = 1522 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 – Compare with Path MTU (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Outer Packet} = 1522 \text{ bytes} > \text{Path MTU} = 1500 \text{ bytes}
$$

This exceeds the standard Ethernet MTU by 22 bytes → the packet would be **fragmented**, which IPsec anti-replay cannot handle (a fragmented packet's ESP sequence number is only in the first fragment, leaving subsequent fragments vulnerable to replay).

**Step 4 – Apply Mitigation (2 marks)**

The standard mitigation is to **reduce the TCP MSS (Maximum Segment Size) on the customer gateway** so that the inner TCP payload never produces an outer packet > 1500 bytes.

The Maximum Permissible Inner TCP Payload $M$ is computed as:

$$
M + 62 = 1500
$$

$$
M = 1500 - 62 = 1438 \text{ bytes}
$$

The corresponding TCP MSS is:

$$
\text{MSS} = M - 20 \text{ (TCP header)} = 1418 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Configure the VPN gateway's TCP MSS clamping to **1418 bytes** (or set the interface MTU to **1438 bytes**). This ensures that no outer packet exceeds 1500 bytes, eliminating fragmentation and preserving IPsec anti-replay protection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the Outer IP Header** in the overhead calculation. Many students add ESP overhead (42 bytes) but forget the new IP header. Always remember: ESP Tunnel Mode adds **TWO IP headers** total in the path.
> 2. **Confusing HMAC-SHA-256 truncation** – The full output is 32 bytes, but RFC 4868 permits truncation to 16 bytes. State your assumption explicitly.
> 3. **Stating "set MTU to 1500"** as a fix – the *outer* MTU is 1500, but the *inner* MTU must be reduced. The correct answer is to **reduce TCP MSS to 1380–1418** (CSPs like AWS recommend 1387 for IPsec).
> 4. **Writing "IPsec encrypts the IP header"** – Only ESP in **Tunnel Mode** encrypts the inner IP header. In Transport Mode the inner IP header is in the clear. Use the precise term.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points for KTU Module 2 – Cloud VPN:**

- **Definition core:** VPN = **virtual** + **encrypted tunnel** over public network. It is *not* a separate physical network.

- **Three security properties:** **CIA** – Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication. (All three are required for a "secure" VPN per NIST SP 800-77.)

- **Two IPsec protocols:** **AH** (integrity only, IP proto 51) vs **ESP** (CIA, IP proto 50). Cloud VPNs use **ESP**.

- **Two IPsec modes:** **Transport** (host-to-host, payload only) vs **Tunnel** (gateway-to-gateway, entire inner IP packet). Cloud site-to-site = **Tunnel Mode ESP always**.

- **IKEv2 is the modern standard** (RFC 7296, 4 messages, single phase). Avoid IKEv1 in 2024 designs.

- **Diffie-Hellman** provides **forward secrecy** if a new key is generated per Child SA. Recommended DH group: **Group 14 (2048-bit)** or higher.

- **Key sizes for FIPS 140-2 compliance:** AES = **128/192/256 bits**; RSA = **2048+ bits**; EC = **256+ bits**.

- **Cloud VPN Services to remember for KTU:**
  * **AWS:** Virtual Private Gateway (VPG) + Customer Gateway (CGW) + Transit Gateway (TGW); also **AWS Client VPN** (SSL-based).
  * **Azure:** VPN Gateway (Basic, VpnGw1–5 SKUs) + Local Network Gateway; Point-to-Site (P2S) uses SSTP/IKEv2/OpenVPN.
  * **GCP:** Cloud VPN (HA VPN uses two interfaces for 99.99% SLA; Classic VPN is deprecated).

- **MTU default for IPsec cloud tunnels:** **1436 bytes** (AWS) / **1350 bytes** (Azure with heavy overhead). Always enable **TCP MSS clamping** to ~**1387 bytes**.

- **Anti-Replay Window:** ESP uses a **sliding window of 32 or 64 packets**; sequence numbers wrap at $2^{32}$ which mandates re-keying before exhaustion.

- **PFS (Perfect Forward Secrecy):** Even if the long-term private key is compromised, past sessions remain secure. **Always enable** in production cloud VPNs.

- **Common exam-ready acronyms to memorize cold:**
  * **SA** = Security Association (simplex).
  * **SPI** = Security Parameters Index (32-bit).
  * **IKE** = Internet Key Exchange.
  * **ISAKMP** = Internet Security Association and Key Management Protocol.
  * **DPD** = Dead Peer Detection.
  * **NAT-T** = NAT Traversal (uses UDP 4500).
  * **MOBIKE** = Mobile IKE (IKEv2 only, allows IP roaming).
  * **GCM** = Galois/Counter Mode (AEAD, provides both encryption and integrity in one step).
  * **AEAD** = Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data.

- **What NOT to use in 2024:** **PPTP** (broken RC4 + MS-CHAPv2), **IKEv1 Aggressive Mode**, **DES/3DES** (deprecated by NIST since 2023), **MD5/SHA-1** for HMAC.

- **Compliance hook:** For RBI Cyber Security Framework, financial institutions **must** use IPsec VPN with AES-256 + IKEv2 + PFS for any inter-DC cloud traffic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Balancing in Cloud: Load Balancer Types, Configurations, and Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Load Balancing in Cloud: Load Balancer Types, Configurations, and Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Balancing (LB)** in cloud computing is the systematic and dynamic distribution of incoming network traffic, computational workloads, or service requests across a pool of backend resources (virtual machines, containers, physical servers, or microservices) using one or more intermediate devices called **Load Balancers (LBs)**. The primary objective is to optimize resource utilization, maximize throughput, minimize response time, prevent overload on any single node, and ensure **High Availability (HA)** and **fault tolerance** in a distributed cloud environment.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, load balancing is positioned as a **core pillar of cloud network architecture**, sitting between the client edge (Internet/Users) and the server farm (backend pool). It is governed by policies known as **Load Balancing Algorithms** and is implemented at different layers of the **TCP/IP or OSI reference model**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Toll Plaza System on a Highway**
>
> Imagine a 6-lane highway (your cloud application) that suddenly receives a massive influx of 1,000 cars (user requests) heading towards a single toll booth. If all cars funnel into one lane, traffic jams, breakdowns, and waiting times skyrocket. The solution? A **traffic controller (Load Balancer)** stands at the entry point and dynamically directs cars to the least crowded of 6 toll booths (backend servers). Some booths might be faster (more powerful VMs), some might be reserved for VIPs (specific request types), and if one booth catches fire, the controller instantly reroutes traffic away. This is **exactly** what a cloud load balancer does — but with HTTP requests, TCP packets, and virtual machines.

### 1.3 Why Load Balancing is Non-Negotiable in the Cloud

| Cloud Imperative | Role of Load Balancer |
|---|---|
| **Elasticity** | Adds/removes backend nodes without service disruption |
| **High Availability (99.99% SLA)** | Automatic failover when a node crashes |
| **Horizontal Scalability** | Distributes load across N replicated instances |
| **SSL Offloading** | Terminates encryption centrally, freeing backend CPUs |
| **Geographic Distribution** | Routes users to the nearest data center (GSLB) |

**Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):**
- **Throughput** (requests/sec)
- **Latency** (milliseconds)
- **Active Connections** (concurrent sessions)
- **Error Rate** (HTTP 5xx percentage)
- **CPU/Memory Utilization** (%) of backend pool
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Why" and "How"

Load balancing operates through a 4-stage decision pipeline:

1. **Request Reception** — The LB listens on a Virtual IP (VIP) address (e.g., `10.0.0.100:80`). This is the single entry point advertised via DNS.
2. **Health Probing** — The LB continuously sends **Health Checks** (HTTP `GET /health`, TCP SYN, or ICMP ping) to each backend. Unhealthy nodes are removed from the rotation pool.
3. **Algorithm Execution** — When a request arrives, the LB consults a scheduling algorithm (Round Robin, Least Connections, etc.) to pick a backend.
4. **Forwarding & Tracking** — The request is forwarded (typically via **NAT — Network Address Translation** or **Direct Server Return**). The LB tracks the session to maintain **persistence (stickiness)** for stateful applications.

### 2.2 Classification of Load Balancers (KTU High-Yield)

**A. By OSI Layer Placement:**

- **Layer 4 (Transport) LB:** Operates on **TCP/UDP headers** (IP, Port). Fast, low-latency, cannot inspect content. Examples: AWS NLB, HAProxy (TCP mode).
