[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** **Define Hybrid Cloud Connectivity. List any FOUR major challenges faced during the integration of public and private clouds.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Hybrid Cloud Connectivity is the set of networking technologies, protocols, and policies that enable secure, reliable, and scalable communication between an organization's on-premises private cloud infrastructure and one or more public cloud service providers (CSPs), forming a unified hybrid computing environment. **Four Major Challenges (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Network Latency and Jitter:** Variable RTT over the public internet degrades real-time application performance. 2. **Security and Attack Surface Expansion:** The interconnection creates additional entry points requiring encryption, segmentation, and Zero Trust enforcement. 3. **Compliance and Data Sovereignty:** Data crossing geographical boundaries may violate regulations like GDPR, HIPAA, or India's DPDPA 2023. 4. **Operational Complexity:** Managing two distinct environments with different APIs, monitoring tools, and skill requirements increases OPEX. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** **Explain the role of an IPsec VPN and a Direct Cloud Interconnect (such as AWS Direct Connect) in a hybrid cloud setup. Compare them on THREE parameters.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **IPsec VPN (1.5 marks):** An IPsec VPN creates an **encrypted tunnel over the public internet** between the on-premises network and the cloud VPC/VNet. It uses IKEv2 for key exchange and ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) for data encryption, typically employing AES-256-GCM and SHA-256. It is **cost-effective, quick to deploy**, and offers flexibility, but its performance is subject to internet variability. **Direct Cloud Interconnect (1 mark):** A Direct Cloud Interconnect (e.g., AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute) is a **dedicated, private fiber-optic link** between the customer's data center and the CSP's edge location. It provides **predictable latency, consistent bandwidth (1/10/100 Gbps), and higher SLAs (up to 99.99%)**. **Comparison Table (0.5 marks):** | Parameter | IPsec VPN | Direct Interconnect | |---|---|---| | **Cost** | Low (only internet needed) | High (dedicated port + carrier fees) | | **Latency** | Variable (10–80 ms) | Predictable, often < 5 ms | | **Bandwidth SLA** | Best-effort | Guaranteed (1–100 Gbps) | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A — [14 Marks]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **three-layer architecture of hybrid cloud connectivity**. Explain the role of each layer and identify at least TWO technologies/protocols associated with each layer. **(b) [7 Marks)** A multinational company connects its **on-premises data center in Chennai** to the **AWS Mumbai region** using an IPsec VPN. The distance is approximately **1300 km**. Given: - Propagation speed in fiber: $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s - Packet size: $P = 1200$ bytes - Bandwidth: $B = 50$ Mbps - Per-hop processing delay: $d_{proc} = 3$ ms - Number of hops: $H = 10$ - Traffic intensity: $\rho = 0.5$ **Calculate:** 1. Propagation delay 2. Transmission delay 3. Total processing delay 4. Queuing delay 5. Total RTT Discuss whether this link is **suitable for a video conferencing application** that requires RTT < 150 ms. --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:** The hybrid cloud connectivity architecture is organized into **three logical layers**, each handling a specific aspect of the end-to-end communication path. **Layer 1: Physical / Underlay Connectivity (2 marks)** This is the actual physical medium that carries the bits. - *Technologies:* Fiber-optic leased lines, MPLS circuits, 5G wireless, public internet, AWS Direct Connect / Azure ExpressRoute. - *Role:* Provides the raw bandwidth and physical reach between on-premises and cloud PoPs (Points of Presence). **Layer 2: Overlay / Encrypted Connectivity (2.5 marks)** This layer creates virtual, secure tunnels over the underlay. - *Technologies:* IPsec (IKEv2 + ESP), SSL/TLS VPN, WireGuard, GRE + IPsec, VXLAN, MACsec. - *Role:* Authenticates peers, encrypts payload, ensures confidentiality, integrity, and anti-replay protection. **Layer 3: Application / Service-Mesh Connectivity (2.5 marks)** This layer governs how distributed applications and microservices communicate across the hybrid boundary. - *Technologies:* API Gateways (Kong, AWS API Gateway), Service Meshes (Istio, Linkerd), DNS-based service discovery, CDN (CloudFront, Cloudflare), OAuth 2.0 / OIDC. - *Role:* Provides service-level routing, authentication, rate-limiting, observability, and policy enforcement. > A neatly labeled **block diagram** showing the three stacked layers — on top of an on-premises DC, connecting through a CSP region — would earn full marks. (Students should reproduce the diagram shown in Section 4.1.) --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Given Data:** - $L = 1300$ km $= 1.3 \times 10^6$ m - $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s - $P = 1200$ bytes $= 9600$ bits - $B = 50$ Mbps $= 50 \times 10^6$ bps - $d_{proc} = 3$ ms per hop - $H = 10$ hops - $\rho = 0.5$ **Step 1 — Propagation Delay (1 mark)** $$ d_{prop} = \frac{L}{v_{prop}} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 6.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6.5 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2 — Transmission Delay (1 mark)** $$ d_{trans} = \frac{P}{B} = \frac{9600}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 1.92 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.192 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Total Processing Delay (1 mark)** $$ d_{proc,total} = H \times d_{proc} = 10 \times 3 = 30 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 4 — Queuing Delay (1.5 marks)** Using M/M/1 approximation with $I = d_{trans} = 0.192$ ms: $$ d_{queue} = \frac{I \cdot \rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{0.192 \times 0.5}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{0.096}{0.5} = 0.192 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 5 — Total One-Way Delay and RTT (1.5 marks)** $$ d_{one\text{-}way} = d_{prop} + d_{trans} + d_{proc,total} + d_{queue} $$ $$ d_{one\text{-}way} = 6.5 + 0.192 + 30 + 0.192 = 36.884 \text{ ms} $$ $$ RTT = 2 \times d_{one\text{-}way} = 2 \times 36.884 = 73.77 \text{ ms} $$ **Conclusion on Video Conferencing Suitability (1 mark):** Since the calculated RTT of **73.77 ms** is well within the **< 150 ms** threshold, the link **IS suitable** for video conferencing. However, the per-hop processing delay (30 ms) dominates the budget — to improve quality, the number of hops should be reduced by using a **Direct Connect link** or an **SD-WAN** solution with intelligent path optimization. Video conferencing typically requires not just low RTT but also **low jitter (< 30 ms)** and **packet loss < 0.1%**, which should also be measured. --- #### **QUESTION B — [14 Marks]** (Alternative Choice) **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **key challenges in hybrid cloud integration**, and propose appropriate **network-level, security-level, and management-level solutions** for each. **(b) [7 Marks]** An enterprise deploys a **redundant hybrid cloud link** consisting of: - Path-1: IPsec VPN over ISP-1 with availability $A_1 = 0.98$ - Path-2: Direct Connect with availability $A_2 = 0.995$ Calculate: 1. The **combined availability** if both paths are used in **parallel redundancy**. 2. The **expected annual downtime** in hours. 3. Explain the **concept of BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection)** and how it enables sub-second failover in this setup. --- **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:** | # | Challenge | Network Solution | Security Solution | Management Solution | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Latency** | Direct Connect, CDN, Anycast, Edge nodes | N/A | Latency monitoring with synthetic probes | | 2 | **Bandwidth** | LACP, ECMP, WAN optimization, SD-WAN | QoS policies for traffic shaping | Capacity planning dashboards | | 3 | **Security** | Network segmentation, micro-segmentation | Zero Trust (NIST 800-207), mTLS, IPsec | SIEM, SOC, CASB | | 4 | **Compliance** | Geo-fencing, regional VPCs | DLP, encryption at rest and in transit | Audit logging, compliance-as-code | | 5 | **Identity Federation** | Hub-and-spoke DNS, service mesh | SAML 2.0 / OIDC SSO, MFA | SCIM-based user lifecycle management | | 6 | **Operational Complexity** | Unified IaC (Terraform), GitOps | Policy-as-code (OPA) | AIOps, FinOps, unified observability | | 7 | **Vendor Lock-in** | Kubernetes, multi-cloud CNI (Cilium) | Portable IAM (Keycloak) | OpenTofu, Crossplane | | 8 | **Disaster Recovery** | Multi-region active-passive, Pilot Light | Encrypted cross-region snapshots | Automated DR runbooks | (Each row worth 0.875 marks — distribution: ~2 challenges per category for depth.) --- **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Unavailability of each path (1 mark)** $$ U_1 = 1 - A_1 = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02 $$ $$ U_2 = 1 - A_2 = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005 $$ **Step 2 — Combined System Unavailability (Parallel) (1.5 marks)** For parallel redundancy, the system is down **only if all paths are down simultaneously**: $$ U_{system} = U_1 \times U_2 = 0.02 \times 0.005 = 0.0001 = 10^{-4} $$ **Step 3 — Combined Availability (1 mark)** $$ A_{system} = 1 - U_{system} = 1 - 10^{-4} = 0.9999 = 99.99\% $$ **Step 4 — Annual Downtime (1.5 marks)** Total minutes in a year: $525600$ minutes. $$ t_{down} = U_{system} \times 525600 = 10^{-4} \times 525600 = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year} $$ In hours: $$ t_{down} = \frac{52.56}{60} \approx 0.876 \text{ hours/year} \approx 52.56 \text{ minutes/year} $$ **Step 5 — BFD Explanation (2 marks)** **Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD)** is a lightweight, protocol-agnostic failure detection mechanism defined in **RFC 5880**. It works by exchanging rapid hello packets (typically every **50 ms**) between two adjacent systems. If a configurable number of consecutive packets (multiplier, e.g., 3) are missed, BFD declares the link down within **sub-second detection times** (often **< 150 ms**). In the hybrid cloud setup, BFD is configured between the on-premises edge router and the cloud VPN gateway (or the Direct Connect router). When BFD detects failure on Path-1, the routing protocol (e.g., BGP) withdraws the route, and traffic is **automatically rerouted to Path-2** via SD-WAN or ECMP, achieving near-instant failover — critical for voice, video, and transactional workloads. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Hybrid Cloud Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing "Hybrid Cloud" with "Multi-Cloud"** — Hybrid = Private + Public, typically with orchestration. Multi-Cloud = Multiple public providers, not necessarily with a private component. Examiners will deduct marks for this confusion. > > 2. **Forgetting to double the one-way delay for RTT** — A single missed multiplication by 2 in a latency question can cost 1.5–2 marks. > > 3. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Mbps vs MB/s, ms vs μs, km vs m. Always show unit conversions explicitly. > > 4. **Ignoring Encryption Overhead in Numerical Problems** — IPsec adds 50–70 bytes of overhead, affecting MTU and effective throughput. Examiners reward students who mention this. > > 5. **Drawing diagrams without labels or arrows** — An unlabeled diagram fetches 0 marks. Always include: **block names, protocol labels, and directional arrows**. > > 6. **Memorizing but not applying** — Don't just list "VPN, Direct Connect, SD-WAN" — explain **when to use which**, with trade-offs. > > 7. **Ignoring the 2024 DPDPA (Digital Personal Data Protection Act)** — This is the **Indian data protection law** and is highly relevant for compliance questions in KTU 2024. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for ESE — Last-Minute Quick Reference** - [x] **Hybrid Cloud** = Private Cloud + Public Cloud + Orchestration Layer. **NOT the same as Multi-Cloud.** - [x] **Three Connection Methods:** IPsec VPN (cheap, variable), Direct Connect (expensive, reliable), SD-WAN (intelligent, hybrid). - [x] **IPsec Components:** IKEv2 (key exchange) + ESP (encryption) + AH (integrity, optional). Modern cipher: **AES-256-GCM**. - [x] **Direct Connect Providers:** Equinix, Megaport, Colt. Speeds: 1, 10, 100 Gbps. SLAs: 99.9% to 99.99%. - [x] **Transit Gateway** eliminates O(n²) full-mesh peering complexity. - [x] **Latency Formula:** $RTT = 2 \cdot (d_{prop} + d_{trans} + d_{proc} + d_{queue})$ - [x] **BDP Formula:** $BDP = B \times RTT$ — determines minimum TCP window size. - [x] **MTU Reduction:** IPsec = 50–70 bytes overhead → set inner MTU to **1438 bytes**. - [x] **Parallel Availability:** $A_{system} = 1 - \prod U_i$ — multiplies unavailabilities, not availabilities. - [x] **Compliance Laws to Remember:** **GDPR** (EU), **HIPAA** (US Healthcare), **PCI-DSS** (Payments), **DPDPA 2023** (India). - [x] **Identity Federation Protocols:** **SAML 2.0** (Enterprise SSO), **OAuth 2.0** (Authorization), **OIDC** (Authentication on top of OAuth). - [x] **Zero Trust Model (NIST SP 800-207):** "Never trust, always verify" — replaces perimeter-based security. - [x] **SASE** (Secure Access Service Edge) = SD-WAN + CASB + ZTNA + SWG (Secure Web Gateway). - [x] **BFD Detection Time:** Typically **< 150 ms** with 50 ms hello × 3 multiplier. - [x] **Video Conferencing RTT Threshold:** **< 150 ms** (ITU-T G.114). Voice: < 100 ms one-way. Gaming: < 50 ms. - [x] **Default AWS Direct Connect SLA:** **99.9%** monthly uptime. Azure ExpressRoute: **99.95%**. - [x] **Top Public Cloud Providers (Market Share 2024):** AWS (~32%), Azure (~23%), GCP (~11%). - [x] **Cost Watch:** Cloud **egress** (data leaving the cloud) is the most expensive charge. Inter-region traffic is typically **$0.02–$0.09 per GB**. --- **End of Module 2 Notes — Hybrid Cloud Connectivity** *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PECCT521 | Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Virtual Networking and SDN: Virtual Networks (VNet) Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Virtual Networking and SDN: Virtual Networks (VNet) Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Virtual Network (VNet)** in cloud computing is a logically isolated software-defined network overlay that runs on top of a shared physical network infrastructure. It enables cloud tenants to provision, configure, and manage private IP address spaces, subnets, routing policies, and security boundaries entirely through **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** control planes without any dependence on underlying Layer 2/3 hardware configurations.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, a VNet is treated as the **fundamental networking primitive** of Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) cloud platforms, providing tenants with the following first-class configuration objects:

- **Address Space** — A contiguous CIDR block of private IPv4 (and optionally IPv6) ranges.
- **Subnets** — Logical subdivisions of the VNet address space mapped to specific workload tiers.
- **Route Tables** — User-defined forwarding rules overriding the default system routes.
- **Network Security Groups (NSGs)** — Stateful Layer 3/4 access-control lists.
- **VNet Peering** — Non-transitive, low-latency links between VNets.
- **Service Endpoints / Private Endpoints** — Private connectivity to PaaS services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A VNet is *not* a VLAN. While VLANs are Layer 2 broadcast-domain partitions tied to physical switches, VNets are **Layer 3 software constructs** built via overlay tunnels (VXLAN/GRE) orchestrated by an SDN controller. This is a frequently tested conceptual distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **co-working office building** (the physical data center) shared by many different companies (cloud tenants). Each company needs its own private office space where they can:

- Choose their own **room numbering scheme** (address space).
- Divide their office into **departments** — HR, Engineering, Finance (subnets).
- Post **security guards** at certain doorways with strict access rules (NSGs).
- Set up **internal mail routes** between departments (route tables).
- Build **private walkways** to neighboring offices they trust (VNet peering).
- Reserve **dedicated elevator shafts** to a specific building service like a bank vault (service endpoints).

The building's **facility manager** (the SDN controller) enforces all of this without the companies ever touching the building's electrical wiring or elevators (physical hardware). That facility manager — the SDN control plane — is what makes a VNet a true *software-defined* network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** The default minimum VNet address space in Azure is **/29 (8 addresses)**, and the maximum is **/8 (16,777,216 addresses)**. Each VNet can contain up to **3,000 subnets** under standard Azure limits — a number worth remembering for Part A questions.

## 1.3 SDN's Role in VNet Configuration

Software-Defined Networking decouples the **control plane** (decision-making) from the **data plane** (packet forwarding). In VNet configuration, this manifests as:

- The cloud customer interacts with a **northbound API** (Azure Portal, REST, PowerShell, CLI).
- The **SDN controller** translates these declarative intents into flow rules.
- **Virtual switches (vSwitches)** on hypervisors and **virtual routers** enforce the rules at the **southbound interface** (Open vSwitch, OVSDB, OpenFlow).

This abstraction is what makes VNet configuration *programmable*, *reproducible*, and *tenant-isolated* — three properties traditional networking cannot deliver at scale.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 VNet Configuration Architecture — Layered Breakdown

A VNet is composed of six logically distinct configuration layers, each governed by the SDN controller:

### Layer 1 — Address Space Allocation
- Tenant declares one or more **non-overlapping CIDR blocks** (e.g., `10.0.0.0/16`).
- The SDN controller reserves this range in its **global tenant routing table**.
- Sub-blocks are **carved out as subnets**; the gateway subnet (e.g., `/27`) is reserved for VPN/ExpressRoute gateways.

### Layer 2 — Subnet Segmentation
- Subnets are **non-overlapping** divisions of the VNet address space.
- Each subnet is bound to an **availability zone** (zone-redundant deployment) or a specific physical rack.
- The **first three addresses** in every Azure subnet are reserved:
  - `x.x.x.0` — Network address
  - `x.x.x.1` — Default gateway (virtual router)
  - `x.x.x.2`, `x.x.x.3` — Azure DNS mapping

### Layer 3 — Route Table Construction
- **System default routes** are automatically created for:
  - Local VNet traffic (intra-VNet)
  - On-premises traffic via gateway
  - Internet-bound traffic via default route `0.0.0.0/0`
- **User-Defined Routes (UDRs)** allow overriding these defaults — typically used to force traffic through a Network Virtual Appliance (NVA) for inspection.

### Layer 4 — Network Security Group (NSG) Binding
- NSGs contain **inbound and outbound security rules** with a 5-tuple match: *Source IP, Source Port, Destination IP, Destination Port, Protocol*.
- Default rules have lower priority (higher numerical value); custom rules have higher priority (lower numerical value, evaluated first).
- NSGs can be bound at the **subnet level** (preferred) or the **NIC level**.

### Layer 5 — Peering & Connectivity
- **VNet Peering** uses the underlying provider's backbone (never the public internet).
- It is **non-transitive**: VNet A peered to B, B peered to C → A *cannot* reach C automatically.
- Gateways can be configured as transitive pivots if needed.

### Layer 6 — Service Endpoints & Private Endpoints
- **Service Endpoints** route PaaS traffic over the Azure backbone by extending the VNet identity to the PaaS service.
- **Private Endpoints** inject a **network interface (NIC)** with a private IP from your VNet into the PaaS service — true private connectivity.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable under Module 3 of PECCT521. Pay particular attention to subnetting math — it is a guaranteed question in every KTU ESE cycle.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description |
|---|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Number of usable hosts in a subnet | $H = 2^{n} - 2$ | $n$ = number of host bits. Azure reserves 5 addresses, so $H_{Azure} = 2^{n} - 5$ |
| 2 | Number of subnets from a parent block | $S = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = number of borrowed bits |
| 3 | CIDR block to subnet mask | $M = 32 - p$ | $p$ = prefix length, $M$ = host bits |
| 4 | Wildcard mask | $W = 2^{M} - 1$ | $W$ = inverse of subnet mask, used in NSG rules |
| 5 | New prefix when splitting a /N into equal subnets | $P_{new} = N + \log_{2}(k)$ | $k$ = number of subnets required |
| 6 | Address range of subnet $i$ | $\text{Range}_{i} = [\text{Base} + i \cdot 2^{M}, \text{Base} + (i+1) \cdot 2^{M} - 1]$ | Used to validate non-overlap |
| 7 | VNet address space minimum | $\vert \text{Addr}\vert_{\min} = /8$ | 16,777,216 addresses |
| 8 | VNet address space maximum (Azure) | $\vert \text{Addr}\vert_{\max} = /29$ | 8 addresses (smallest usable) |
| 9 | Azure reserved IPs per subnet | 5 | $x.x.x.0, x.x.x.1, x.x.x.2, x.x.x.3, \text{last}$ |
| 10 | Peering non-transitivity | $T = 0$ | $T$ = transitively reachable paths between peered VNets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write $H = 2^{n} - 2$ for Azure subnets. While technically correct for classical IPv4 networking, Azure reserves **5 addresses** per subnet, so the *effective* usable count is $H_{Azure} = 2^{n} - 5$. Examiners award the mark for the correct platform-specific formula.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

VNet configuration underpins virtually every modern cloud deployment:

- **Multi-tier web applications** use subnets to separate web servers, application servers, and databases — implementing defense-in-depth via NSGs.
- **Hybrid cloud architectures** use VNet peering + VPN Gateway to seamlessly extend on-premises Active Directory and file shares into Azure.
- **Microsegmentation in Zero-Trust models** is achieved by binding granular NSGs to individual NICs.
- **Disaster Recovery (DR)** topologies rely on **global VNet peering** across regions for sub-second replication.
- **Kubernetes (AKS)** deployments integrate with VNets via the **Azure CNI plugin**, which assigns real VNet IPs to pods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Subnetting a /16 VNet into Equal /20 Subnets

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A cloud architect provisions an Azure VNet with address space `10.20.0.0/16`. The architect must create **four equal-sized subnets** for Web, App, DB, and Management tiers. Compute the network address, usable host range, broadcast address, and wildcard mask for each subnet.

### Step 1 — Identify the parent and target prefix length

The parent block is `10.20.0.0/16`, meaning $N = 16$ prefix bits and $M = 32 - 16 = 16$ host bits.

The architect needs 4 equal subnets. Using the new-prefix formula:

$$P_{\text{new}} = N + \log_{2}(k) = 16 + \log_{2}(4) = 16 + 2 = 18$$

Wait — four subnets of size $\frac{2^{16}}{4} = 2^{14}$ addresses each would give a new prefix of $32 - 14 = 18$? Let us recompute carefully. We need *each* subnet to be /20, so $P_{\text{new}} = 20$.

$$k = 2^{P_{\text{new}} - N} = 2^{20 - 16} = 2^{4} = 16 \text{ subnets total}$$

Since we need only 4, we use 4 of the 16 available. Each subnet has:

$$M_{\text{new}} = 32 - 20 = 12 \text{ host bits}$$

### Step 2 — Compute hosts and ranges

Total addresses per subnet:

$$A = 2^{M_{\text{new}}} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ addresses}$$

Block size (increment between subnet network addresses):

$$B = 2^{M_{\text{new}}} = 4096$$

In dotted-decimal, $B = 4096 = 16 \times 256$, so the third octet increments by 16.

### Step 3 — Derive the four subnets

Let us build the table:

| Subnet | Network Address | First Usable | Last Usable (Azure: $2^{n}-5$ rule) | Broadcast (classical) | Wildcard Mask |
|--------|----------------|--------------|----------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Web | 10.20.0.0/20 | 10.20.0.4 | 10.20.15.250 | 10.20.15.255 | 0.0.15.255 |
| App | 10.20.16.0/20 | 10.20.16.4 | 10.20.31.250 | 10.20.31.255 | 0.0.15.255 |
| DB | 10.20.32.0/20 | 10.20.32.4 | 10.20.47.250 | 10.20.47.255 | 0.0.15.255 |
| Mgmt | 10.20.48.0/20 | 10.20.48.4 | 10.20.63.250 | 10.20.63.255 | 0.0.15.255 |

### Step 4 — Wildcard mask derivation

For a /20 mask, the network portion is the first 20 bits, so the wildcard is the inverted 12 host bits:

$$W = \overline{255.255.240.0} = 0.0.15.255$$

In binary form, the last 12 bits are all 1s, and the first 20 bits are all 0s. Converting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Network bits (20)} &: \underbrace{11111111.11111111.1111}_{20 \text{ ones}}.00000000.00000000 \\
\text{Wildcard bits (12)} &: \underbrace{00000000.00000000.0000}_{20 \text{ zeros}}.11111111.11111111 \\
\text{Decimal} &: 0.0.15.255
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Validate non-overlap

To ensure the subnets are contiguous and non-overlapping, verify the boundary condition:

$$\text{Range}_i^{\text{end}} + 1 = \text{Range}_{i+1}^{\text{start}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Web end} + 1 &= 10.20.15.255 + 1 = 10.20.16.0 = \text{App start} \checkmark \\
\text{App end} + 1 &= 10.20.31.255 + 1 = 10.20.32.0 = \text{DB start} \checkmark \\
\text{DB end} + 1 &= 10.20.47.255 + 1 = 10.20.48.0 = \text{Mgmt start} \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Logic:** The boundary check $E_i + 1 = S_{i+1}$ guarantees (a) no gaps in addressing, (b) no overlapping subnets, and (c) the complete coverage of the parent /16 address space. KTU examiners award full marks only when this validation step is explicitly shown.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Computing the Number of Hosts with Azure's 5-Address Reservation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** Design a subnet within `10.0.0.0/24` that will host approximately 250 virtual machines, leaving headroom for Azure's reserved addresses and future growth. Choose the *smallest* subnet that satisfies the requirement.

### Step 1 — Classical calculation

For 250 hosts using the classical formula:

$$2^{n} - 2 \geq 250 \implies 2^{n} \geq 252 \implies n = 8 \text{ (since } 2^{8} = 256\text{)}$$

A /24 has 8 host bits, giving exactly 254 usable hosts classically — *not* enough headroom.

### Step 2 — Expand to /23

We need headroom. Try $n = 9$ (a /23 subnet):

$$2^{9} - 2 = 510 \text{ classical hosts}$$

For Azure, the usable count is:

$$H_{Azure} = 2^{9} - 5 = 512 - 5 = 507 \text{ usable hosts}$$

### Step 3 — Verify the chosen subnet

Since $507 \geq 250$, a /23 is the smallest sufficient subnet. The subnet is:

$$\text{Network} = 10.0.0.0/23, \quad \text{Range} = [10.0.0.0, 10.0.1.255]$$

Usable Azure range: `10.0.0.4` to `10.0.1.250` (excluding the 5 reserved addresses: `.0`, `.1`, `.2`, `.3`, and `1.255`).

### Step 4 — Headroom verification

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Required} &= 250 \\
\text{Available} &= 507 \\
\text{Headroom} &= 507 - 250 = 257 \text{ addresses} \\
\text{Headroom \%} &= \frac{257}{507} \times 100 \approx 50.7\%
\end{aligned}$$

This 50.7% headroom is acceptable for production workloads per Azure's Well-Architected Framework guidance.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — VNet Peering Bandwidth and Latency Decision

When designing a hub-spoke topology, an architect must choose between **VNet Peering** and **VPN Gateway** for spoke-to-spoke communication. The decision is driven by:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Peering bandwidth} &= \text{up to } 100 \text{ Gbps (same region)} \\
\text{VPN Gateway bandwidth} &= \text{up to } 1.25 \text{ Gbps (VpnGw3 SKU)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Latency} : \quad t_{\text{peer}} < 1 \text{ ms intra-region}, \quad t_{\text{VPN}} \approx 5{-}10 \text{ ms}$$

For latency-sensitive workloads (e.g., real-time financial trading), peering is the only acceptable choice despite the higher cost.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — VNet Logical Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Region1["Azure Region East US"]
        subgraph HubVNet["Hub VNet 10.0.0.0/16"]
            Gateway["GatewaySubnet /27 - VPN Gateway"]

            SharedSvc["Shared Services Subnet /24 - AD, DNS"]
        end

        subgraph Spoke1VNet["Spoke1 VNet 10.1.0.0/16"]

        subgraph Spoke2VNet["Spoke2 VNet 10.2.0.0/16"]

            TestSub["Test Subnet /24"]

        Peering1["VNet Peering - Non-Transitive"]
        Peering2["VNet Peering - Non-Transitive"]
        UDR["User Defined Route Table - 0.0.0.0/0 to NVA"]

    end

    Internet["Internet"]

    Internet -->|HTTPS 443| NSGWeb
```
