
## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — VNet Configuration Workflow (Declarative → Enforced)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Tenant declares intent via Azure Portal / REST API / PowerShell"]
    B["Step 2: ARM Template or Terraform validates CIDR blocks for non-overlap"]
    C["Step 3: SDN Controller receives declarative spec"]
    D["Step 4: SDN Controller programs vSwitches via OpenFlow / OVSDB"]
    E["Step 5: Virtual Router instances install UDRs in flow tables"]
    F["Step 6: NSG rules pushed to vSwitch ACLs"]
    G["Step 7: VNet peering state synchronized across regions"]
    H["Step 8: Health probes verify reachability - Network Watcher"]
    I["Step 9: Continuous compliance monitoring - Azure Policy"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    C --> F
    C --> G
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Subnet Allocation Tree

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root["Parent Block 10.20.0.0/16 - 65536 addresses"]
    Root --> S1["Web /20 - 10.20.0.0/20"]
    Root --> S2["App /20 - 10.20.16.0/20"]
    Root --> S3["DB /20 - 10.20.32.0/20"]
    Root --> S4["Mgmt /20 - 10.20.48.0/20"]
    Root --> S5["Reserved /20 - 10.20.64.0/20"]
    Root --> S6["... up to 16 subnets /20"]
    Root --> S16["Last /20 - 10.20.240.0/20"]

    S1 --> W1["WebVM01 .4"]
    S1 --> W2["WebVM02 .5"]
    S1 --> W3["... up to .4090"]
    S1 --> W4["Reserved .0 .1 .2 .3 .4095"]

    S3 --> D1["DBPrimary .4"]
    S3 --> D2["DBReplica .5"]
    S3 --> D3["... up to .4090"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define a Virtual Network (VNet) in the context of cloud computing. List any four configuration components of an Azure VNet.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Virtual Network (VNet)** is a logically isolated, software-defined network overlay that enables cloud tenants to provision private IP address spaces and define their own subnet topology, routing, and security policies over a shared physical infrastructure using Software-Defined Networking (SDN) control planes.

**Four configuration components of an Azure VNet:**

1. **Address Space** — CIDR blocks defining the private IP range (e.g., `10.0.0.0/16`).
2. **Subnets** — Non-overlapping subdivisions of the address space for workload isolation.
3. **Network Security Groups (NSGs)** — Stateful ACLs that filter inbound/outbound traffic at subnet or NIC level.
4. **Route Tables (UDRs)** — User-defined forwarding rules that override default system routes, typically used to direct traffic through NVAs.

*(Optional 5th: VNet Peering — non-transitive links to other VNets.)*

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Four components with brief explanation: 2 marks (0.5 each)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
*Differentiate between VNet Peering and VPN Gateway in terms of three parameters: (i) transitiveness, (ii) bandwidth, and (iii) traffic path.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | VNet Peering | VPN Gateway |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| (i) Transitiveness | **Non-transitive** (A↔B, B↔C does not imply A↔C) | Transitive only if explicitly configured via routing |
| (ii) Bandwidth | **Up to 100 Gbps** intra-region | Up to 1.25 Gbps (VpnGw3 SKU), typically 650 Mbps |
| (iii) Traffic Path | Provider's **private backbone** (never the public internet) | Encrypted tunnel over the **public internet** (IPsec/IKE) |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Three parameters correctly contrasted: 3 marks (1 each)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks in Part B by (1) forgetting to validate that VNet address spaces are **non-overlapping** before designing peering, (2) using the classical $2^{n}-2$ formula for Azure subnets instead of $2^{n}-5$, and (3) failing to mention the **non-transitive** nature of VNet peering when sketching hub-spoke topologies. Memorize these three points.

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Pattern]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Explain the architecture of a Software-Defined Network (SDN) as applied to VNet configuration. With a neat diagram, describe the roles of the Northbound API, SDN Controller, and Southbound Interface.*

**Model Answer:**

**SDN Architecture for VNet Configuration (7 marks):**

Software-Defined Networking decouples the network's **control plane** from the **data plane**, centralizing intelligence in a software controller. When applied to VNet provisioning, SDN has three hierarchical layers:

**1. Application Layer (Northbound API) — 2 marks**
- Cloud tenants interact through declarative interfaces: Azure Portal, Azure CLI, PowerShell, REST APIs, Terraform, or ARM templates.
- The tenant expresses *intent* (e.g., "create VNet `10.0.0.0/16` with three subnets and an NSG allowing port 443").
- The API translates this intent into a structured request forwarded to the controller.

**2. Control Layer (SDN Controller / Network Operating System) — 3 marks**
- The SDN controller (e.g., Azure Fabric Controller) is the brain of the VNet.
- It performs **admission control** (validating CIDR non-overlap across tenants), **resource allocation** (assigning physical hosts, VLAN IDs, VXLAN VNI), **state distribution** (synchronizing flow rules across regions), and **telemetry** (collecting metrics for Network Watcher).
- It maintains the **global topology view** of all VNets, peerings, and routes.

**3. Infrastructure Layer (Southbound Interface + Data Plane) — 2 marks**
- The controller communicates with physical and virtual switches via **OpenFlow**, **OVSDB**, or vendor-specific protocols (e.g., Cisco NX-API).
- Hypervisor-level **vSwitches** (Hyper-V Virtual Switch, Open vSwitch) enforce isolation through **VXLAN encapsulation** — each VNet gets a unique 24-bit VNI.
- Packets entering a VM are tagged with the VNI; only switches aware of that VNI forward the packet, providing tenant isolation over shared hardware.

**Diagram (must be drawn by student):** Three-layer stack showing Northbound API → SDN Controller → Southbound → vSwitches/Physical Switches → VMs.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Three layers named and explained: 4 marks
- Protocols and mechanisms (OpenFlow, VXLAN): 2 marks
- Neat diagram: 1 mark

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A cloud architect needs to provision an Azure VNet with address space `172.16.0.0/16` and divide it into **8 equal subnets** for a multi-tier application. Compute the network address, usable host range, and broadcast address for each subnet. Validate that the subnets are non-overlapping.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine new prefix length (1 mark)**

$$P_{\text{new}} = N + \log_{2}(k) = 16 + \log_{2}(8) = 16 + 3 = 19$$

Each subnet is a /19, with $M_{\text{new}} = 32 - 19 = 13$ host bits.

**Step 2 — Compute block size (1 mark)**

$$B = 2^{13} = 8192 \text{ addresses per subnet}$$

In dotted-decimal, $8192 = 32 \times 256$, so the third octet increments by 32.

**Step 3 — Derive the 8 subnets (3 marks)**

| # | Subnet Name | Network Address | First Usable | Last Usable (Azure) | Broadcast (Classical) |
|---|-------------|-----------------|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Tier1 | 172.16.0.0/19 | 172.16.0.4 | 172.16.31.250 | 172.16.31.255 |
| 2 | Tier2 | 172.16.32.0/19 | 172.16.32.4 | 172.16.63.250 | 172.16.63.255 |
| 3 | Tier3 | 172.16.64.0/19 | 172.16.64.4 | 172.16.95.250 | 172.16.95.255 |
| 4 | Tier4 | 172.16.96.0/19 | 172.16.96.4 | 172.16.127.250 | 172.16.127.255 |
| 5 | Tier5 | 172.16.128.0/19 | 172.16.128.4 | 172.16.159.250 | 172.16.159.255 |
| 6 | Tier6 | 172.16.160.0/19 | 172.16.160.4 | 172.16.191.250 | 172.16.191.255 |
| 7 | Tier7 | 172.16.192.0/19 | 172.16.192.4 | 172.16.223.250 | 172.16.223.255 |
| 8 | Tier8 | 172.16.224.0/19 | 172.16.224.4 | 172.16.255.250 | 172.16.255.255 |

**Step 4 — Non-overlap validation (2 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Tier1 broadcast} + 1 &= 172.16.31.255 + 1 = 172.16.32.0 = \text{Tier2 network} \checkmark \\
\text{Tier2 broadcast} + 1 &= 172.16.63.255 + 1 = 172.16.64.0 = \text{Tier3 network} \checkmark \\
\text{Tier3 broadcast} + 1 &= 172.16.95.255 + 1 = 172.16.96.0 = \text{Tier4 network} \checkmark \\
\text{Tier4 broadcast} + 1 &= 172.16.127.255 + 1 = 172.16.128.0 = \text{Tier5 network} \checkmark \\
\text{Tier5 broadcast} + 1 &= 172.16.159.255 + 1 = 172.16.160.0 = \text{Tier6 network} \checkmark \\
\text{Tier6 broadcast} + 1 &= 172.16.191.255 + 1 = 172.16.192.0 = \text{Tier7 network} \checkmark \\
\text{Tier7 broadcast} + 1 &= 172.16.223.255 + 1 = 172.16.224.0 = \text{Tier8 network} \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

All 8 subnets are **contiguous and non-overlapping**, completely covering the parent `172.16.0.0/16` block.

**Mark Distribution:**
- New prefix calculation: 1 mark
- Block size and increment: 1 mark
- Correct subnet table: 3 marks
- Non-overlap validation with all 7 boundary checks: 2 marks

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*With a neat diagram, explain the Hub-Spoke VNet topology in Azure. Discuss its advantages and the role of User-Defined Routes (UDRs) in this topology.*

**Model Answer:**

**Hub-Spoke VNet Topology (7 marks):**

The **Hub-Spoke** topology is a reference architecture where a central **Hub VNet** acts as a connectivity point for multiple **Spoke VNets**. The Hub hosts shared services (firewall, VPN/ExpressRoute gateway, Active Directory); the Spokes host individual workloads.

**Diagram (must be drawn):**
- Central Hub VNet with: GatewaySubnet (VPN/ER), NVA Subnet (Firewall), Shared Services Subnet (DNS, AD)
- Multiple Spoke VNets (Spoke1, Spoke2, Spoke3) each containing workload subnets
- Peering links from each Spoke to the Hub (with bidirectional peering enabled)
- UDRs in each Spoke pointing default route `0.0.0.0/0` to the Hub's NVA

**Advantages — 3 marks:**
1. **Centralized security**: All egress traffic from Spokes is forced through the Hub's NVA/firewall via UDRs, providing a single chokepoint for inspection.
2. **Cost optimization**: One VPN/ExpressRoute gateway in the Hub serves all Spokes, avoiding redundant gateway SKUs.
3. **Isolation**: Spokes are isolated from each other (peering is non-transitive), so a compromise in one Spoke does not propagate to others.
4. **Scalability**: New Spokes can be added by simply creating a peering link, with no changes to existing Spokes.
5. **Centralized shared services**: AD, DNS, patching servers reside in the Hub, reducing duplication.

**Role of UDRs — 2 marks:**
- By default, peered VNets communicate directly; traffic does *not* traverse the Hub.
- A Spoke's UDR with `0.0.0.0/0 → NVA_IP` forces all outbound internet-bound and inter-Spoke traffic through the Hub's firewall.
- This is how the **non-transitive** nature of peering is converted into a *controlled transitive* behavior using routing.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Diagram with all components: 2 marks
- Five advantages (any 3 in depth): 3 marks
- UDR role explained: 2 marks

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Compare Service Endpoints and Private Endpoints in Azure VNet configuration. Mention the security implications of each.*

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | Service Endpoint | Private Endpoint |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Mechanism** | Extends VNet identity to a PaaS service over the public backbone | Injects a NIC with a private IP from your VNet into the PaaS service |
| **DNS Resolution** | Public DNS name resolves to **public IP** of PaaS service | Public DNS name resolves to **private IP** from your VNet (via Private DNS Zone) |
| **Public Access** | PaaS service still has public endpoint; VNet traffic is *optimized* but not *private* | Public endpoint can be **disabled** — true private connectivity |
| **Data Exfiltration Risk** | Higher — public IP is still reachable; NSG must lock down | Near zero — no public IP exposure if disabled |
| **IP Address Plan** | No new IP consumption in VNet | Consumes one IP from VNet per private endpoint |
| **Performance** | Optimized routing via Azure backbone | Optimal — traffic stays entirely on Azure backbone, never leaves |
| **Supported Services** | Limited set (Storage, SQL, CosmosDB, Key Vault, Service Bus, etc.) | All Azure PaaS services (broader coverage) |
| **Cost** | **Free** | Charged per hour + per GB data processed |
| **Configuration Complexity** | Simple — toggle on the subnet | Moderate — requires Private DNS Zone integration |

**Security Implications — 3 marks:**

1. **Service Endpoints** are *not* a true private connection. The PaaS service retains a public IP, and if a misconfigured NSG or firewall rule allows `0.0.0.0/0` on the service, the data is still accessible from the internet. They reduce *attack surface* but do not eliminate it.
2. **Private Endpoints** are the **Zero-Trust gold standard**. By disabling the public endpoint, the service is reachable *only* from the VNet (or peered VNets, or on-premises via Private Link). This eliminates data exfiltration vectors and is mandated in many regulatory frameworks (PCI-DSS, HIPAA).
3. **DNS poisoning risk**: Private Endpoints require Private DNS Zones; misconfiguration can cause name resolution to fall back to public IPs, accidentally re-exposing the service.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Comparison table covering 6+ parameters: 4 marks
- Security implications: 3 marks

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- ✅ **VNet Definition**: A logically isolated, software-defined Layer 3 network on shared physical infrastructure.
- ✅ **VNet ≠ VLAN**: VNets are L3 software constructs (VXLAN-encapsulated); VLANs are L2 hardware-bound partitions.
- ✅ **Address Space Limits**: Azure VNets range from **/8 to /29**; a single VNet can hold up to **3,000 subnets**.
- ✅ **Reserved Addresses**: Every Azure subnet reserves **5 addresses** (network, gateway, 2 DNS, last), so $H_{Azure} = 2^{n} - 5$.
- ✅ **SDN Layers**: Northbound API → SDN Controller → Southbound Interface (OpenFlow/OVSDB) → vSwitches.
- ✅ **NSG Rule Evaluation**: Lower priority number = higher precedence; default rules have priority 65000+; rules are **stateful**.
- ✅ **VNet Peering**: **Non-transitive**, up to **100 Gbps** intra-region, uses provider's private backbone.
- ✅ **UDRs**: Override default routes; commonly used to direct traffic through NVAs in hub-spoke topologies.
- ✅ **Service Endpoint vs Private Endpoint**: Service Endpoints = optimized public routing (free); Private Endpoints = true private IPs (paid, Zero-Trust ready).
- ✅ **GatewaySubnet**: Must be named exactly `GatewaySubnet`; minimum size **/27**; reserved for VPN/ExpressRoute gateways.
- ✅ **Validation Rule**: Always verify $E_i + 1 = S_{i+1}$ for non-overlapping subnet design — KTU's most-tested formula.
- ✅ **Wildcard Mask**: Inverse of subnet mask; used in NSG service tags and route table next-hop definitions.
- ✅ **Hub-Spoke**: Centralized security + cost optimization; UDRs convert non-transitive peering into controlled transit.
- ✅ **Formula Recap**: $S = 2^{n}$, $H_{classical} = 2^{n} - 2$, $H_{Azure} = 2^{n} - 5$, $P_{new} = N + \log_{2}(k)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of SDN and Implementation in Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Principles of SDN and Implementation in Cloud # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition: Software Defined Networking (SDN) **Software Defined Networking (SDN)** is a network architecture paradigm that decouples the network **control plane** (which decides where traffic is sent) from the **data/forwarding plane** (which actually forwards the traffic), and consolidates the control plane into a logically centralized software-based controller. The Open Networking Foundation (ONF) defines SDN as *"the physical separation of the network control plane from the forwarding plane, where a control plane controls several devices."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** SDN replaces the *vertically integrated* traditional network stack (where each switch/router has its own proprietary control software baked into hardware) with a *horizontally layered* model where intelligence is extracted into software. The three foundational pillars of SDN are: 1. **Decoupling of Control and Data Planes** — Separation of "what to do" from "how to do it." 2. **Logically Centralized Control** — A single software entity (controller) has a global view. 3. **Programmability of the Network** — Behavior is defined by software applications running on top of the controller via open APIs. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The GPS & Autonomous Car Model Imagine traditional networking as **driving a car using only the steering wheel and your eyes, with no GPS and no central traffic authority**. Each car decides independently, reactively, often causing jams, accidents, and inefficient detours. This mirrors legacy networks where each router runs its own distributed routing protocol (OSPF, BGP) with only local visibility. SDN, in contrast, is like an **autonomous-vehicle system with a central traffic command center (a "Google Maps + Air Traffic Control" hybrid)**: - **Sensors (Data Plane):** Cars report position/speed. In SDN, switches forward packets based on flow rules. - **Central Command Center (Control Plane):** The controller computes optimal routes globally. - **Apps (Application Layer):** Routing apps, load balancers, firewalls run as software modules. | Legacy Network | SDN-Enabled Network | |----------------|---------------------| | Each device decides | Central controller decides | | Distributed intelligence | Logically centralized intelligence | | Vendor-locked firmware | Vendor-neutral, programmable | | Manual CLI config | REST API / App-driven config | ## 1.3 Definition: SDN in the Cloud Context When SDN principles are deployed within a **Cloud Data Center**, the goal is to enable **elastic, multi-tenant, on-demand network provisioning**. In a cloud, tenants may spawn hundreds of Virtual Machines (VMs) per second. Manually configuring VLANs and ACLs for each tenant is impossible. SDN provides the *automation fabric* that lets cloud orchestration platforms (OpenStack, Kubernetes, VMware vSphere) treat the network as a programmable resource — equivalent to compute or storage. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics in SDN:** > - **Flow Table Size:** typically **~2,000 to 200,000 entries** (TCAM-based) per OpenFlow switch. > - **Control Channel Latency:** OpenFlow messages traverse the **TCP port 6653** (default since 2014) with typical RTTs in the order of **1–10 ms** in DC environments. > - **Setup Rate:** Modern controllers sustain **~10,000+ flow setups/sec** in production deployments. ## 1.4 Why SDN Matters for Cloud Computing — The Core Drivers > [!IMPORTANT] > 1. **Multi-tenancy:** Each cloud tenant must have isolated, overlapping IP address spaces. SDN enables this via **overlay networks** (VXLAN, GRE). > 2. **Elasticity:** Resources scale up/down dynamically; the network must follow suit within **seconds**, not days. > 3. **Service Chaining:** Traffic must traverse a sequence of virtual functions (Firewall → Load Balancer → IDS) — defined in software. > 4. **Vendor Neutrality:** Avoid lock-in by using open standards (OpenFlow, NETCONF, OVSDB). > 5. **Operational Cost Reduction:** Gartner reports up to **~60% reduction in CapEx** for new services when SDN is adopted in DCs (qualitative industry figure). --- ## 1.5 Network Function Virtualization (NFV) — The Companion Paradigm **NFV** virtualizes entire network *functions* (routers, firewalls, DNS) so they run as software on commodity x86 servers. While **SDN** separates control from forwarding, **NFV** decouples network functions from proprietary hardware. Both paradigms are complementary and frequently co-deployed in cloud environments (often abbreviated together as **SDN/NFV**). $$ \text{Traditional} = \text{Dedicated Hardware} + \text{Proprietary Software} $$ $$ \text{SDN/NFV} = \text{Commodity Hardware} + \text{Open Software (Controller + VNFs)} $$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Layer SDN Architecture The SDN architecture is canonically described as a **three-layer model** plus two interface planes (APIs). ### Layer 1 — Application Layer (Top) Contains the **business logic and network services** that express desired network behavior. These are software programs that consume the controller's Northbound API. **Examples:** - Load balancers (e.g., distributed L4/L7 balancers) - Routing applications (e.g., BGP speaker, segment routing app) - Security applications (firewall, DDoS mitigation) - Monitoring/analytics apps (telemetry, traffic matrix computation) ### Layer 2 — Control Layer (Middle) The **SDN Controller** — the "network operating system" (NOS). It: 1. Maintains a global, abstract view of the network topology (graph database). 2. Translates app requirements into flow rules. 3. Communicates with switches using the **Southbound API**. > [!NOTE] > Popular open-source controllers include **NOX/POX** (research), **Floodlight** (Java), **OpenDaylight (ODL)** (Java/OSGi), **ONOS** (Java, carrier-grade), and **Ryu** (Python). Cloud deployments commonly use **OVS Controller** integrated with **OpenStack Neutron**. ### Layer 3 — Infrastructure Layer (Bottom) The **physical and virtual switches/routers** that actually forward packets. Each device contains **flow tables** populated by the controller. The most common implementation is the **Open vSwitch (OVS)** in cloud data centers. ### The Two Interface Planes - **Northbound API (App ↔ Controller):** Typically **REST (HTTP/JSON)** or **gRPC**. Used by applications to query topology, install flows, read statistics. - **Southbound API (Controller ↔ Switch):** Dominated by the **OpenFlow protocol** (versions 1.0 through 1.5), with alternatives like **NETCONF/YANG**, **OVSDB**, and **P4Runtime**. ## 2.2 The OpenFlow Protocol — Anatomy of a Flow Entry OpenFlow is the canonical southbound protocol. Each switch maintains **one or more flow tables**, and each flow table contains **flow entries** structured as follows: | Field Category | Specific Fields | Purpose | |----------------|-----------------|---------| | **Match Fields** | Ingress Port, Ethernet src/dst MAC, VLAN ID, VLAN Priority, MPLS label, IPv4 src/dst, L4 src/dst port, IP Protocol | Define which packets this rule applies to | | **Priority** | 16-bit integer | Tie-breaking when multiple entries match | | **Counters** | Packet count, Byte count, Duration (sec/nsec) | Telemetry | | **Instructions** | Apply actions, Go-to-Table, Write-Metadata | What to do with the packet | | **Timeouts** | idle_timeout, hard_timeout | Flow lifetime | | **Cookie** | 64-bit opaque value | Controller-side identification | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Action Set (typical actions):** `OUTPUT, DROP, SET_VLAN_VID, SET_VLAN_PCP, SET_DL_SRC, SET_DL_DST, SET_NW_SRC, SET_NW_DST, SET_NW_TOS, SET_TP_SRC, SET_TP_DST, GROUP, PUSH_VLAN, POP_VLAN, METER`. ## 2.3 Packet Processing Pipeline When a packet arrives at an OpenFlow switch: 1. The packet's headers are parsed and matched against the **first flow table** starting from entry 0. 2. On a **table-miss** (no match), the switch either **drops**, **sends to controller** (`action = CONTROLLER, OFPP_CONTROLLER`), or **continues** to the next table. 3. On a match, the instruction set is executed — actions are applied and counters incremented. 4. If the instruction is `GOTO_TABLE`, the pipeline proceeds to the next table. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Quantity | Definition / Formula | Typical Unit | Notes | |-------------------|---------------------|--------------|-------| | $N_f$ | Number of flow entries in a switch TCAM | entries | Limits per-switch scalability | | $T_{setup}$ | Flow setup latency (per-flow) | ms | $T_{setup} = T_{prop} + T_{proc}^{ctrl} + T_{install}$ | | $\lambda_{req}$ | Arrival rate of new-flow requests | flows/sec | Determines controller load | | $\rho$ | Controller CPU utilization (dimensionless) | — | $\rho = \lambda_{req} \cdot \bar{s}$, where $\bar{s}$ is avg. service time (Little's Law) | | $L_q$ | Avg. queue length of pending requests (M/M/1) | requests | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | | $W_q$ | Avg. waiting time in queue | seconds | $W_q = \dfrac{L_q}{\lambda_{req}} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$ | | $T_{overhead}$ | Per-packet processing overhead in SDN switch | µs | Higher than L2-only due to TCAM lookups | | $B_{south}$ | Southbound bandwidth | Mbps | Determines scalability of central controller | | $R_{packets}$ | Packet-in rate to controller | pkt/sec | High values indicate reactive control; should be minimized | > [!IMPORTANT] > The controller must satisfy the **stability condition** for a stable M/M/1 queue: $\rho < 1$, i.e., $\lambda_{req} < \mu$ (arrival rate strictly less than service rate). Otherwise the controller queue grows unbounded and flows experience exponential delay. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Google's B4 (2013):** Google's private WAN connecting data centers. SDN-based traffic engineering achieved **near 70% link utilization** (vs. typical 30–40% in MPLS networks) by centrally computing TE paths. - **Microsoft Azure:** Uses **SONiC (Software for Open Networking in the Cloud)** — an open-source NOS running on white-box switches, all orchestrated by Azure's SDN fabric. - **OpenStack Neutron:** The de-facto cloud networking module that exposes REST APIs to provision tenant networks; under the hood uses ML2 plugin + OVS + an SDN controller (OVS, ODL, or ONOS). ## 2.6 SDN Deployment Models in Cloud | Model | Description | Cloud Use Case | |-------|-------------|----------------| | **Centralized (single controller)** | One controller manages all switches | Small DCs, lab environments | | **Hierarchical (multi-controller)** | Master-slave controller cluster | Mid-scale DCs | | **Distributed (flat cluster)** | Multiple controllers share state (e.g., ONOS, ODL) | Large-scale production DCs | | **Hybrid** | Mix of SDN and traditional routing | Carrier networks, transition phase | ## 2.7 Network Virtualization & Overlays in Cloud > [!NOTE] > **Overlay Networks** are the *workhorse* of cloud SDN. They tunnel tenant traffic over a shared underlay. Key technologies: - **VXLAN (Virtual Extensible LAN) — RFC 7348:** Encapsulates L2 frames in UDP packets on port **4789**. Provides a **24-bit VNI** (VXLAN Network Identifier), enabling up to $\mathbf{2^{24} = 16,777,216}$ logical networks — vastly more than the **4094** limit of VLANs. - **NVGRE (Network Virtualization using GRE) — RFC 7637:** Uses GRE with 24-bit VSID. - **GENEVE (Generic Network Virtualization Encapsulation) — RFC 8926:** Generic, extensible encapsulation; the modern preferred choice. - **STT (Stateless Transport Tunneling):** TCP-like but stateless, used by Nicira/NVP. ## 2.8 OpenStack Neutron — The Reference Cloud SDN Implementation **Neutron** is OpenStack's networking-as-a-service component. Its modular architecture uses: - **ML2 (Modular Layer 2) Plugin:** Core plugin decoupling type drivers (VLAN, VXLAN, GRE) from mechanism drivers (OVS, LinuxBridge, OVN). - **OVN (Open Virtual Network):** A lightweight SDN controller built by the OVS community; natively understands logical switches, routers, ACLs. - **Service Plugins:** L3 router, Load Balancer (Octavia), VPN, Firewall-as-a-Service. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Controller Queue Stability (M/M/1 Model) The SDN controller is often modeled as an **M/M/1 queue** to evaluate its capacity to handle flow-setup requests from data-plane switches. **Given:** - $\lambda$ = arrival rate of new-flow / packet-in requests (requests/sec) - $\mu$ = service rate of the controller (requests/sec) - $\rho$ = traffic intensity (utilization) **Step 1 — Define utilization.** The fraction of time the controller is busy serving requests. $$ \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Little's Law for queue length.** For an M/M/1 queue, the average number of requests in the system is: $$ L = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} $$ **Step 3 — Derive the average number in the queue only.** The number in service is $\rho$ (one in service with probability $\rho$, else zero). Therefore: $$ L_q = L - \rho = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho} - \rho = \frac{\rho - \rho(1-\rho)}{1-\rho} = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} $$ **Step 4 — Derive average waiting time using Little's Law.** By Little's Law, $L_q = \lambda \cdot W_q$, hence: $$ W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho^2 / (1-\rho)}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)} $$ **Step 5 — Derive total time in system (waiting + service).** $$ W = W_q + \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} $$ **Step 6 — Stability condition.** For $W$ to remain finite, the denominator must be positive: $$ \mu - \lambda > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda < \mu \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \rho < 1 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Numerical Example:** If a controller can process $\mu = 50{,}000$ flow setups/sec and the data plane generates $\lambda = 40{,}000$ flow setups/sec, then: > - $\rho = 0.8$ > - $L_q = 0.8^2 / 0.2 = 3.2$ queued requests > - $W = 1 / (50000 - 40000) = 0.0001$ s = $100\ \mu s$ per request. ## 3.2 Derivation: Overlay Tunnel Encapsulation Overhead When VXLAN is used, every original L2 frame is encapsulated, adding overhead. Let: - $S_{orig}$ = original payload size (bytes) - $O_{encap}$ = encapsulation overhead added **Step 1 — Enumerate VXLAN header overhead (per RFC 7348):** - Outer Ethernet header: 14 bytes - Outer IP header: 20 bytes - Outer UDP header: 8 bytes - VXLAN header: 8 bytes - Inner Ethernet header (already in original frame, but the *original frame* is preserved as payload, so we add 0 for inner): 0 additional **Step 2 — Total overhead:** $$ O_{VXLAN} = 14 + 20 + 8 + 8 = 50 \text{ bytes} $$ > [!NOTE] > Some sources cite 50 bytes; if the outer Ethernet also carries an 802.1Q tag, add 4 bytes → **54 bytes**. **Step 3 — Effective MTU consideration.** To avoid fragmentation, the physical MTU must be at least: $$ MTU_{min} = MTU_{orig} + O_{encap} = 1500 + 50 = 1550 \text{ bytes (typical jumbo frame is 9000)} $$ **Step 4 — Bandwidth efficiency ratio.** $$ \eta = \frac{S_{orig}}{S_{orig} + O_{VXLAN}} $$ **Step 5 — Evaluate for a 1500-byte original frame.** $$ \eta = \frac{1500}{1500 + 50} = \frac{1500}{1550} \approx 0.9677 = 96.77\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For small packets (e.g., 64-byte ACKs), efficiency drops significantly: $\eta = 64/114 \approx 56.1\%$. This is why cloud fabrics use **jumbo frames (MTU 9000)** to maintain efficiency. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Traditional vs. SDN Cloud Networking | Parameter | Traditional Cloud Network | SDN-Enabled Cloud Network | KTU Exam Insight | |-----------|--------------------------|---------------------------|------------------| | **Control Plane** | Distributed (per-device) | Logically centralized (controller) | Decoupling is the central concept | | **Provisioning Time** | Hours to days (manual CLI) | Seconds (API call) | Often asked as a "compare" question | | **Tenant Isolation** | VLAN + VRF (limited to 4094) | VXLAN (16M+ logical nets) | Overlay networks enable scale | | **Policy Enforcement** | Per-device ACL config | Centralized policy app | Single point of truth | | **Failure Detection** | Polling / SNMP traps | Real-time telemetry streams | Better observability | | **Vendor Lock-in** | High (proprietary NOS) | Low (open protocols) | Often a 7-mark question | | **CapEx/OpEx** | High (specialized HW) | Lower (commodity HW) | Frequently tested | | **Innovation Cycle** | Hardware refresh (3–5 yr) | Software release (weeks) | SDN enables agility | | **Network as Code** | Not natively supported | First-class (Ansible, Terraform) | Cloud-native question | | **Multi-tenancy Scalability** | Limited | Effectively unlimited via overlay | Cloud-grade question | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: How a Flow is Established in an SDN Cloud (OpenFlow + VXLAN Example) **Scenario:** Tenant A in OpenStack launches a VM; VM sends first packet to a VM in another host. 1. **VM emits ARP request** for destination IP. Packet hits the local **OVS bridge** (`br-int`). 2. **Table-miss in OVS flow table.** OVS encapsulates the packet in an `OFPT_PACKET_IN` message (with default action `CONTROLLER`) and sends it to the **Neutron server / OVN controller** over the OpenFlow channel (TCP 6653). 3. **Controller consults logical topology.** OVN's northbound DB has a `Logical_Switch_Port` entry for the destination VM with its assigned tunnel IP. 4. **Controller decides path.** It selects a **tunnel endpoint (VTEP)** on the destination compute host. 5. **Controller installs flow entries** via `OFPT_FLOW_MOD`: - Match: inner MAC of destination VM. - Action: encapsulate with **VXLAN header** (UDP dst port 4789, VNI = 1001), set outer IP = VTEP of dest host, output on `br-tun`. 6. **Packet is forwarded** through the underlay fabric (spine/leaf) to the destination VTEP. 7. **At destination, OVS decapsulates** and delivers to the target VM. 8. **Subsequent packets** match the installed flow and bypass the controller (reactive path cached in OVS TCAM). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three-Layer SDN Architecture with Interfaces ```mermaid flowchart TB app1["APPLICATION LAYER
Load Balancer App"] app2["APPLICATION LAYER
Routing App"] app3["APPLICATION LAYER
Firewall App"] app4["APPLICATION LAYER
Telemetry App"] nbapi["NORTHBOUND API
REST or gRPC"] ctrlCore["CONTROL LAYER
SDN CONTROLLER"] topoDB["Topology Database"] flowMgr["Flow Manager"] statEngine["State Engine"] sbapi["SOUTHBOUND API
OpenFlow / NETCONF / OVSDB"] sw1["DATA PLANE LAYER
Switch 1"] sw2["DATA PLANE LAYER
Switch 2"] sw3["DATA PLANE LAYER
Switch 3"] sw4["DATA PLANE LAYER
Switch 4"] app1 --> nbapi app2 --> nbapi app3 --> nbapi app4 --> nbapi nbapi --> ctrlCore ctrlCore --> topoDB ctrlCore --> flowMgr ctrlCore --> statEngine ctrlCore --> sbapi sbapi --> sw1 sbapi --> sw2 sbapi --> sw3 sbapi --> sw4 ``` ## 4.2 OpenFlow Packet Processing Pipeline (Single Switch View) ```mermaid flowchart LR pktIn["Incoming Packet"] --> parse["Parse Headers"] parse --> t0["Table 0 Lookup"] t0 -- "Match Found" --> action["Apply Action Set"] t0 -- "No Match" --> tmiss{"Table-Miss?"} tmiss -- "Drop" --> drop["Drop Packet"] tmiss -- "Send to Controller" --> ctrl["PACKET_IN to Controller"] tmiss -- "Goto Next Table" --> t1["Table 1 Lookup"] t1 -- "Match Found" --> action t1 -- "No Match" --> tmiss action --> counters["Update Counters"] counters --> egress["Egress Port"] ctrl -.->|"Controller installs flow"| t0 ``` ## 4.3 SDN in OpenStack Cloud — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB user["Cloud User / Tenant"] horizon["Horizon Dashboard / CLI"] neutron["Neutron Server
REST API :9696"] ml2["ML2 Plugin Core"] typeDrv["Type Drivers
VLAN, VXLAN, GRE"] mechDrv["Mechanism Drivers
OVS, OVN, LinuxBridge"] ovnCtrl["OVN Northbound DB"] ovnSouth["OVN Southbound DB"] ovs1["OVS br-int on Host A"] ovs2["OVS br-int on Host B"] vtepA["VTEP on Host A"] vtepB["VTEP on Host B"] vmA["VM A Tenant 1"] vmB["VM B Tenant 1"] vtepCloud["Underlay Network
Spine-Leaf Fabric"] user --> horizon horizon --> neutron neutron --> ml2 ml2 --> typeDrv ml2 --> mechDrv mechDrv --> ovnCtrl ovnCtrl --> ovnSouth ovnSouth --> ovs1 ovnSouth --> ovs2 ovs1 --> vtepA ovs2 --> vtepB vmA --> ovs1 vmB --> ovs2 vtepA --> vtepCloud vtepCloud --> vtepB ``` ## 4.4 Controller Cluster (Distributed SDN — High Availability) ```mermaid flowchart LR sw1["Switch 1"] --> c1["Controller Node 1"] sw2["Switch 2"] --> c1 sw3["Switch 3"] --> c2["Controller Node 2"] sw4["Switch 4"] --> c3["Controller Node 3"] c1 <-. "Raft Consensus" .-> c2 c2 <-. "Raft Consensus" .-> c3 c1 <-. "Raft Consensus" .-> c3 c1 --> raft["Shared State Store
Consistent Hashing"] c2 --> raft c3 --> raft ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Define Software Defined Networking. List any two advantages of SDN in a cloud data center.** **Model Answer:** **Software Defined Networking (SDN)** is a network architecture approach that decouples the **control plane** (decision-making) from the **data plane** (packet forwarding), consolidating the control plane into a **logically centralized, software-based controller** that programs forwarding devices via open APIs such as OpenFlow. **Two advantages in a cloud data center:** 1. **Rapid, automated provisioning:** Tenant networks, ACLs, and load balancers can be instantiated in seconds via API calls, matching the elasticity requirement of cloud workloads. 2. **Vendor neutrality & multi-tenancy at scale:** Through overlay technologies like VXLAN, SDN supports up to **16 million** logical networks — far beyond VLAN's 4094 limit — enabling massive multi-tenant cloud environments without vendor lock-in. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **What is the role of the Northbound API and the Southbound API in an SDN architecture?** **Model Answer:** - **Northbound API** is the interface between the **application layer** and the **SDN controller**. It exposes the controller's abstracted view of the network (topology, statistics, flow installation) to applications, typically using **REST (HTTP/JSON)** or **gRPC**. It allows apps to express intent — e.g., "block traffic from subnet X to subnet Y." - **Southbound API** is the interface between the **SDN controller** and the **data plane switches**. The dominant implementation is the **OpenFlow protocol** (TCP port 6653), with alternatives such as **NETCONF/YANG** and **OVSDB**. It carries `PACKET_IN`, `FLOW_MOD`, and `STATS_REQUEST` messages between controller and switches. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks (Module 3, Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-layer SDN architecture** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each layer and the role of the two APIs. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain how **OpenFlow** processes a packet arriving at an SDN switch when no matching flow entry exists in the flow table. #### Model Solution — (a) The SDN architecture is structured into **three vertical layers** and **two interface planes**: **1. Application Layer (Top):** This layer contains end-user **network applications** that communicate desired network behavior. Examples include routing apps, load balancers, security firewalls, and analytics tools. These apps do not interact directly with switches; they communicate through the controller using the **Northbound API**. **2. Control Layer (Middle):** This layer contains the **SDN Controller** (also called the *Network Operating System*). It maintains: - A **global topology view** (graph database of all switches, links, hosts). - A **flow management module** that computes and installs flow rules. - A **state synchronization engine** for cluster operations. The controller abstracts low-level device details and presents a uniform interface to applications above it. Examples: **ONOS, OpenDaylight, Floodlight, OVN, Ryu**. **3. Infrastructure / Data Plane Layer (Bottom):** This layer contains the **physical and virtual switches** that perform the actual packet forwarding. Each switch has one or more **flow tables** populated by the controller. Each entry specifies a match rule, action set, counters, and timeouts. Common implementation: **Open vSwitch (OVS)** in virtualized environments, **white-box switches** with SONiC in production DCs. **Interface Planes:** - **Northbound API** (App ↔ Controller): REST/gRPC. - **Southbound API** (Controller ↔ Switch): OpenFlow, NETCONF, OVSDB. #### Model Solution — (b) When a packet arrives at an OpenFlow switch and **no flow entry matches** in the current table (i.e., a *table-miss* occurs), the switch executes the **table-miss action** defined per table. The most common table-miss action is `OFPT_ACTION_OUTPUT: CONTROLLER`. **Step-by-step processing:** 1. **Packet arrival:** Switch receives the packet on an ingress port. The pipeline parser extracts header fields (L2, L3, L4). 2. **Table lookup:** The switch searches the flow table starting from entry 0 in priority order. No entry matches the packet's headers. 3. **Table-miss action:** The configured table-miss action triggers — most often `OUTPUT` to the controller's TCP port 6653. The switch **encapsulates the entire packet** (or a configurable portion) into an `OFPT_PACKET_IN` message. 4. **Control channel transmission:** The `PACKET_IN` message is sent over the secure channel to the controller, which carries the **buffer ID**, **ingress port**, and the **packet (or its reason field)**. 5. **Controller decision:** The controller applies its application logic — checks ACLs, consults routing, queries MAC/IP tables — and computes a flow rule. For example, *"match destination IP 10.0.0.5, action: output to port 3"*. 6. **Flow installation:** The controller sends an `OFPT_FLOW_MOD` message back to the switch with the new flow entry, specifying match fields, action set, priority, and timeouts. 7. **Subsequent packets:** With the flow now installed, all subsequent matching packets are forwarded at line-rate directly by the switch hardware/TCAM, **bypassing the controller** (fast path). 8. **Counters updated:** Each packet that hits the flow increments the entry's packet and byte counters, which the controller can poll via `OFPT_STATS_REQUEST` for telemetry. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Students often forget to mention that the controller can either **buffer** the packet (`OFPP_CONTROLLER` with reason `OFPR_NO_MATCH`) or **drop** it. The default in many implementations is buffering. > 2. Many answers fail to highlight the **performance implication**: the first packet incurs the *slow path* (controller round trip ≈ 1–10 ms), while subsequent packets are *fast path* (≈ µs). Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. Students frequently omit the **counter update** step. The controller uses these for SLA monitoring and link-utilization analysis. > 4. In diagram-based parts, students often draw a **single table** instead of showing the **multi-table pipeline** (T0 → T1 → ... → Tn) — a 1-mark penalty is common. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Module 3, Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of SDN in cloud computing**. Explain how **OpenStack Neutron** leverages SDN principles to provide networking-as-a-service to cloud tenants. **(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to **overlay networks**, explain **VXLAN**. State the VXLAN header overhead and derive the bandwidth efficiency for a 1500-byte Ethernet frame. #### Model Solution — (a) **Role of SDN in Cloud Computing:** Cloud computing demands that network resources be provisioned with the **same agility** as compute and storage. SDN provides this by transforming the network from a *static, manually-configured infrastructure* into a **programmable, automated fabric**. The specific roles are: 1. **Multi-tenancy:** Cloud providers host thousands of tenants with overlapping private IP ranges. SDN enables this through **logical network isolation** via overlays. 2. **Elasticity:** SDN allows networks to scale with workloads — adding/removing tenant subnets in seconds. 3. **Service chaining:** Traffic can be steered through a sequence of virtual network functions (firewall → IDS → load balancer) using SDN flow rules. 4. **Self-service portals:** Tenants can define their own topologies via APIs, which are realized by the SDN controller. 5. **Cost efficiency:** Commodity white-box hardware + open-source controllers reduce CapEx/OpEx. **OpenStack Neutron and SDN:** **Neutron** is the networking component of OpenStack, providing a **REST API (port 9696)** for tenants to create networks, subnets, routers, and load balancers. Its internal architecture reflects SDN principles: - **ML2 (Modular Layer 2) Plugin:** Decouples *what kind* of network (type driver: VLAN/VXLAN/GRE/flat) from *how* it is implemented (mechanism driver: OVS/OVN/LinuxBridge/SDN controller). - **OVN (Open Virtual Network):** The modern SDN controller for Neutron. It maintains: - *Northbound DB* — logical topology as specified by tenants. - *Southbound DB* — physical topology learned from each compute host's `ovs-vswitchd`. - *OVN Controller* — runs on each hypervisor, translating logical flows into OVS flow entries. - **Service plugins:** L3 router, Load Balancer (Octavia), VPNaaS, FWaaS, all of which are exposed as APIs and orchestrated by Neutron. When a tenant launches a VM, Neutron instructs OVN, which in turn programs the OVS bridge on the compute host. Tenant packets are encapsulated in **VXLAN** and tunneled across the underlay fabric to the destination VTEP. This entire flow is **API-driven, automated, and centralized** — a textbook SDN implementation. #### Model Solution — (b) **VXLAN (Virtual Extensible LAN — RFC 7348):** VXLAN is a **Layer-2-over-Layer-3 overlay** encapsulation protocol designed to overcome the 4094-VLAN limitation in large cloud data centers. It works by wrapping the **entire original L2 frame** inside a UDP packet, which is then routed over a standard IP underlay network. **VXLAN header structure:** | Header Component | Size (bytes) | Description | |------------------|--------------|-------------| | Outer Ethernet | 14 | Src/Dst MAC of underlay routers | | Outer IP | 20 | Src = source VTEP, Dst = dest VTEP | | Outer UDP | 8 | Dst port = **4789** (IANA-assigned) | | VXLAN Header | 8 | Contains 24-bit VNI + flags | | **Total Overhead** | **50** | | The **24-bit VNI (VXLAN Network Identifier)** allows for $\mathbf{2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216}$ logical Layer-2 segments — sufficient for hyperscale multi-tenant clouds. **Bandwidth efficiency derivation:** For an original Ethernet frame of $S_{orig} = 1500$ bytes transmitted over VXLAN: $$ S_{total} = S_{orig} + O_{VXLAN} = 1500 + 50 = 1550 \text{ bytes} $$ $$ \eta = \frac{S_{orig}}{S_{total}} = \frac{1500}{1550} = 0.9677 \approx 96.77\% $$ For a small 64-byte frame: $$ \eta = \frac{64}{64 + 50} = \frac{64}{114} \approx 56.14\% $$ > [!NOTE] > The severe drop for small packets is the reason **jumbo frames (MTU 9000)** are universally enabled in cloud fabrics — they amortize the encapsulation overhead over a much larger payload, keeping $\eta$ above **98%** even for typical TCP MSS of 1460 bytes. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Students often **omit the outer UDP header (8 bytes)**, citing only 42 bytes overhead. Correct total = **50 bytes** (or 54 with 802.1Q). > 2. Many forget the **VTEP** concept — VXLAN Tunnel Endpoints are the entities that encapsulate/decapsulate. A diagram must show two VTEPs at both ends of the tunnel. > 3. In efficiency derivations, students frequently **forget to add 50 to the denominator**, computing $\eta = 1500/50$ (absurd) instead of the correct $1500/1550$. > 4. Some confuse VXLAN with **NVGRE or GENEVE**. Examiners expect clear distinctions: NVGRE uses GRE (no UDP), GENEVE is the modern, extensible replacement. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Principles of SDN and Implementation in Cloud** - **SDN = Decoupling + Centralization + Programmability.** Memorize this triad. - **Three layers:** Application → Control → Infrastructure. **Two APIs:** Northbound (REST/gRPC) + Southbound (OpenFlow / NETCONF / OVSDB). - **OpenFlow default port = 6653** (since 2014; was 6633 earlier). Memorize this — frequently asked. - **Flow table fields:** Match Fields, Priority, Counters, Instructions, Timeouts, Cookie. Each is a potential short-answer question. - **Table-miss action options:** Drop / Send to Controller / Goto Next Table. **The slow path vs. fast path distinction is examinable.** - **Controller stability requires** $\lambda < \mu$, i.e., $\rho < 1$ in the M/M/1 model. **$W = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$** — derivable, do not skip. - **VLAN limit = 4094; VXLAN VNI = 24-bit = 16,777,216 logical networks.** Always cite this in overlay questions. - **VXLAN overhead = 50 bytes** (14 Eth + 20 IP + 8 UDP + 8 VXLAN). **Efficiency for 1500 B frame ≈ 96.77%.** - **Cloud SDN controllers in production:** **OVN** (Open vSwitch-based, lightweight), **ONOS**, **OpenDaylight**, **Floodlight**, **Ryu**. - **OpenStack Neutron = Networking-as-a-Service.** Use **ML2 plugin** + **OVN/OVS** as the SDN fabric. Components: API server, ML2 plugin, type drivers, mechanism drivers, service plugins. - **NFV ≠ SDN:** NFV virtualizes **network functions** (e.g., virtual router); SDN virtualizes the **control plane**. They are **complementary**, not interchangeable. - **Industry exemplars:** Google B4 (70% link utilization), Microsoft Azure SONiC, Amazon VPC (overlay-based, internal implementation). - **Memorize acronyms:** **OVS** (Open vSwitch), **OVN** (Open Virtual Network), **VTEP** (VXLAN Tunnel Endpoint), **VNI** (VXLAN Network Identifier), **ML2** (Modular Layer 2), **NOS** (Network Operating System), **ONF** (Open Networking Foundation), **OF** (OpenFlow). - **Always draw a labelled block diagram** for architecture questions — partial diagrams lose 2–3 marks typically. - **Real-world numerical questions** may involve: flow table size, controller throughput (packets/sec), overlay efficiency. Practice the M/M/1 and efficiency derivations thoroughly. --- **End of Module 3 Notes — Principles of SDN and Implementation in Cloud**

topics.title: Network Function Virtualization (NFV) and Microservices: NFV Components and Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Function Virtualization (NFV) and Microservices: NFV Components and Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Network Function Virtualization (NFV) — Formal Definition

**Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** is a network architecture paradigm, standardized by the **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)** in **2012**, that decouples **network functions** (such as firewalling, load balancing, routing, DNS, NAT, and intrusion detection) from **proprietary dedicated hardware appliances** and implements them as **software-based Virtualized Network Functions (VNFs)** running on standardized Commercial-Off-The-Shelf (COTS) compute, storage, and networking infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT521 Module 3):**
> NFV is the foundational technology that enables Telecom Service Providers (TSPs) and cloud operators to deliver agile, elastic, and programmable network services without being locked into vendor-specific hardware. ETSI NFV Industry Specification Group (ISG) is the canonical reference body.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Triad of NFV:**
> 1. **Decoupling** — Software separated from hardware
> 2. **Virtualization** — Resources abstracted via hypervisors/containers
> 3. **Orchestration** — Automated lifecycle management of virtual functions

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Software-Defined Restaurant" 🍽️

Imagine a traditional restaurant kitchen:

- **Old Model (Before NFV):** Each dish (pizza, sushi, pasta) requires a **dedicated specialized appliance** — a brick pizza oven, a Japanese sushi counter, a pasta extrusion machine. The restaurant cannot repurpose a pizza oven to make sushi. This is how traditional telecom networks work: a **Cisco firewall box** can only firewall; a **F5 load balancer** can only balance.

- **NFV Model:** The restaurant installs **one universal programmable robotic kitchen** (a generic COTS server). The robot is given **software recipes** (VNFs) — give it the firewall recipe, and it firewalls; give it the load-balancer recipe, and it load-balances. The same hardware serves many functions.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of NFV as "Virtualizing the network operator's toolbox."** A single pool of commodity hardware can now host dozens of network functions that were once locked inside expensive, proprietary boxes.

---

## 1.3 Microservices — Formal Definition

**Microservices** (or *microservice architecture*) is a cloud-native architectural style that structures an application as a **collection of loosely coupled, independently deployable, fine-grained services**. Each microservice:
- Owns a **single business capability** (Single Responsibility Principle)
- Runs in its **own process** (often a container)
- Communicates via **lightweight protocols** (HTTP/REST, gRPC, message queues)
- Is **independently scalable** and **independently deployable**
- May be **polyglot** (written in different languages using different data stores)

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Departmental Supermarket" 🏬

Consider a traditional **monolithic bank application**: one giant executable handles accounts, loans, transactions, and notifications. Updating the loan module requires redeploying the *entire* application.

The **microservices** version is like a **supermarket divided into independent departments** — grocery, electronics, clothing. Each department:
- Has its **own staff, registers, and inventory system**
- Can be **renovated or expanded** without closing the whole store
- Communicates with other departments via **standardized protocols** (phone, internal mail)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Microservices Matter in NFV:** Modern VNFs are increasingly decomposed into **cloud-native microservices** (also called **CNFs — Cloud-native Network Functions**), replacing monolithic virtual machines. This is a direct evolution enabled by containers and Kubernetes.

---

## 1.5 Key Performance & Standard Metrics in NFV

| Metric | Standard Value/Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **VNF instantiation time** | < 90 seconds (ETSI target) | Service agility |
| **NFV availability** | **99.999%** (Five Nines) | Carrier-grade reliability |
| **Service function chain latency** | < 10 ms (per hop) | End-to-end QoS |
| **Resource utilization gain** | **30% – 70%** over dedicated appliances | CAPEX/OPEX reduction |
| **Container cold-start** | < 5 seconds (with Pod Sandboxing) | Microservice elasticity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ETSI NFV Reference Architectural Framework — Layered Analysis

The **ETSI NFV Reference Architecture** (ETSI GS NFV 002) defines a layered framework with **three primary domains** interconnected by reference points.

### Layer 1: Virtualized Network Function (VNF) — *The Software Plane*

- **Definition:** A software implementation of a network function (e.g., vRouter, vFirewall, vEPC, vCDN).
- **Element Manager (EM):** Manages the FCAPS (Fault, Configuration, Accounting, Performance, Security) of one or more VNFs.
- **VNF Component (VNFC):** An internal sub-unit of a VNF mapped to a single VM or container.

### Layer 2: NFV Infrastructure (NFVI) — *The Resource Plane*

NFVI pools and virtualizes **three categories of resources**:

| Resource Domain | Physical Element | Virtualization Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute** | COTS x86/ARM servers | Hypervisors (KVM, ESXi), Containers (Docker, runC) |
| **Storage** | SSDs, NAS, SAN | Logical Volume Manager, Ceph, vSAN |
| **Network** | ToR switches, NICs | vSwitch (OVS), SR-IOV, VLAN/VXLAN overlays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The NFVI is hardware-agnostic.** A VNF running on Intel x86 in datacenter A can migrate to AMD EPYC servers in datacenter B without code changes — a property called **workload portability**.

### Layer 3: Management and Orchestration (MANO) — *The Control Plane*

MANO is the "brain" of NFV and consists of **three functional blocks**:

#### (a) Virtualized Infrastructure Manager (VIM)
- Manages NFVI resources within one administrative domain.
- **Examples:** OpenStack (compute/storage), VMware vCenter, Kubernetes (containers).
- Functions: VM/container lifecycle, resource allocation, fault monitoring, inventory.

#### (b) VNF Manager (VNFM)
- Manages the **lifecycle of individual VNFs**: instantiation, scaling (in/out), healing, termination, updating.
- Maintains **VNF Descriptors (VNFD)** — declarative templates (TOSCA/YAML).
- **Examples:** ONAP VNFM, Tacker (OpenStack), F5 VNF Manager.

#### (c) NFV Orchestrator (NFVO)
- **Cross-domain orchestrator** — coordinates multi-VNF services.
- Manages **Network Service Descriptors (NSDs)**.
- Handles **Service Function Chaining (SFC)**: ordering of VNFs for a composite service.
- Performs **resource orchestration** across multiple VIMs.

### The Supporting Block: OSS/BSS
- **Operations Support System (OSS):** Fault, performance, configuration management.
- **Business Support System (BSS):** Billing, CRM, product catalog.
- Acts as the **interface between MANO and the service provider's business processes**.

---

## 2.2 Microservices Architecture — Deeper Analysis

### 2.2.1 Anatomy of a Microservice System

| Component | Role | Common Implementations |
|---|---|---|
| **API Gateway** | Single entry point; routing, auth, rate-limiting | Kong, NGINX, AWS API Gateway, Ambassador |
| **Service Registry & Discovery** | Tracks service instances dynamically | Consul, Eureka, etcd |
| **Service Mesh** | Sidecar proxies for inter-service communication | Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect |
| **Container Orchestrator** | Schedules and manages containers | Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, Nomad |
| **Message Broker** | Asynchronous communication | Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS |
| **Observability Stack** | Logs, metrics, traces | Prometheus + Grafana, ELK, Jaeger |

### 2.2.2 Communication Patterns

1. **Synchronous:** REST (HTTP/JSON), gRPC (HTTP/2 + Protobuf), GraphQL.
2. **Asynchronous:** Message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ), Event-driven (Pub/Sub).
3. **Service-to-Service:** Sidecar pattern via Service Mesh (e.g., Envoy in Istio).

### 2.2.3 Design Principles (The "12-Factor App" Heritage)

- **Codebase:** One repo per service.
- **Dependencies:** Explicitly declared, isolated.
- **Config:** Externalized (12-factor config).
- **Backing Services:** Treated as attached resources.
- **Build, Release, Run:** Strict separation.
- **Processes:** Stateless, share-nothing.
- **Port Binding:** Self-contained, export services via port.
- **Concurrency:** Scale out via process model.
- **Disposability:** Fast startup, graceful shutdown.
- **Dev/Prod Parity:** Same backing services in dev/staging/prod.
- **Logs:** Event streams, routed externally.
- **Admin Processes:** One-off tasks run as processes.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet — Quantitative NFV Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are high-yield for KTU numerical/analytical questions.

### 2.3.1 VNF Resource Footprint Model

The total compute demand of a VNF chain can be expressed as:

$$C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot \rho_i + C_{\text{overhead}}$$

where:
- $C_i$ = compute demand of VNF $i$ (in vCPUs)
- $\rho_i$ = resource utilization factor of VNF $i$ ($0 < \rho_i \le 1$)
- $C_{\text{overhead}}$ = overhead of hypervisor/container runtime (typically **5%–15%** of total allocated)
- $n$ = number of VNFs in the chain

### 2.3.2 Service Function Chain (SFC) Latency

$$L_{\text{SFC}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i + L_{\text{link}} \cdot (n - 1)$$

where:
- $L_i$ = processing latency of VNF $i$ (ms)
- $L_{\text{link}}$ = transmission latency between consecutive VNFs
- $(n - 1)$ = number of inter-VNF links

### 2.3.3 Cost Efficiency Ratio (CER)

$$\text{CER} = \frac{C_{\text{NFV}} + O_{\text{NFV}}}{C_{\text{legacy}} + O_{\text{legacy}}}$$

where $C$ = capital expenditure, $O$ = operational expenditure. A CER **< 1** indicates NFV is more cost-effective. Industry case studies (e.g., AT&T Domain 2.0) report **CER ≈ 0.4 – 0.6**.

### 2.3.4 Horizontal Scaling Factor

For a stateless microservice/VNF under load:

$$N_{\text{instances}} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda \cdot T_{\text{process}}}{\mu_{\text{instance}}} \right\rceil$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = request rate (req/s)
- $T_{\text{process}}$ = average processing time per request (s)
- $\mu_{\text{instance}}$ = capacity per instance (req/s)

### 2.3.5 Utilization-Based Auto-Scaling Rule

$$\text{Scale-out if: } U_{\text{CPU}} > U_{\text{threshold}} \text{ for } t > t_{\text{cooldown}}$$

$$\text{Scale-in if: } U_{\text{CPU}} < U_{\text{lower}} \text{ for } t > t_{\text{cooldown}}$$

where $U_{\text{threshold}}$ is typically **70%–80%** and $U_{\text{lower}}$ is **30%–40%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Boundary Conditions & Constants**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Standard / Source |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier-grade availability | **99.999%** (≈ 5.26 min/yr downtime) | ITU-T E.800 |
| Hypervisor overhead | **5% – 15%** of allocated CPU | Industry empirical |
| Container image size (CNF) | **50 MB – 500 MB** | Docker Hub averages |
| VNF instantiation SLA | **< 90 s** | ETSI NFV ISG |
| Kubernetes default namespace | `default` | K8s v1.x |
| Service Mesh sidecar memory | **~50 MB** per sidecar (Envoy default) | Istio documentation |
| K8s readiness probe initial delay | 0 – 30 s | K8s API |
| gRPC default port | **50051** | gRPC convention |
| REST/HTTP default port | **80 (HTTP) / 443 (HTTPS)** | IETF RFC 9110 |
| Kubernetes pod count per node (max) | **110** (default) | K8s docs |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of NFV + Microservices |
|---|---|
| **5G Core (5GC)** | All 5G core functions (AMF, SMF, UPF) are designed as **cloud-native microservices (CNFs)** in 3GPP Release 16+. |
| **SD-WAN** | Virtualized WAN edge replaces branch-office routers. |
| **vCPE** | Virtual Customer Premises Equipment — CPE functions delivered from operator cloud. |
| **Telco Edge (MEC)** | Microservice-based VNFs deployed at base station / aggregation sites. |
| **Enterprise Networking** | Service chaining of vFW → vLB → vWAF in private cloud. |
| **IoT Gateways** | Lightweight microservices for protocol translation, filtering. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — VNF Chain Resource Footprint

### Problem Statement

A telecom operator deploys a **Service Function Chain (SFC)** with the following VNFs:

| VNF | $C_i$ (vCPUs) | $\rho_i$ (Utilization) |
|---|---|---|
| vFirewall | 4 | 0.65 |
| vLoadBalancer | 6 | 0.80 |
| vNAT | 2 | 0.50 |
| vIDS | 8 | 0.75 |

The hypervisor overhead is **10%** of the total compute demand. Compute $C_{\text{total}}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the core equation.

We use the resource footprint model:

$$C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot \rho_i + C_{\text{overhead}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the weighted demand of each VNF.

For **vFirewall** (i = 1):

$$C_1 \cdot \rho_1 = 4 \times 0.65 = 2.60 \text{ vCPUs}$$

For **vLoadBalancer** (i = 2):

$$C_2 \cdot \rho_2 = 6 \times 0.80 = 4.80 \text{ vCPUs}$$

For **vNAT** (i = 3):

$$C_3 \cdot \rho_3 = 2 \times 0.50 = 1.00 \text{ vCPUs}$$

For **vIDS** (i = 4):

$$C_4 \cdot \rho_4 = 8 \times 0.75 = 6.00 \text{ vCPUs}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the weighted compute demands.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} C_i \cdot \rho_i = 2.60 + 4.80 + 1.00 + 6.00 = 14.40 \text{ vCPUs}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the hypervisor overhead.

$$C_{\text{overhead}} = 0.10 \times 14.40 = 1.44 \text{ vCPUs}$$

**Step 5:** Add overhead to weighted demand.

$$C_{\text{total}} = 14.40 + 1.44 = \boxed{15.84 \text{ vCPUs}}$$

### Interpretation
- The chain effectively requires **15.84 vCPUs** of physical compute.
- The **unutilized portion** (1 - 0.65) × 4 = 1.40 vCPUs for the vFirewall alone represents *idle compute* that can be reclaimed for **horizontal scaling** or **other tenants** — a key NFV advantage.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Horizontal Scaling of a Microservice

### Problem Statement

A stateless microservice `payment-svc` handles a request rate of $\lambda = 3000$ req/min. Each instance can process $\mu_{\text{instance}} = 150$ req/min with an average processing time $T_{\text{process}} = 0.25$ s. Determine the minimum number of instances required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Convert the request rate to consistent units.

$$\lambda = 3000 \text{ req/min} = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ req/s}$$

**Step 2:** State the scaling equation.

$$N_{\text{instances}} = \left\lceil \frac{\lambda \cdot T_{\text{process}}}{\mu_{\text{instance}}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the parameters.

$$N_{\text{instances}} = \left\lceil \frac{50 \text{ req/s} \times 0.25 \text{ s}}{150 \text{ req/min} / 60 \text{ s/min}} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4:** Normalize the per-instance capacity.

$$\mu_{\text{instance}} = \frac{150 \text{ req/min}}{60 \text{ s/min}} = 2.5 \text{ req/s}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the numerator.

$$\lambda \cdot T_{\text{process}} = 50 \times 0.25 = 12.5 \text{ req (in-flight)}$$

**Step 6:** Divide and apply the ceiling operator.

$$N_{\text{instances}} = \left\lceil \frac{12.5}{2.5} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.0 \rceil = \boxed{5 \text{ instances}}$$

### Interpretation
- **5 instances** is the absolute minimum. In production, an HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) would add a **safety margin** of 20–30%, deploying **6 – 7 pods** to absorb traffic bursts.
- This is precisely the **auto-scaling equation** K8s HPA computes internally using the formula:

$$\text{desiredReplicas} = \left\lceil \text{currentReplicas} \times \frac{\text{currentMetricValue}}{\text{targetMetricValue}} \right\rceil$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Service Function Chain Latency Budget

### Problem Statement

An SFC has **n = 4 VNFs**. The processing latencies and inter-VNF link latencies are tabulated below. Verify whether the chain meets a **50 ms end-to-end latency SLA**.

| VNF / Link | Latency (ms) |
|---|---|
| vFW ($L_1$) | 2.5 |
| vLB ($L_2$) | 3.0 |
| vNAT ($L_3$) | 1.5 |
| vIDS ($L_4$) | 8.0 |
| Link 1→2 ($L_{\text{link}}$) | 1.0 |
| Link 2→3 | 1.2 |
| Link 3→4 | 0.8 |

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply the SFC latency equation.

$$L_{\text{SFC}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i + L_{\text{link}} \cdot (n - 1)$$

**Step 2:** Sum the processing latencies.

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} L_i = 2.5 + 3.0 + 1.5 + 8.0 = 15.0 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the link latencies (3 inter-VNF links for n = 4).

$$L_{\text{link}} \cdot (n - 1) = (1.0 + 1.2 + 0.8) = 3.0 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4:** Total SFC latency.

$$L_{\text{SFC}} = 15.0 + 3.0 = \boxed{18.0 \text{ ms}}$$

**Step 5:** SLA compliance check.

$$L_{\text{SFC}} = 18.0 \text{ ms} \; \le \; 50 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark$$

### Interpretation
- The chain **comfortably meets** the 50 ms SLA with a margin of 32 ms.
- The **vIDS** is the dominant contributor (44% of total). If SLA pressure increases, the vIDS is the primary optimization target (e.g., offload to SmartNIC/DPU).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — VNF (VM-based) vs CNF (Container-based Microservice)

| Dimension | VNF (VM-based) | CNF (Container-based Microservice) |
|---|---|---|
| **Boot Time** | 30 – 90 s | **< 1 s** (warm); < 5 s (cold) |
| **Image Size** | 1 – 10 GB | **50 – 500 MB** |
| **Isolation** | Strong (hypervisor) | Weaker (Linux namespaces + cgroups) |
| **Resource Overhead** | 5% – 15% | **1% – 3%** |
| **Granularity** | Coarse (one VM = one VNF) | **Fine (many CNFs = one network function)** |
| **Orchestrator** | OpenStack + KVM | **Kubernetes + containerd** |
| **Scaling** | Slow, vertical | **Fast, horizontal** |
| **Update Strategy** | Maintenance window | **Rolling update, canary, blue-green** |
| **State** | Often stateful | **Mostly stateless, external state** |
| **Deployment** | Days to weeks | **Minutes** |
| **Use Case** | Legacy telecom workloads | **5G core, edge, cloud-native TSPs** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The telecom industry is transitioning from **VNF-on-VM** to **CNF-on-Kubernetes** as defined by **3GPP TS 23.558** and **ETSI NFV Release 4**. Expect exam questions contrasting these two paradigms.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ETSI NFV Reference Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[OSS and BSS - Business and Operations Layer]
    nodeB[NFV Orchestrator - NFVO - Cross Domain Service Orchestration]
    nodeC[VNF Manager - VNFM - VNF Lifecycle Management]
    nodeD[Virtualized Infrastructure Manager - VIM - Resource Management]

    nodeE[VNF 1 - Example vFirewall]
    nodeF[VNF 2 - Example vLoadBalancer]
    nodeG[VNF 3 - Example vRouter]
    nodeH[Element Manager - EM - FCAPS for VNFs]

    nodeI[Compute Resources - COTS x86 or ARM Servers]
    nodeJ[Storage Resources - SSD NAS SAN]
    nodeK[Network Resources - ToR Switches vSwitches OVS]

    nodeL[Virtualization Layer - Hypervisor KVM ESXi or Container Runtime]

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeE
    nodeC --- nodeF
    nodeC --- nodeG
    nodeH --- nodeE
    nodeH --- nodeF
    nodeH --- nodeG
    nodeD --- nodeL
    nodeL --- nodeI
    nodeL --- nodeJ
    nodeL --- nodeK
    nodeE --- nodeL
    nodeF --- nodeL
    nodeG --- nodeL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Top layer (Management):** OSS/BSS interfaces with NFVO for business-level orchestration.
- **Middle layer (MANO):** NFVO ↔ VNFM ↔ VIM form the orchestration triad.
- **Lower-middle layer (VNFs):** Software network functions managed by VNFM and EM.
- **Bottom layer (NFVI):** Compute + Storage + Network resources, virtualized by the hypervisor/container layer.

---

## 4.2 Service Function Chaining (SFC) — Packet Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeUser[Client Traffic]
    nodeIngress[SFF1 - Service Function Forwarder]
    nodeFW[VNF 1 vFirewall]
    nodeLB[VNF 2 vLoadBalancer]
    nodeNAT[VNF 3 vNAT]
    nodeIDS[VNF 4 vIDS]
    nodeEgress[SFF2 - Service Function Forwarder]
    nodeDest[Destination Service]

    nodeUser --> nodeIngress
    nodeIngress --> nodeFW
    nodeFW --> nodeLB
    nodeLB --> nodeNAT
    nodeNAT --> nodeIDS
    nodeIDS --> nodeEgress
    nodeEgress --> nodeDest
```

> [!NOTE]
> **SFF (Service Function Forwarder):** A network element responsible for forwarding traffic to the next VNF in the chain based on the **NSH (Network Service Header)** (IETF RFC 8300).

---

## 4.3 Microservices Architecture with Service Mesh

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeClient[External Client]
    nodeGW[API Gateway - Kong or NGINX]
    nodeDisc[Service Registry - Consul or Eureka]

    subgraph nodeMesh[Service Mesh - Istio Control Plane]
        nodeIstiod[Istiod - Configuration and Certificate Authority]
    end

    subgraph nodeSvcA[Service A Pod - auth-svc]
        nodeAppA[auth-svc Container]
        nodeSideA[Envoy Sidecar Proxy]
    end

    subgraph nodeSvcB[Service B Pod - payment-svc]
        nodeAppB[payment-svc Container]
        nodeSideB[Envoy Sidecar Proxy]
    end

    subgraph nodeSvcC[Service C Pod - notify-svc]
        nodeAppC[notify-svc Container]
        nodeSideC[Envoy Sidecar Proxy]
    end

    nodeMsg[Message Broker - Kafka]

    nodeClient --> nodeGW
    nodeGW --> nodeDisc
    nodeGW --> nodeSideA
    nodeSideA --- nodeAppA
    nodeSideB --- nodeAppB
    nodeSideC --- nodeAppC
    nodeSideA <--> nodeSideB
    nodeSideB <--> nodeSideC
    nodeAppB --> nodeMsg
    nodeMsg --> nodeAppC
    nodeIstiod -. configures .-> nodeSideA
    nodeIstiod -. configures .-> nodeSideB
    nodeIstiod -. configures .-> nodeSideC
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **API Gateway** is the single ingress.
- Each microservice pod has a **sidecar proxy (Envoy)** that intercepts all traffic — this is the **service mesh pattern**.
- **Istiod** pushes configuration (routes, retries, mTLS certificates) to all sidecars.
- **Kafka** decouples `payment-svc` and `notify-svc` asynchronously.

---

## 4.4 NFV MANO Stack — Function Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeBSS[Business Plane]
        nodeBSS1[OSS - Operations Support]
        nodeBSS2[BSS - Business Support]
    end

    subgraph nodeMANO[Management and Orchestration Plane MANO]
        nodeNFVO[NFVO - Network Service Orchestrator]
        nodeVNFM[VNFM - VNF Manager]
        nodeVIM[VIM - Virtualized Infrastructure Manager]
    end

    subgraph nodeApp[Application Plane]
        nodeVNF1[VNF 1]
        nodeVNF2[VNF 2]
        nodeVNF3[VNF 3]
        nodeEM[Element Manager]
    end

    subgraph nodeNFVI[Infrastructure Plane NFVI]
        nodeComp[Compute]
        nodeStor[Storage]
        nodeNet[Network]
        nodeHyper[Virtualization Layer]
    end

    nodeBSS1 --> nodeNFVO
    nodeBSS2 --> nodeNFVO
    nodeNFVO --> nodeVNFM
    nodeNFVO --> nodeVIM
    nodeVNFM --> nodeEM
    nodeEM --> nodeVNF1
    nodeEM --> nodeVNF2
    nodeEM --> nodeVNF3
    nodeVNFM --> nodeVNF1
    nodeVNFM --> nodeVNF2
    nodeVNFM --> nodeVNF3
    nodeVIM --> nodeHyper
    nodeHyper --> nodeComp
    nodeHyper --> nodeStor
    nodeHyper --> nodeNet
    nodeVNF1 --> nodeHyper
    nodeVNF2 --> nodeHyper
    nodeVNF3 --> nodeHyper
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked to "draw the NFV architecture," always show **all three planes** (Infrastructure, Application, Management) and label the **MANO triad** (NFVO, VNFM, VIM) explicitly. This is the most-credited diagram in KTU board exams for this module.

---

## 4.5 VNF vs CNF — Side-by-Side Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph nodeLegacy[Legacy VNF on VM Model]
        nodeL1[Single VM]
        nodeL2[Monolithic VNF Image]
        nodeL3[Hypervisor KVM]
        nodeL1 --- nodeL2
        nodeL2 --- nodeL3
    end

    subgraph nodeCloudNative[Cloud Native CNF on Kubernetes]
        nodeC1[Pod 1 Container A]
        nodeC2[Pod 2 Container B]
        nodeC3[Pod 3 Container C]
        nodeC4[Kubernetes Node]
        nodeC1 --- nodeC4
        nodeC2 --- nodeC4
        nodeC3 --- nodeC4
    end
```
