# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Network Function Virtualization (NFV). List any four benefits of NFV over traditional hardware-based network functions.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** is an architecture paradigm standardized by ETSI that decouples network functions from proprietary hardware appliances, implementing them as software-based Virtualized Network Functions (VNFs) running on standardized COTS compute, storage, and networking infrastructure.

**Four benefits:**
1. **Reduced CAPEX** — No need for vendor-locked dedicated hardware; commodity servers suffice.
2. **Reduced OPEX** — Centralized management; power, cooling, and space savings.
3. **Elastic Scalability** — VNFs can be scaled in/out via software commands in minutes.
4. **Faster Time-to-Market** — New services deployed via software without hardware procurement cycles.
5. **Vendor Independence** — Hardware decoupled from software; multi-vendor sourcing possible.
*(Any four well-explained = 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a microservice? Compare microservices with monolithic architecture in terms of deployment and scalability.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **microservice** is a small, loosely coupled, independently deployable service that owns a single business capability and communicates with other services via lightweight protocols (HTTP/REST, gRPC, or message queues).

| Aspect | Monolithic | Microservices |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment** | Entire application redeployed as a single unit | Each service independently deployable |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (scale whole app) | Horizontal (scale individual services) |
| **Failure Impact** | Single failure may crash entire app | Failure isolated to one service |
| **Tech Stack** | Single language/framework | Polyglot (multiple languages) |
| **Team Structure** | Single large team | Small, autonomous teams per service |

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for comparison table.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question offers an internal choice. The module chosen is **Module 3**. Solve either **Question A** or **Question B**. Each question has sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **ETSI NFV Reference Architecture** in detail, with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of **NFVO, VNFM, and VIM** in the MANO framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **components of NFV Infrastructure (NFVI)**. How does NFVI support workload portability across heterogeneous hardware? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

### (a) ETSI NFV Reference Architecture & MANO Framework

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **ETSI NFV Reference Architecture**, defined in **ETSI GS NFV 002**, organizes the NFV ecosystem into three functional domains: **VNF (Application)**, **NFVI (Infrastructure)**, and **MANO (Management and Orchestration)**, with **OSS/BSS** providing the service-provider business interface.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Draw the diagram from Section 4.1 with all labels — NFVO, VNFM, VIM, VNFs, NFVI resources, OSS/BSS. Neat, labelled, with reference points shown.)*

**MANO Roles (3 marks):**

| Block | Full Form | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **NFVO** | NFV Orchestrator | Orchestrates network services across multiple VIMs; handles SFC and resource orchestration; manages NSD. |
| **VNFM** | VNF Manager | Manages individual VNF lifecycle — instantiation, scaling, healing, termination, update. |
| **VIM** | Virtualized Infrastructure Manager | Manages NFVI resources (compute/storage/network) within one administrative domain; examples: OpenStack, vCenter, Kubernetes. |

**Conclusion:** Together, MANO delivers **end-to-end automation** of network service lifecycle.

---

### (b) NFVI Components and Workload Portability

**NFVI Components (4 marks):**

NFVI consists of **three pooled resource domains** unified by a **virtualization layer**:

1. **Compute Resources** — COTS x86/ARM servers providing raw processing. Virtualized via **hypervisors (KVM, ESXi)** or **container runtimes (containerd, runC)**.
2. **Storage Resources** — Block (SAN), file (NAS), and object storage; virtualized via software-defined storage (Ceph, vSAN).
3. **Network Resources** — Physical switches, NICs, SDN controllers; virtualized via **vSwitches (OVS, Linux Bridge)** and overlay technologies (VXLAN, Geneve).

The **virtualization layer** abstracts these resources, exposing them to VNFs as virtual compute, virtual storage, and virtual networks.

**Workload Portability (3 marks):**

Workload portability refers to the ability to migrate a VNF/CNF from one hardware platform to another **without code or configuration changes**. NFVI enables this through:

- **Hardware abstraction** — VNFs interact with virtual resources, not physical devices.
- **Standardized interfaces** — ETSI-defined reference points (Vi-Ha, Nf-Vi) decouple VNFs from hardware specifics.
- **Declarative descriptors** — TOSCA/YAML templates describe VNF requirements abstractly, allowing MANO to schedule them on any compliant NFVI.
- **Heterogeneous hardware support** — VNFs running on Intel can move to AMD, ARM, or GPU-equipped servers as long as the NFVI exposes equivalent virtual resources.

**Conclusion:** Portability eliminates **vendor lock-in** and enables **disaster recovery**, **geo-redundancy**, and **multi-cloud strategies**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** What are **microservices**? Explain the key components of a microservices architecture, including the roles of **API Gateway, Service Registry, and Service Mesh**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **VM-based VNFs** with **container-based CNFs (Cloud-native Network Functions)**. Discuss the role of **Kubernetes** in deploying 5G core network functions. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### (a) Microservices and Their Key Components

**Definition (1 mark):**
**Microservices** is a cloud-native architectural style that structures an application as a suite of small, independently deployable services, each owning a single business capability, communicating via lightweight protocols, and designed for failure.

**Key Components (6 marks):**

1. **API Gateway (2 marks):**
   - **Role:** Single entry point for all client requests; performs request routing, composition, authentication, rate-limiting, and protocol translation.
   - **Examples:** Kong, NGINX, AWS API Gateway, Ambassador.
   - **Why:** Clients should not need to know the internal topology of services; the gateway abstracts this.

2. **Service Registry & Discovery (2 marks):**
   - **Role:** Maintains a dynamic directory of service instances and their network locations.
   - **Mechanism:** Services register on startup, deregister on shutdown; clients query the registry to find healthy instances.
   - **Examples:** Consul, Eureka, etcd, Kubernetes DNS.

3. **Service Mesh (2 marks):**
   - **Role:** A dedicated infrastructure layer for handling service-to-service communication.
   - **Implementation:** Sidecar proxies (e.g., Envoy) deployed alongside each service instance.
   - **Functions:** Mutual TLS (mTLS), retries, circuit breaking, observability (metrics, traces), traffic shifting (canary, blue-green).
   - **Examples:** Istio, Linkerd, Consul Connect.

**Conclusion:** Together, these components enable **resilient, observable, and secure** microservice ecosystems.

---

### (b) VNF vs CNF and Kubernetes in 5G Core

**VNF vs CNF Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | VNF (VM-based) | CNF (Container/Kubernetes-based) |
|---|---|---|
| **Boot Time** | 30 – 90 s | < 1 s |
| **Image Size** | 1 – 10 GB | 50 – 500 MB |
| **Resource Overhead** | 5% – 15% | 1% – 3% |
| **Scaling** | Slow, vertical | Fast, horizontal |
| **Orchestrator** | OpenStack + KVM | Kubernetes + containerd |
| **Updates** | Maintenance windows | Rolling, canary |
| **Use Case Fit** | Legacy telecom | 5G core, edge, cloud-native |

**Kubernetes in 5G Core (3 marks):**
- **3GPP Release 16+** specifies the 5G core as a set of **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** network functions: **AMF, SMF, UPF, AUSF, NRF, PCF, UDM, NSSF**.
- Each of these is **deployed as a set of microservices (CNFs)** on **Kubernetes**.
- Kubernetes provides:
  - **Auto-scaling** via HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler) and VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler) for elastic traffic handling.
  - **Self-healing** via liveness/readiness probes — critical for carrier-grade availability (99.999%).
  - **Rolling updates** for zero-downtime upgrades.
  - **Multi-tenancy** via namespaces — useful for slice isolation in **network slicing**.
  - **Service mesh integration** (Istio) for inter-NF communication with mTLS.

**Conclusion:** The combination of **NFV + Microservices + Kubernetes** forms the technological foundation of **5G, edge computing, and cloud-native telecom networks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in NFV/Microservices Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing NFVO with VNFM** — NFVO handles **cross-domain service orchestration** (multi-VNF, multi-VIM); VNFM handles **single-VNF lifecycle**. Examiners *specifically* deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Omitting OSS/BSS** — In ETSI architecture diagrams, OSS/BSS is a *mandatory* top-layer block. Diagrams missing it lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "VNF = VM"** — A VNF is a **software function**; a VM is its *deployment substrate*. A VNF can run on VMs **or** containers. Use precise terminology.
> 4. **Microservice = Container** — Containers are *commonly used* to package microservices, but a microservice is an **architectural style**, not a technology. State this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **No reference points in diagrams** — Labelling **Vi-Ha, Nf-Vi, Or-Vi, Or-Vnfm** in the ETSI architecture fetches **bonus marks** in KTU board valuations.
> 6. **Forgetting scaling direction** — Always state **scale-out (horizontal)** and **scale-in** in auto-scaling questions, not just "scaling."
> 7. **Service Mesh confused with API Gateway** — API Gateway handles **north-south** (client-to-service) traffic; Service Mesh handles **east-west** (service-to-service) traffic. This distinction is frequently tested.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — NFV & Microservices: Last-Minute Revision Sheet**

- **NFV = Decoupling network functions from hardware** (ETSI, 2012).
- **Three NFV Domains:** VNF, NFVI, MANO. Plus **OSS/BSS** as the business layer.
- **MANO Triad:** **NFVO** (orchestrates services), **VNFM** (manages VNF lifecycle), **VIM** (manages NFVI resources).
- **NFVI = Compute + Storage + Network**, all virtualized via a hypervisor or container runtime.
- **VNFs** = software network functions (vFW, vLB, vNAT, vRouter, vEPC).
- **CNFs (Cloud-native Network Functions)** = VNFs decomposed into microservices running on **Kubernetes**.
- **Microservices = loosely coupled, independently deployable, single-responsibility services** communicating via **HTTP/REST, gRPC, or message queues**.
- **Key Microservice Components:** API Gateway, Service Registry, Service Mesh (sidecar), Container Orchestrator, Message Broker.
- **Service Mesh** (e.g., Istio with Envoy sidecar) handles **east-west traffic**, security (mTLS), and observability.
- **API Gateway** handles **north-south traffic**, routing, auth, and rate-limiting.
- **5G Core** (3GPP Rel 16+) = **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** with NFs as microservices on **Kubernetes**.
- **Container advantages:** < 1s boot, 50–500 MB image, 1–3% overhead, fast horizontal scaling.
- **VM/VNF disadvantages:** 30–90s boot, GB-sized images, 5–15% overhead, slow updates.
- **Workload portability** is enabled by hardware abstraction and standardized interfaces (Vi-Ha, Nf-Vi).
- **Auto-scaling formula:** $N = \lceil \lambda \cdot T_{\text{process}} / \mu_{\text{instance}} \rceil$ (use **ceiling** function).
- **SFC latency** = sum of VNF processing latencies + sum of inter-VNF link latencies.
- **ETSI availability target:** **99.999%** (Five Nines) for carrier-grade NFs.
- **Service Function Chaining (SFC):** Ordered sequence of VNFs; uses **NSH (RFC 8300)** and **SFF (Service Function Forwarder)**.
- **Reference Points to Remember:** **Vi-Ha** (VIM–Hardware), **Nf-Vi** (VNF–VIM), **Or-Vi** (NFVO–VIM), **Or-Vnfm** (NFVO–VNFM), **Ve-Vnfm** (VNF–VNFM).
- **Kubernetes = de-facto orchestrator** for CNFs; provides HPA, VPA, self-healing, rolling updates, namespaces.
- **Cloud-native = microservices + containers + DevOps + CI/CD + observability.**

> **End of KTU Module 3 Notes — NFV & Microservices: Components and Architecture** *(PECCT521 — Network Fundamentals for Cloud | 2024 Scheme)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Networking for Containers (Docker, Kubernetes)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Networking for Containers (Docker, Kubernetes)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Container Networking?

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Container networking is the mechanism by which containers — lightweight, OS-level virtualized runtime environments — are provided with virtual network interfaces, IP addresses, routing tables, DNS services, and connectivity rules to communicate with each other, with the host machine, and with the external world. In the context of **Docker** and **Kubernetes**, it encompasses the **Container Network Model (CNM)**, the **Container Network Interface (CNI)**, overlay networks, service discovery, and network policies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Container networking is the *backbone* of microservice-based cloud-native architectures. Without it, pods cannot reach each other, services cannot be discovered, and load balancing becomes impossible.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine a high-rise **apartment building**:

- The **building** = your **Host Machine / Node**
- Each **apartment** = a **Container / Pod**
- The **mailing address (flat number)** = the **Container IP Address**
- The **building's internal switchboard** = the **Bridge Network (e.g., `docker0`)**
- The **building's intercom system** = **DNS-based Service Discovery**
- The **security guard with a visitor list** = **Network Policies**
- The **road connecting multiple buildings** = the **Overlay Network**
- The **building management office** = the **kube-proxy** (manages traffic rules)

Just as apartments share the building's water and electricity infrastructure but have *isolated* plumbing and wiring, containers share the host's kernel and network stack but are *namespaced* into their own network views. The networking layer makes sure every apartment (container) has water (packets) when it needs it, without one apartment flooding another.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Container** | A process + its filesystem + its namespaces, packaged for execution. |
| **Pod** | The *smallest deployable unit* in Kubernetes; one or more co-located containers sharing a network namespace. |
| **Bridge Network** | A software-based L2 switch (e.g., `docker0`) connecting containers on a single host. |
| **Overlay Network** | A virtual L3 network spanning multiple hosts (e.g., VXLAN-based). |
| **CNI (Container Network Interface)** | A specification + set of plugins that configure container networking. |
| **Service** | A stable virtual IP + DNS name that load-balances traffic to a set of pods. |
| **Namespace** (Linux) | OS-level isolation primitive providing each container its own network view. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Default CIDR Ranges** (memorize for KTU exams):
> - Docker default bridge: **`172.17.0.0/16`**
> - Kubernetes pod network: **`10.244.0.0/16`** (Flannel default)
> - Kubernetes service network: **`10.96.0.0/12`**

---

## 1.4 Why Does Container Networking Matter in Cloud?

In a cloud-native system, applications are decomposed into **dozens to thousands of microservices**, each running in its own container. Networking must therefore deliver:

1. **L3/L4 connectivity** between any two pods, anywhere in the cluster.
2. **Service discovery** (pods are ephemeral — their IPs change on restart).
3. **Load balancing** across multiple pod replicas.
4. **East-West traffic optimization** (pod-to-pod within a datacenter).
5. **North-South traffic control** (external client → service).
6. **Security isolation** through **NetworkPolicies** (Kubernetes equivalent of firewall rules).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Foundational Models: CNM vs. CNI

The container ecosystem converged on **two competing networking standards** that every KTU student must distinguish.

### 2.1.1 Container Network Model (CNM) — *Docker's Standard*

CNM was designed by Docker and is implemented by libnetwork. Its three core constructs are:

1. **Sandbox** — A container's isolated network stack (its own interfaces, routing table, DNS).
2. **Endpoint** — A virtual interface (veth pair) connecting a sandbox to a network.
3. **Network** — A software-defined, routable subnet grouping multiple endpoints.

$$
\text{CNM Architecture} = \{\text{Sandbox} \leftrightarrow \text{Endpoint} \leftrightarrow \text{Network}\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** CNM is *Docker-native*. If the question asks "Docker uses which model?" — answer **CNM**.

### 2.1.2 Container Network Interface (CNI) — *Kubernetes' Standard*

CNI is a CNCF-governed specification. It is **simpler** than CNM and follows a *plugin-based* philosophy:

- When a pod is scheduled, the kubelet invokes the CNI plugin binary.
- The plugin receives a **JSON configuration** describing the network.
- The plugin is responsible for **ADD** (attach container to network) and **DEL** (detach) operations.
- The plugin assigns the IP, configures routes, and returns a result to kubelet.

$$
\text{CNI Flow: } \text{kubelet} \xrightarrow{\text{JSON spec}} \text{CNI Plugin} \xrightarrow{\text{ADD/DEL}} \text{Network Configured}
$$

---

## 2.2 Docker Networking — The Five Driver Types

Docker provides **five built-in network drivers**. KTU exams frequently test the differences.

| Driver | Scope | Use Case | IPAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **bridge** | Single host | Default for standalone containers | Local subnet `172.17.0.0/16` |
| **host** | Removes isolation | High-performance, no NAT | Host's IP stack |
| **none** | No networking | Custom/secure workloads | None |
| **overlay** | Multi-host (Swarm) | Distributed services | VXLAN-based |
| **macvlan** | Physical LAN exposure | Legacy apps needing real MAC | Per-container MAC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bridge vs. Host — Key Distinction:**
> - `bridge`: container gets its own IP; uses NAT to reach the host's external network.
> - `host`: container *shares* the host's network namespace; **no NAT**, **no port mapping**.

### 2.2.1 The Default Bridge in Detail — `docker0`

When Docker starts, it creates a **virtual Ethernet bridge** named `docker0` (Linux `bridge` kernel module). Every container gets one end of a **veth pair**; the other end is attached to `docker0`. The architecture:

$$
\text{Container (eth0)} \xleftrightarrow{\text{veth pair}} \text{docker0 bridge} \xleftrightarrow{\text{host routing}} \text{eth0 (host)}
$$

**IP Allocation Logic:**

$$
\text{Next available IP} = \text{lowest free address in } 172.17.0.0/16 \text{ (skipping } .1 \text{ reserved for gateway)}
$$

---

## 2.3 Kubernetes Networking — The Four Golden Rules

Kubernetes mandates a specific networking model. **Memorize these four invariants** — they appear in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions almost every semester.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Kubernetes Networking Contract (RFC-coined as the "Kubernetes Networking Model"):**
> 1. Every **Pod** gets its **own routable IP address**.
> 2. **Pods on any node** can communicate with **all other pods** without NAT.
> 3. **Agents on a node** (kubelet, daemons) can communicate with all pods on that node.
> 4. **Pods in the host network** can communicate with all other pods without NAT.

These four rules imply a **flat, L3 routable network** spanning every node — implemented by CNI plugins like **Flannel**, **Calico**, **Weave**, and **Cilium**.

---

## 2.4 The Kubernetes Networking Stack — Layer by Layer

| Layer | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **L2 (Container ↔ Pod)** | `cni0` bridge (Linux bridge) | Connects containers within a Pod to the Pod's network namespace. |
| **L3 (Pod ↔ Pod across nodes)** | CNI plugin (Flannel/Calico) | Encapsulates and routes packets (VXLAN, IPIP, BGP). |
| **L4 (Service abstraction)** | `kube-proxy` | Implements virtual IP → pod IP translation (iptables/IPVS). |
| **L7 (Ingress)** | Ingress Controller (NGINX, Traefik) | HTTP/HTTPS routing, TLS termination, path/host-based rules. |
| **DNS** | CoreDNS | Resolves `service.namespace.svc.cluster.local` → ClusterIP. |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Concept Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Container networking is *more architectural than algebraic*, so the "formulas" below are **subnet math, port math, and replication math** that KTU examiners love to test.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CIDR block for `N` pods** | $\lceil \log_2(N) \rceil$ host bits | $N = 1024 \Rightarrow 32 - 10 = 22 \Rightarrow /22$ |
| 2 | **Available IPs in `/C`** | $2^{(32 - C)} - 2$ | $/24 = 254$ usable hosts |
| 3 | **Max pods per node (K8s default)** | $2^{11} - 2 = 2046$ | Default `--max-pods-per-node` |
| 4 | **NodePort range** | $30000 - 32767$ | 2768 ports available |
| 5 | **ClusterIP range (default)** | `10.96.0.0/12` | $\approx 1{,}048{,}574$ services |
| 6 | **Service Mesh sidecar overhead** | $+1$ pod per app pod | 100 app pods $\Rightarrow$ 100 sidecar pods |
| 7 | **VXLAN overhead** | $50$ bytes (VXLAN header + UDP) | Per-packet encapsulation cost |
| 8 | **BGP peer limit (Calico default)** | $50$ peers per node | Scale-up boundary |

### 2.5.1 Service Types — Comparison Table

| Service Type | IP Address | Scope | Mark Likely on Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ClusterIP** | Internal cluster IP | Intra-cluster only | ⭐⭐⭐ Most common |
| **NodePort** | Exposes on every node's IP at a static port | External access via `<NodeIP>:<NodePort>` | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **LoadBalancer** | Provisions a cloud LB (AWS ELB, GCP LB) | External traffic | ⭐⭐ |
| **ExternalName** | CNAME DNS alias | Service-to-external redirection | ⭐ |
| **Headless Service** | No ClusterIP; returns pod IPs directly via DNS | StatefulSets | ⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.6 kube-proxy — The Traffic Director

`kube-proxy` runs on **every node** and watches the Kubernetes API for changes to Services and Endpoints. It programs the node's networking rules to forward traffic. There are **three modes**:

1. **iptables (default in older K8s)**
   - Watches API server → writes iptables rules.
   - Packet flow: `PREROUTING → KUBE-SERVICES → KUBE-SVC-XXX → KUBE-SEP-XXX (DNAT to pod IP)`.
   - Linear scanning, O(n) per packet.

2. **IPVS (default in K8s ≥ 1.11+)**
   - Uses Linux kernel IP Virtual Server.
   - Supports multiple load-balancing algorithms: `rr`, `lc`, `dh`, `sh`, `sed`, `nq`.
   - Hash table lookup → O(1) per packet.

3. **Userspace (legacy, deprecated)**
   - kube-proxy itself acts as a proxy process.
   - Slow, removed in K8s 1.18+.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Modern service meshes (Istio, Linkerd) **replace kube-proxy** with eBPF-based data planes (Cilium) for sub-millisecond latency and mutual TLS between every pod.

---

## 2.7 CNI Plugins — Head-to-Head

| Plugin | Encapsulation | Network Policy | Performance | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flannel** | VXLAN | ❌ (needs Calico for it) | Good | Simple clusters |
| **Calico** | None / IPIP / VXLAN | ✅ Full L3/L4 policies | Excellent | Production, security |
| **Weave Net** | VXLAN-like | ✅ | Good | Mid-size clusters |
| **Cilium** | Native / VXLAN | ✅ L3/L4/L7 | Excellent (eBPF) | High-performance, observability |
| **Antrea** | VXLAN / Geneve | ✅ | Excellent | VMware/K8s-on-vSphere |

---

## 2.8 Network Policies — The K8s Firewall

A **NetworkPolicy** is a Kubernetes object that controls **ingress and egress** traffic to/from pods using **label selectors**.

```yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: frontend
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8080
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing a NetworkPolicy does **nothing** unless a CNI plugin that *enforces* it is installed. Flannel does **not** enforce policies. Always mention the CNI name in your exam answer.

---

## 2.9 The Pod-to-Pod Communication Flow (Cross-Node)

The most common KTU Part B (14-mark) question: *"Explain how a packet travels from Pod A on Node 1 to Pod B on Node 2."*

The canonical 7-step path:

$$
\text{Pod A eth0} \to \text{veth} \to \text{cni0 bridge} \to \text{flannel.1 (VXLAN)} \to \text{eth0 (Node 1)} \xrightarrow{\text{underlay}} \text{eth0 (Node 2)} \to \text{flannel.1} \to \text{cni0} \to \text{veth} \to \text{Pod B eth0}
$$

We will fully expand this in **SECTION 3**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Subnet Sizing for a Kubernetes Cluster

**Problem:** A startup is designing a Kubernetes cluster for **500 pods** spread across **20 nodes**, plus a **service network** of 50 services. They want the pod network to support **headroom of 2×** for scaling.

**Required:**
1. Calculate the minimum CIDR block for the pod network.
2. Verify if `10.244.0.0/16` is sufficient.
3. Compute the usable IPs.

---

### Step 1 — Determine the *raw* number of pod IPs required.

$$
N_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{pods}} \times \text{headroom multiplier}
$$

$$
N_{\text{raw}} = 500 \times 2 = 1000 \text{ pod IPs (minimum)}
$$

**Logic:** Each pod needs one IP; we provision 2× to allow scaling, node restarts, and rolling updates.

---

### Step 2 — Round up to the next power of two, then derive the host bits.

$$
N_{\text{rounded}} = 2^{\lceil \log_2(1000) \rceil}
$$

$$
N_{\text{rounded}} = 2^{10} = 1024
$$

**Logic:** CIDR subnets must have host counts as powers of two; we round 1000 up to 1024.

---

### Step 3 — Compute the number of host bits.

$$
h = \log_2(N_{\text{rounded}}) = \log_2(1024) = 10 \text{ host bits}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Compute the prefix length.

$$
C = 32 - h = 32 - 10 = 22
$$

So the minimum pod CIDR is **`/22`**.

---

### Step 5 — Verify `10.244.0.0/16`.

`/16` provides $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ total addresses, minus 2 (network + broadcast) = **65,534 usable IPs**.

For 1000 required pods, `10.244.0.0/16` is *more than enough*. ✅

---

### Step 6 — Compute usable IPs of `/16` formally.

$$
\text{Usable} = 2^{(32 - 16)} - 2 = 2^{16} - 2 = 65{,}534
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer for the KTU Examiner:**
> Minimum pod CIDR = **`/22`**; the chosen `10.244.0.0/16` is sufficient, providing **65,534 usable IPs** — a headroom of $\frac{65534 - 1000}{1000} \times 100\% \approx 6453\%$ overprovisioning.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving the NodePort Range Formula

**Problem:** Prove why the NodePort range is `30000–32767`.

---

### Step 1 — Recall the IANA assignment.

The IANA reserves ports `0–1023` for *well-known services*, `1024–49151` for *registered services*, and `49152–65535` for *dynamic/ephemeral*.

Kubernetes must avoid both `0–1023` (privileged) and `49152–65535` (ephemeral collision risk). This leaves a 2768-port window in the *registered* range.

---

### Step 2 — Define the bounds.

$$
P_{\text{low}} = 30000
$$

$$
P_{\text{high}} = 32767
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the cardinality.

$$
\text{Range size} = P_{\text{high}} - P_{\text{low}} + 1 = 32767 - 30000 + 1 = 2768 \text{ ports}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Express as a 15-bit space.

$$
2768 = 2^{11} + 0.5 \times 2^{12} \approx 2^{11.44}
$$

The K8s API field `service.nodePort` is constrained to an `int32` with a default min/max, encoded as:

$$
\text{nodePort} \in [P_{\text{low}}, P_{\text{high}}] \subset \mathbb{Z}_{32}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Validation.

If we tried to shift the range up to `32768–35535`, we collide with the ephemeral port range. If we shift down to `1024–3791`, we conflict with services like SSH (22), HTTP (80), HTTPS (443). Hence **`30000–32767`** is the consensus safe window. ✅

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Step-by-Step Cross-Node Pod Communication (Flannel VXLAN)

**Setup:**
- Pod A: `10.244.1.5` on Node 1
- Pod B: `10.244.2.7` on Node 2
- Flannel VXLAN backend with VTEP MACs learned via etcd.

---

### Step 1 — Source Packet Generation

Pod A's application (e.g., NGINX) generates a TCP SYN destined for `10.244.2.7:80`.

The packet's IP header reads:

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Source IP | `10.244.1.5` |
| Destination IP | `10.244.2.7` |
| Protocol | TCP (6) |
| TTL | 64 |

---

### Step 2 — Routing Decision in Pod A's Network Namespace

The pod's routing table contains:

$$
\text{default via } 10.244.1.1 \text{ dev eth0}
$$

The kernel performs a longest-prefix match, identifies that the destination is **not local**, and sends the packet to the default gateway `10.244.1.1` via the `veth` pair.

---

### Step 3 — Arrival at `cni0` Bridge (Node 1)

The packet exits the veth and lands on the `cni0` Linux bridge. The bridge's forwarding database (FDB) checks the destination MAC — since the destination IP is on a *different* node, the bridge floods the packet to all ports and sends it to the flannel interface.

---

### Step 4 — Flannel's Routing Decision

Flannel's interface `flannel.1` (a VXLAN device) has a route table entry:

$$
10.244.2.0/24 \text{ dev flannel.1}
$$

The kernel matches this route and decides: *"This destination is reachable through VXLAN."*

---

### Step 5 — VXLAN Encapsulation

Flannel looks up its FDB (populated from etcd) to find the **VTEP IP** of Node 2, say `192.168.1.12`. It then wraps the original packet inside a new outer header:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer Ethernet} &: \text{SrcMAC} = \text{Node 1}, \; \text{DstMAC} = \text{Node 2 gateway} \\
\text{Outer IP} &: \text{SrcIP} = 192.168.1.11, \; \text{DstIP} = 192.168.1.12 \\
\text{Outer UDP} &: \text{SrcPort} = \text{random}, \; \text{DstPort} = 4789 \text{ (VXLAN)} \\
\text{VXLAN Header} &: \text{VNI} = 1, \; \text{Flags} = 0x08 \\
\text{Inner Packet} &: \text{Original TCP/IP from Pod A to Pod B}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

This is the **VXLAN encapsulation** — a 50-byte overhead on top of the original packet.

---

### Step 6 — Underlay Transit

The encapsulated packet traverses the *physical* network (or VPC underlay) using the outer IP. Standard L3 routing delivers it to Node 2.

---

### Step 7 — Decapsulation on Node 2

Node 2's `flannel.1` interface receives the packet on UDP port 4789. The kernel strips the VXLAN header and **injects the inner packet** into the local network stack, with the original `10.244.2.7` destination.

---

### Step 8 — Final Hop to Pod B

The kernel looks up `10.244.2.7` in Node 2's routes:

$$
10.244.2.0/24 \text{ dev cni0}
$$

The packet crosses `cni0` → `veth` → into Pod B's `eth0`. **Pod B receives the TCP SYN.** ✅

---

### 3.3.1 Total Latency Budget (Qualitative)

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{app}} + T_{\text{veth}} + T_{\text{bridge}} + T_{\text{encap}} + T_{\text{underlay}} + T_{\text{decap}} + T_{\text{bridge}} + T_{\text{veth}} + T_{\text{app}}
$$

For intra-datacenter hops, the **encapsulation + decapsulation cost is typically 20–50 µs** — negligible compared to the application processing time.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Service-to-Pod Load Balancing (iptables mode)

**Setup:** A `Service: my-svc` of type `ClusterIP = 10.96.0.42` has **3 pod endpoints**: `10.244.1.10`, `10.244.1.11`, `10.244.2.20`.

**Sequence of iptables chains traversed:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
&\text{PREROUTING} \to \text{KUBE-SERVICES} \\
&\text{KUBE-SERVICES} \xrightarrow{\text{match } 10.96.0.42} \text{KUBE-SVC-XXXX} \\
&\text{KUBE-SVC-XXXX} \xrightarrow{\text{statistic --mode random --prob 0.333}} \text{KUBE-SEP-AAA} \to \text{DNAT to } 10.244.1.10 \\
&\text{KUBE-SVC-XXXX} \xrightarrow{\text{prob 0.5 (of remaining)}} \text{KUBE-SEP-BBB} \to \text{DNAT to } 10.244.1.11 \\
&\text{KUBE-SVC-XXXX} \xrightarrow{\text{else}} \text{KUBE-SEP-CCC} \to \text{DNAT to } 10.244.2.20}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Logic:** iptables doesn't support true weighted random selection; it simulates equal probability using successive `-m statistic --mode random` rules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Probability Verification:** The first rule fires with $p = \frac{1}{3}$. If missed, the second rule fires with $p = \frac{1}{2}$ of the remaining $\frac{2}{3}$ — which is $\frac{1}{3}$. The final else-rule handles the last $\frac{1}{3}$. **Total = 1.0** ✅

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — CIDR Subnetting for a Multi-Tier Application

**Problem:** Design a `NetworkPolicy` and subnetwork for a 3-tier app:
- **Frontend:** 50 pods
- **Backend:** 100 pods
- **Database:** 5 pods (StatefulSet)

---

### Step 1 — IP allocation per tier.

| Tier | Pods Required | CIDR | Usable IPs |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frontend | 50 | `10.244.10.0/26` | $2^{6} - 2 = 62$ |
| Backend | 100 | `10.244.10.64/25` | $2^{7} - 2 = 126$ |
| Database | 5 | `10.244.10.192/29` | $2^{3} - 2 = 6$ |

---

### Step 2 — Verify the binary boundaries.

$$
/26 \Rightarrow 11\text{xxxxxx} \Rightarrow \text{boundary at } .64
$$

$$
/25 \Rightarrow 10\text{xxxxxx} \Rightarrow \text{boundary at } .128
$$

$$
/29 \Rightarrow 11000\text{xxx} \Rightarrow \text{boundary at } .192
$$

All subnets are aligned to their natural power-of-two boundaries — no IP waste.

---

### Step 3 — Policy enforcement.

```yaml
# Allow Frontend → Backend
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      tier: backend
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              tier: frontend
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8080
```

```yaml
# Allow Backend → Database ONLY
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-backend-to-db
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      tier: database
  policyTypes: ["Ingress"]
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              tier: backend
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of rule evaluation matters:** Kubernetes evaluates all NetworkPolicies *additively*. A pod is isolated if **any** policy selects it and **no** rule allows the traffic. This is unlike AWS Security Groups (which are allow-by-default per attachment).
