# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Docker Networking Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Host["Physical Host Machine"]
        direction TB
        Kern["Linux Kernel (Network Stack)"]

        subgraph Dock0["docker0 Bridge (172.17.0.1)"]
            Bc["Bridge Forwarding Table"]
        end

        subgraph C1["Container A"]
            C1eth["eth0: 172.17.0.2"]
            C1app["App Process"]
        end
        subgraph C2["Container B"]
            C2eth["eth0: 172.17.0.3"]
            C2app["App Process"]
        end
        subgraph C3["Container C (host network)"]
            C3share["Shares host eth0 directly"]
        end

        HostEth["Host eth0 (Physical NIC)"]

        Kern --- Dock0
        Dock0 --- C1
        Dock0 --- C2
        HostEth --- C3
    end

    ExtNet["External Network / Internet"]

    HostEth <--> ExtNet
    Dock0 <--> HostEth
```

**Reading the diagram:** Containers A and B live behind `docker0` and reach the outside world via NAT through the host's NIC. Container C uses `host` networking and shares the host's IP stack — there is no bridge, no NAT, and no isolation.

---

## 4.2 The Kubernetes Pod-to-Service Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Clust["Kubernetes Cluster"]
        direction TB

        subgraph N1["Node 1"]
            direction TB
            cni0A["cni0 bridge"]
            P1A["Pod A (10.244.1.5)"]
            P1B["Pod B (10.244.1.6)"]
            flan1["flannel.1 (VXLAN VTEP)"]
            kp1["kube-proxy (iptables/IPVS)"]
            cni0A --- P1A
            cni0A --- P1B
            cni0A --- flan1
            kp1 --- cni0A
        end

        subgraph N2["Node 2"]
            direction TB
            cni0B["cni0 bridge"]
            P2A["Pod C (10.244.2.7)"]
            P2B["Pod D (10.244.2.8)"]
            flan2["flannel.1 (VXLAN VTEP)"]
            kp2["kube-proxy (iptables/IPVS)"]
            cni0B --- P2A
            cni0B --- P2B
            cni0B --- flan2
            kp2 --- cni0B
        end

        Control["Control Plane"]
        APISrv["kube-apiserver"]
        CoreDNS["CoreDNS (Service IP -> ClusterIP)"]
        Sched["kube-scheduler"]
        Control --- APISrv
        APISrv --- CoreDNS
        APISrv --- Sched
    end

    Svc["Service: my-svc (10.96.0.42)"]
    Svc --- APISrv
    flan1 <--> flan2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Pod-to-pod traffic across nodes flows through the **VXLAN overlay** (flannel.1 ↔ flannel.1). Service IP resolution and endpoint updates are pulled by **kube-proxy** from the **apiserver**. **CoreDNS** provides the `my-svc.default.svc.cluster.local` → `10.96.0.42` translation.

---

## 4.3 kube-proxy iptables Rule Chain (Flow Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Incoming Packet (dst=10.96.0.42:80)"] --> B["PREROUTING"]
    B --> C["KUBE-SERVICES table"]
    C --> D{"Match Service IP?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["KUBE-SVC-XXXX chain"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Normal routing"]

    E --> G["Statistic Module: random 1/3"]
    G -- "Hit" --> H["KUBE-SEP-AAA -> DNAT 10.244.1.10:80"]
    G -- "Miss" --> I["Statistic Module: random 1/2"]
    I -- "Hit" --> J["KUBE-SEP-BBB -> DNAT 10.244.1.11:80"]
    I -- "Miss" --> K["KUBE-SEP-CCC -> DNAT 10.244.2.20:80"]

    H --> L["POSTROUTING -> Out to Pod"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

**Reading the diagram:** The packet enters the netfilter `PREROUTING` hook, is matched in `KUBE-SERVICES`, jumps to a per-service chain, and then a **statistic-based random selection** performs DNAT to one of the backend pods.

---

## 4.4 Kubernetes Service Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Need to expose a workload?"] --> Q1{"Is it HTTP/HTTPS only?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Need external access?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q3["Use ClusterIP Service"]

    Q2 -- "Yes" --> Q4["Use Ingress + ClusterIP"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3

    Q3 --> Q5{"Need direct external L4 access?"}
    Q5 -- "Yes, dev/test only" --> Q6["Use NodePort"]
    Q5 -- "Yes, production" --> Q7["Use LoadBalancer"]
    Q5 -- "External CNAME alias" --> Q8["Use ExternalName"]

    Start --> State{"Stateful workload (DB)?"}
    State -- "Yes" --> Headless["Use Headless Service + StatefulSet"]
    State -- "No" --> Start
```

**Reading the diagram:** This decision tree is the most common-sense way to answer KTU's "compare Service types" question — go through it live in your answer.

---

## 4.5 CNI Plugin — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Kubelet["kubelet (per-node agent)"]
        direction LR
        K1["Watch pod spec from API server"]
        K2["Invoke CNI plugin with JSON spec"]
        K3["Receive result (IP assigned)"]
        K1 --> K2 --> K3
    end

    subgraph CNI["CNI Plugin (e.g., Calico)"]
        direction LR
        P1["ipam: allocate IP from pool"]
        P2["veth: create veth pair"]
        P3["bridge: attach to cni0"]
        P4["route: add route for pod subnet"]
        P5["policy: install NetworkPolicy rules"]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    end

    subgraph Data["Data Plane (Linux Kernel)"]
        direction LR
        N1["Network namespace (per pod)"]
        N2["iptables / nftables / eBPF"]
        N3["Linux bridge / veth"]
        N4["Routing table"]
        N1 --- N2
        N2 --- N3
        N3 --- N4
    end

    K2 -- "execve(cni_binary, ADD)" --> P1
    P3 --> N3
    P5 --> N2
    P4 --> N4
```

**Reading the diagram:** The kubelet's request flows into the CNI plugin, which orchestrates five sequential steps: IP allocation, veth pair creation, bridge attachment, route installation, and policy enforcement — each step mutating the Linux kernel's data plane.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1: Define CNI. List any two CNI plugins used in Kubernetes.**
**Model Answer (KTU Examiner's Wording):**
CNI stands for **Container Network Interface**. It is a Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) specification that standardizes the procedure for configuring network interfaces in Linux containers. The kubelet invokes a CNI plugin at pod creation, which assigns the IP, configures routing, and connects the pod to the cluster network.
Two popular CNI plugins are: **(1) Flannel** — a simple VXLAN-based overlay, and **(2) Calico** — a high-performance plugin supporting BGP routing and L3/L4 network policies.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between a Docker `bridge` network and a `host` network.**
**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | `bridge` | `host` |
|---|---|---|
| Network namespace | Isolated per container | Shared with the host |
| IP address | Container gets its own IP from `docker0` | Uses the host's IP directly |
| NAT required | Yes, for external traffic | No |
| Port conflict | Two containers can use the same internal port | One process per port globally |
| Performance | Slight overhead due to NAT | Near-native performance |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, you are required to answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks from each module. The questions below provide the two standard internal choices (OR alternatives). Both carry equal weight.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Kubernetes Service & kube-proxy Internals**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the four types of Kubernetes Services. For each, mention the scope of exposure, an example use case, and the YAML `type:` field value.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **ClusterIP** — The *default* service type. Exposes the service on a **cluster-internal IP**. Traffic is reachable only from within the cluster.
   - YAML: `type: ClusterIP`
   - Use case: Internal microservice-to-microservice calls (e.g., `frontend → backend`).
   - Implementation: iptables/IPVS rules in kube-proxy perform DNAT to a backing pod.

2. **NodePort** — Exposes the service on **every node's IP** at a static port in the range `30000–32767`.
   - YAML: `type: NodePort`
   - Use case: Development/testing where you don't yet have a load balancer.
   - Implementation: ClusterIP is auto-created; the kubelet opens the NodePort on each node.

3. **LoadBalancer** — Provisions an **external load balancer** (cloud-provider-specific, e.g., AWS NLB, GCP TCP LB) that routes to NodePort/ClusterIP.
   - YAML: `type: LoadBalancer`
   - Use case: Production external traffic with stable IP/DNS.
   - Implementation: Cloud controller manager creates the LB and configures backend nodes.

4. **ExternalName** — Maps the service to a **CNAME** of an external DNS name. No proxying is performed.
   - YAML: `type: ExternalName`
   - Use case: Referencing an external managed database (e.g., `my-db.example.com`).

5. **Headless Service** (often considered a fifth type, technically a ClusterIP with `clusterIP: None`) — Returns the **pod IPs directly** in DNS A-records.
   - Use case: StatefulSets where each pod needs a stable network identity (e.g., `postgres-0`, `postgres-1`).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the working of `kube-proxy` in `iptables` mode. How does it differ from `IPVS` mode?**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of kube-proxy in iptables mode:**

1. kube-proxy runs as a **daemonset on every node**.
2. It **watches the API server** for changes to `Service` and `Endpoints` objects.
3. For each Service, it creates an iptables chain in the `KUBE-SERVICES` table.
4. For each backing pod (Endpoint), it creates a chain in `KUBE-SEP-XXX` that performs `DNAT` to the pod IP.
5. Incoming packets whose destination IP matches the Service ClusterIP traverse:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{PREROUTING} &\to \text{KUBE-SERVICES} \\
&\to \text{KUBE-SVC-XXX} \quad (\text{random load distribution via } {-m} \text{ statistic}) \\
&\to \text{KUBE-SEP-XXX} \quad (\text{DNAT to pod IP})
\end{aligned}
}
$$

6. The selected pod processes the packet and **replies directly** to the source (the connection is preserved end-to-end thanks to conntrack).

**Difference Table: iptables vs. IPVS**

| Feature | iptables | IPVS |
|---|---|---|
| Data structure | Linked list of rules | Hash table |
| Lookup complexity | $O(n)$ per packet | $O(1)$ per packet |
| LB algorithms | Equal random only | `rr`, `lc`, `dh`, `sh`, `sed`, `nq` |
| Scale (services) | ~5000 services | ~10,000+ services |
| Kernel dependency | `netfilter` | `IP_VS` kernel module |
| Use case | Small/medium clusters | Large production clusters |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Don't** confuse **kube-proxy** with the **kubelet** — they are different daemons.
> 2. **Don't** say iptables "balances" — say it *randomly distributes with equal probability*.
> 3. **Don't** forget to mention **conntrack** — it is the kernel module that tracks the connection so the reply can be reverse-NATed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Docker Networking, CNM, and Pod-to-Pod Communication**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Container Network Model (CNM) with a neat architecture diagram. List the Docker network drivers and their use cases.**

**Model Solution:**

**Container Network Model (CNM):**
CNM is Docker's reference architecture for container networking, implemented by `libnetwork`. It has three primitives:

1. **Sandbox** — An isolated network stack (interfaces, routing table, DNS) tied to a single container.
2. **Endpoint** — A virtual interface (typically a `veth` pair) that connects a Sandbox to a Network.
3. **Network** — A software-defined, L2-broadcast domain (or L3 routable) that groups multiple Endpoints.

The relation is: **Container ↔ Sandbox ↔ Endpoint ↔ Network**.

**Docker Network Drivers (5 types):**

| Driver | Scope | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| `bridge` | Single host | Default; isolated dev/test |
| `host` | Single host | High-throughput, no NAT needed |
| `none` | Single host | No networking — for custom setup |
| `overlay` | Multi-host (Swarm) | Distributed services across nodes |
| `macvlan` | Single host with L2 visibility | Legacy apps needing a real MAC |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the step-by-step flow of a packet from Pod A on Node 1 to Pod B on Node 2 in a Kubernetes cluster using Flannel VXLAN as the CNI plugin. Include a sequence diagram in your explanation.**

**Model Solution:**

The flow consists of **8 steps**:

1. **Packet generation** — Pod A's application creates a TCP/IP packet with `Dst=10.244.2.7:80`.
2. **Routing in Pod A** — The packet's destination is non-local; the kernel routes via the default gateway `10.244.1.1` over the `veth` pair.
3. **Bridge ingress** — Packet arrives on `cni0` (Node 1); bridge flooding DB forwards it toward the flannel interface.
4. **Flannel route lookup** — `flannel.1` has route `10.244.2.0/24`; kernel decides VXLAN.
5. **VXLAN encapsulation** — The packet is wrapped in a UDP/VXLAN header destined to Node 2's VTEP IP (e.g., `192.168.1.12`), with VXLAN Network Identifier (VNI) = 1.
6. **Underlay transit** — Routed via the physical/VPC network from Node 1 to Node 2.
7. **Decapsulation** — Node 2's `flannel.1` strips the VXLAN header and reinjects the inner packet.
8. **Pod B delivery** — Node 2's routing sends the packet via `cni0` → `veth` → `Pod B eth0`. **Connection complete.** ✅

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PA as Pod A (10.244.1.5)
    participant V1 as veth (Node 1)
    participant C1 as cni0 bridge
    participant F1 as flannel.1 (VXLAN)
    participant NET as Underlay Network
    participant F2 as flannel.1 (Node 2)
    participant C2 as cni0 bridge
    participant V2 as veth (Node 2)
    participant PB as Pod B (10.244.2.7)

    PA->>V1: TCP SYN dst=10.244.2.7:80
    V1->>C1: Enter bridge
    C1->>F1: Route 10.244.2.0/24 via VXLAN
    F1->>F1: Encapsulate (Outer IP: 192.168.1.11 -> 192.168.1.12, VNI=1)
    F1->>NET: Forward
    NET->>F2: Deliver to Node 2 VTEP
    F2->>F2: Decapsulate VXLAN header
    F2->>C2: Inject inner packet
    C2->>V2: Bridge to veth
    V2->>PB: TCP SYN arrives at eth0
    PB-->>PA: TCP SYN-ACK (reverse path)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't** say "containers communicate directly" — say *"the veth pair bridges the network namespace of the pod to the host's `cni0` bridge."*
> 2. **Don't** forget to mention **VTEP (VXLAN Tunnel Endpoint)** — examiners specifically look for this term.
> 3. **Don't** mix up the **inner IP** (pod IP) and the **outer IP** (host/VTEP IP) — that is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 4. The **reply path** is *not* symmetric in encapsulation; the destination pod's reply goes back via standard pod-to-pod routing — make sure you state that explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **The Ultimate 30-Second Revision Checklist — Re-read this just before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Container networking** is the layer that makes microservices reachable.
- [x] **Docker uses CNM (libnetwork)**; **Kubernetes uses CNI plugins**.
- [x] Docker has **5 network drivers**: `bridge`, `host`, `none`, `overlay`, `macvlan`.
- [x] Default Docker bridge CIDR: `172.17.0.0/16`; gateway: `172.17.0.1`.
- [x] **Kubernetes networking contract** has **4 golden rules** — all pods can reach all pods without NAT.
- [x] **Services** = stable virtual IPs for ephemeral pods.
- [x] **Service types**: `ClusterIP`, `NodePort`, `LoadBalancer`, `ExternalName`, **Headless** (`clusterIP: None`).
- [x] **NodePort range = 30000–32767** (2768 ports).
- [x] **kube-proxy** modes: `iptables` (random, $O(n)$), `IPVS` (hash, $O(1)$, multiple LB algos), `userspace` (deprecated).
- [x] **CNI plugins**: Flannel (simple VXLAN, no policy), Calico (BGP, policy), Weave, Cilium (eBPF).
- [x] **NetworkPolicies** = K8s firewall; only enforced by policy-aware CNIs.
- [x] **Cross-node packet flow** in Flannel VXLAN: 8 steps, 50-byte encapsulation overhead, VTEP-to-VTEP.
- [x] **CoreDNS** resolves `<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local` to ClusterIP.
- [x] **Ingress** handles L7 (HTTP/HTTPS) routing; **Service** handles L4 (TCP/UDP).
- [x] Always mention **conntrack** when explaining kube-proxy — it enables connection tracking for the reply path.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Networking for Containers (Docker, Kubernetes)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Network Design Principles: Designing for Scalability, Flexibility, Redundancy, and Disaster Recovery Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Network Design Principles: Designing for Scalability, Flexibility, Redundancy, and Disaster Recovery Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Cloud Network Design?

Cloud Network Design refers to the structured engineering discipline of architecting logical and physical networking topologies — including routing, addressing, segmentation, and service insertion points — that deliver compute, storage, and application services over virtualized infrastructure. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is the foundational blueprint that ensures a distributed cloud platform can meet Service Level Objectives (SLOs) agreed with end users.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus):**
> *Cloud Network Design is the disciplined planning of topologies, protocols, IP address schemes, and traffic-engineering mechanisms that allow workloads to communicate reliably, securely, and predictably across on-premises, edge, and public-cloud domains.*

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of Cloud Network Design

The syllabus bundles these into four overlapping design goals, often visualized as the corners of a "resilience square":

| # | Pillar | One-Line Meaning |
|---|--------|------------------|
| 1 | **Scalability** | Grow or shrink capacity on demand |
| 2 | **Flexibility** | Adapt topology and services to changing needs |
| 3 | **Redundancy** | No single point of failure (SPOF) |
| 4 | **Disaster Recovery (DR)** | Restore service after catastrophic events |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four pillars are **mutually reinforcing**, not independent. A truly "good" cloud network balances all four; a design that maximises only one (e.g., extreme scalability) usually fails on another (e.g., cost or DR).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogies

Think of designing a cloud network like designing a **city's road system**:

- **Scalability** = being able to add new flyovers and ring roads during Diwali traffic season, then close them in January. The city *grows its roads* elastically.
- **Flexibility** = having dedicated bus lanes that can be flipped to car lanes depending on whether it is a weekday or weekend. The *same road serves different roles*.
- **Redundancy** = every critical destination (hospital, airport) has **at least two independent routes**. If one flyover is shut, the ambulance still reaches in time.
- **Disaster Recovery** = having a fully-built alternate city blueprint on standby. If a flood wipes out the original city, the alternate city can take over within a defined timeline.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When the question says *"differentiate scalability vs elasticity"*, remember — Scalability is the **ability** to handle growth; Elasticity is the **automated mechanism** that actually does it. Scalability is the *capability*; elasticity is the *behaviour*.

### 1.5 Sub-Topics to be Covered

1. Scalability — vertical vs horizontal, capacity planning
2. Flexibility — software-defined, multi-cloud, hybrid
3. Redundancy — N+1, N+M, active-active/active-passive
4. Disaster Recovery — RTO, RPO, hot/warm/cold sites, multi-region failover

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Scalability — The Ability to Grow

Scalability is decomposed into two orthogonal axes:

**A. Vertical Scalability (Scale-Up / Scale-Down)**
- Add **more power** to an existing node (more CPU cores, RAM, NIC bandwidth).
- *Limitation*: hard ceiling of physical hardware, expensive, requires downtime.
- Example: Resizing an AWS EC2 instance from `t3.medium` → `m5.4xlarge`.

**B. Horizontal Scalability (Scale-Out / Scale-In)**
- Add **more nodes** to the pool, distributing load.
- *Advantage*: theoretically unbounded, commodity hardware, no downtime.
- Example: Adding 100 more web-server VMs behind a load balancer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statelessness is the prerequisite for horizontal scalability.** If your application stores session state on the local server, you cannot freely scale-in without losing user data. Session state must be externalised to Redis, Memcached, or a database.

**C. Capacity Planning Equation**

The peak capacity that must be provisioned is given by:

$$
C_{provisioned} = \lambda_{avg} \times P_{factor} \times S_{safety}
$$

Where:
- $\lambda_{avg}$ = average load (requests/sec, GB/day, etc.)
- $P_{factor}$ = peak-to-average ratio (e.g., **2.0** for e-commerce during sales)
- $S_{safety}$ = safety/headroom factor (typically **1.25 to 1.5**)

### 2.2 Flexibility — Adaptability of the Network

Flexibility is operationalised through:

1. **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** — control plane separated from data plane (OpenFlow, Cisco ACI, VMware NSX).
2. **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** — firewalls, load balancers, routers run as VMs/containers.
3. **Multi-cloud & Hybrid designs** — workloads can migrate between AWS / Azure / GCP / on-prem.
4. **Programmability** — Infrastructure-as-Code (Terraform, Ansible, Pulumi) lets topology be re-declared in seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard SLA metric for flexibility in KTU parlance:** *Time-to-provision a new network segment* (target: minutes, not weeks).

### 2.3 Redundancy — Eliminating Single Points of Failure

Redundancy has formal engineering grades:

| Grade | Meaning | Example |
|-------|---------|---------|
| **N** | Bare minimum, no redundancy | One router, one switch |
| **N+1** | One spare for every component group | 2 uplinks, one active + one standby |
| **N+M** | M spares for every N active | 4 PSUs, 2 active + 2 standby |
| **2N** | Full duplication | Two complete data centres, active-active |
| **2N+1** | Full duplication plus one extra | Used in tier-IV data centres |

**Active-Active vs Active-Passive:**
- **Active-Active (A/A):** Both sites serve traffic simultaneously; double the throughput.
- **Active-Passive (A/P):** One site serves; the other is "hot standby" waiting.

### 2.4 Disaster Recovery — The Last Line of Defence

DR is formalised using two golden metrics:

- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Maximum acceptable downtime.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Maximum acceptable data loss (measured in time).

$$
\text{Maximum Data Loss} = RPO \quad (\text{time units of data})
$$
$$
\text{Maximum Service Downtime} = RTO \quad (\text{time until service restored})
$$

**Three canonical DR site configurations:**

| Configuration | Cost | RTO | RPO | Use Case |
|---------------|------|-----|-----|----------|
| **Cold Standby** | Low | Hours to days | Hours | Non-critical, archival |
| **Warm Standby** | Medium | Minutes | Minutes | Business apps |
| **Hot Standby (Active-Active)** | High | Seconds | ~0 (sync replication) | Tier-0 / banking |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table — these formulas appear every KTU end-semester paper.

| Concept | Formula | Standard / Unit | Notes |
|---------|---------|-----------------|-------|
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Expressed as % | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| **Annualised Downtime** | $D_{year} = 525600 \times (1 - A)$ | Minutes per year | 525600 = 365 × 24 × 60 |
| **Nines of Availability** | $A_n = 1 - 10^{-n}$ | Dimensionless | 3 nines = 99.9 %, 5 nines = 99.999 % |
| **Cost of Downtime** | $C_{down} = D_{hours} \times R_{hourly}$ | Currency (₹) | $R_{hourly}$ = revenue per hour |
| **Capacity Required** | $C_{req} = \lambda_{avg} \cdot P_{factor} \cdot S_{safety}$ | Same unit as load | All dimensionless multipliers |
| **Data Loss Bound** | $D_{loss} \leq RPO$ | Time (seconds) | Synchronous replication → $RPO \to 0$ |
| **RTO + RPO Relationship** | $RTO \geq RPO$ (always) | Time | You cannot lose less data than time-to-recovery to last good backup |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Substitution in LaTeX:** Within the table above, all absolute values / divisibility are written as `\dfrac{}{}` or `/` to avoid the markdown-table pipe `|` breaking the cell. KTU answer scripts that use `|` in a table row will render as broken markdown.

### 2.6 The "Nine" Reference — Memorise This

$$
A = 1 - 10^{-n} \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; D_{year} = 525600 \cdot 10^{-n} \text{ minutes}
$$

| Nines | Availability | Annual Downtime |
|:-----:|:------------:|:----------------|
| 1 | 90 % | 36.5 days |
| 2 | 99 % | 3.65 days |
| 3 | 99.9 % | 8.76 hours |
| 4 | 99.99 % | 52.6 minutes |
| 5 | 99.999 % | 5.26 minutes |
| 6 | 99.9999 % | 31.5 seconds |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom / ISP backbones** are designed for **5 nines (99.999 %)** because every minute of downtime affects millions of subscribers.
- **E-commerce checkout** must achieve ≤ 13 minutes/year downtime (4 nines) to maintain customer trust.
- **Hospital / Air-Traffic-Control** networks target **6 nines** because downtime → loss of life.
- Cloud providers publish **SLAs** as a contractual legal commitment tied to these numbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From MTBF/MTTR to Annual Downtime

**Given:**
- $MTBF$ = mean time between failures (hours)
- $MTTR$ = mean time to repair (hours)

**Step 1** — Express availability as a probability ratio.

The system is **UP** for $MTBF$ hours, then **DOWN** for $MTTR$ hours, repeating.

$$
A = \frac{\text{Total UP time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

**Step 2** — Convert availability to annualised downtime.

One year = $365 \times 24 = 8760$ hours $= 525{,}600$ minutes.

$$
D_{year} = 8760 \cdot (1 - A) \;\; \text{[hours/year]}
$$

$$
D_{year} = 525600 \cdot (1 - A) \;\; \text{[minutes/year]}
$$

**Step 3** — Verify the "n-nines" identity.

For $n$ nines, $A = 1 - 10^{-n}$. Substituting:

$$
D_{year} = 525600 \cdot 10^{-n} \;\; \text{minutes}
$$

For $n = 3$ (three nines):

$$
D_{year} = 525600 \cdot 10^{-3} = 525.6 \text{ minutes} \approx 8.76 \text{ hours}
$$

This matches the standard textbook value of **8.76 hours/year** for 99.9 % availability.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Compute Required MTBF

**Problem:** A cloud switch vendor promises **99.99 % availability**. If the vendor's $MTTR = 1$ hour, what is the minimum acceptable $MTBF$?

**Step 1** — Apply the availability formula.

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = 0.9999
$$

**Step 2** — Cross-multiply.

$$
MTBF = 0.9999 \cdot (MTBF + MTTR)
$$

$$
MTBF = 0.9999 \cdot MTBF + 0.9999 \cdot MTTR
$$

**Step 3** — Isolate $MTBF$.

$$
MTBF - 0.9999 \cdot MTBF = 0.9999 \cdot MTTR
$$

$$
MTBF \cdot (1 - 0.9999) = 0.9999 \cdot MTTR
$$

$$
MTBF = \frac{0.9999 \cdot MTTR}{0.0001}
$$

**Step 4** — Substitute $MTTR = 1$ hour.

$$
MTBF = \frac{0.9999 \times 1}{0.0001} = 9999 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
MTBF = \frac{9999}{24} \approx 416.6 \text{ days}
$$

**Answer:** The switch must run, on average, **416.6 days** between failures. This is the engineering threshold for 4-nines reliability.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Capacity Planning

**Problem:** A video-streaming cloud service averages **50,000 concurrent streams**. Diwali-night peak is **3.2× the average**. Engineering policy mandates a **30 % safety margin**. Compute the provisioned capacity.

**Step 1** — Identify the variables.

$$
\lambda_{avg} = 50{,}000 \text{ streams}, \quad P_{factor} = 3.2, \quad S_{safety} = 1.30
$$

**Step 2** — Apply the capacity equation.

$$
C_{req} = \lambda_{avg} \cdot P_{factor} \cdot S_{safety}
$$

$$
C_{req} = 50{,}000 \times 3.2 \times 1.30
$$

**Step 3** — Compute stepwise.

$$
50{,}000 \times 3.2 = 160{,}000
$$

$$
160{,}000 \times 1.30 = 208{,}000
$$

**Answer:** Provision **208,000 concurrent-stream capacity**. The auto-scaler should trigger scale-out when load exceeds $208{,}000 \div 1.30 \approx 160{,}000$ sustained streams (i.e., before saturation).

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Cost of Downtime

**Problem:** A B2B SaaS company earns **₹5 lakh per hour** in revenue. They target **4-nines availability**. They actually achieved **99.9 %** last year. Calculate the lost revenue.

**Step 1** — Compute expected downtime at 4 nines.

$$
D_{4n} = 525600 \cdot 10^{-4} = 52.56 \text{ min/year} \approx 0.876 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute actual downtime at 3 nines.

$$
D_{3n} = 525600 \cdot 10^{-3} = 525.6 \text{ min/year} = 8.76 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute excess downtime.

$$
D_{excess} = 8.76 - 0.876 = 7.884 \text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute lost revenue.

$$
C_{loss} = D_{excess} \times R_{hourly} = 7.884 \times 5{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
C_{loss} = 39{,}42{,}000 \text{ ₹} \;=\; \text{₹ } 39.42 \text{ lakh}
$$

**Answer:** The single nines improvement from 3 → 4 would have saved **₹ 39.42 lakh/year** — enough to justify buying a second redundant core switch.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **exam-favourite**. The examiner often gives a scenario and asks "what DR strategy should be used?" The answer must justify *both* the RTO/RPO and the cost trade-off.

| Application Tier | RTO Target | RPO Target | Recommended Strategy | Cost Index |
|------------------|:----------:|:----------:|----------------------|:----------:|
| **Tier 0 — Mission Critical** (Banking core, ATC) | < 1 min | ≈ 0 | **Active-Active multi-region, sync replication** | 10× |
| **Tier 1 — Business Critical** (ERP, CRM) | < 1 hour | < 5 min | **Warm standby, async replication** | 4× |
| **Tier 2 — Operational** (Internal portals) | < 8 hours | < 1 hour | **Cold standby, daily backup** | 2× |
| **Tier 3 — Archival** (Logs, compliance) | < 72 hours | < 24 hours | **Backup to tape/glacier** | 1× |

### 3.6 Step-by-Step DR Failover Flow (Logical Derivation)

1. **Detection** — health-check probe (HTTP, TCP, ICMP) fails $N$ times consecutively.
2. **Declaration** — monitoring system declares the site "DOWN" if failures exceed threshold.
3. **DNS / Anycast shift** — global traffic manager reroutes clients to surviving site.
4. **Database promotion** — standby DB is promoted from replica → primary; write traffic resumes.
5. **Application warm-up** — load balancers at the new site auto-scale up to absorb traffic.
6. **Validation** — synthetic transactions confirm business functionality.
7. **Cutover complete** — $RTO$ clock stops; site 1 enters recovery / forensic mode.

The **time taken for steps 1–7** must be $\leq RTO$, and the **data delta** lost at step 4 must be $\leq RPO$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Cloud-Network Design Showing All Four Pillars

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[End Users Global] --> B[Global Traffic Manager DNS Anycast]
    B --> C[Region 1 Primary Site]
    B --> D[Region 2 Secondary Site]
    C --> E[Edge Load Balancer L4]
    D --> F[Edge Load Balancer L4]
    E --> G[Web Tier Stateless VMs Auto Scaled]
    F --> H[Web Tier Stateless VMs Auto Scaled]
    G --> I[App Tier Service Mesh]
    H --> I
    I --> J[DB Primary Sync Replica]
    J --> K[DB Standby Async Replica Region 2]
    K --> L[Object Storage S3 Cross Region Replication]
    L --> M[Backup Vault Glacier Cold Tier]
    M --> N[Disaster Recovery Orchestrator]
    N --> O[Runbook Automation Terraform Ansible]
    O --> P[Observability Prometheus Grafana]
    P --> B
```

### 4.2 Scalability Architecture — Horizontal Auto-Scaling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Metrics CPU Memory Queue Depth] --> AS[Auto Scaler Controller]
    AS -->|Scale Out| P1[Pool Add VM1]
    AS -->|Scale Out| P2[Pool Add VM2]
    AS -->|Scale Out| P3[Pool Add VM3]
    AS -->|Scale In| R1[Pool Remove VM]
    LB[Application Load Balancer] --> P1
    LB --> P2
    LB --> P3
    LB --> R1
    P1 --> DB[(Shared Stateless Backend)]
    P2 --> DB
    P3 --> DB
    R1 --> DB
```

### 4.3 Redundancy Topology — N+1 with Active-Active Edge

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POP_A[Point of Presence A]
        RA1[Router A1 Active]
        RA2[Router A2 Active]
    end
    subgraph POP_B[Point of Presence B Standby]
        RB1[Router B1 Hot Spare]
    end
    USR[Clients] --> RA1
    USR --> RA2
    RA1 <-->|BGP Multihop| RA2
    RA1 <-->|iBGP| RB1
    RA2 <-->|iBGP| RB1
    RA1 --> CORE[Core Backbone]
    RA2 --> CORE
    RB1 --> CORE
```

### 4.4 Disaster Recovery Lifecycle (RTO / RPO Visualisation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Primary Site
    participant Mon as Monitoring
    participant DNS as Global Traffic Manager
    participant S as Secondary Site
    P->>Mon: Heartbeat every 5s
    Mon-->>Mon: 3 consecutive failures
    Mon->>DNS: Declare Primary DOWN
    DNS->>S: Shift traffic to Secondary
    S->>S: Promote standby DB
    S->>S: Warm up app tier
    Mon->>Mon: RTO clock stops
    Note over P,S: Data lost equals RPO
    P->>P: Forensic recovery begins
```

### 4.5 Layered Defence-in-Depth Network Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Internet Edge WAF DDoS Scrubbing] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2 Transit VPC Peering Direct Connect] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3 Hub VPC Firewalls IDS IPS] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4 Spoke VPC Service Mesh mTLS] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5 Workload Tier Confidential Containers] --> L6
    L6[Layer 6 Data Tier Encrypted at Rest KMS Managed Keys]
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cloud DR Orchestrator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Event Source CloudWatch Alerts] --> B[Event Bus SNS Kafka]
    B --> C[DR Orchestrator Lambda Function]
    C --> D{Decision Engine RTO RPO SLA}
    D -->|Failover| E[DNS Update Route53]
    D -->|Failover| F[DB Promote RDS Aurora]
    D -->|Failover| G[Compute Scale Out ASG]
    E --> H[Validation Synthetic Canary]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Pass| I[Cutover Complete Notify PagerDuty]
    H -->|Fail| J[Rollback Invoke Runbook v2]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Distinguish between Vertical Scalability and Horizontal Scalability. State one real-world cloud-service example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vertical Scalability | Horizontal Scalability |
|--------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Adding more resources (CPU, RAM) to a **single node** | Adding **more nodes** to the resource pool |
| Synonym | Scale-up / Scale-down | Scale-out / Scale-in |
| Hardware ceiling | Yes (limited by physical hardware) | Theoretically unbounded |
| Downtime | Often required | Not required |
| Cost curve | Exponential (diminishing returns) | Linear (commodity hardware) |
| Example | AWS EC2 `t3.medium` → `m5.4xlarge` | AWS EC2 Auto-Scaling Group adding 50 more `t3.medium` |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table differences, 1 mark for examples.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define RTO and RPO. How do they influence the choice of disaster-recovery site configuration (cold / warm / hot)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Maximum acceptable duration of service downtime after a disaster. *(1 mark)*
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time before the disaster. *(1 mark)*
- **Mapping to DR strategy:** *(1 mark)*
  - RTO ≈ hours, RPO ≈ hours → **Cold Standby** (cheapest)
  - RTO ≈ minutes, RPO ≈ minutes → **Warm Standby**
  - RTO ≈ seconds, RPO ≈ 0 → **Hot Standby / Active-Active** (most expensive)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the four pillars of cloud network design — scalability, flexibility, redundancy, and disaster recovery — with one network-design technique for each pillar. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A cloud data centre has $MTBF = 30{,}000$ hours and $MTTR = 6$ hours.
>  (i) Compute the system availability as a percentage.
>  (ii) Convert this availability to "nines" representation.
>  (iii) Calculate the expected annual downtime in minutes.
>  (iv) If revenue is ₹ 8 lakh/hour, compute the annual cost of downtime. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks (1.75 per pillar)**

1. **Scalability** — *Technique:* Horizontal auto-scaling group with stateless web tier behind L4 load balancer; metrics-driven scale-out/scale-in.
2. **Flexibility** — *Technique:* Software-Defined Networking (SDN) with OpenFlow / Cisco ACI controller; topology re-programmed via REST APIs in minutes.
3. **Redundancy** — *Technique:* N+1 power and cooling with dual-homed network paths using BGP multi-homing; active-active edge routers.
4. **Disaster Recovery** — *Technique:* Multi-region active-active deployment with synchronous database replication and Route53 health-check-driven DNS failover; RPO ≈ 0, RTO < 60 s.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Availability = 2 marks**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{30000}{30000 + 6} = \frac{30000}{30006} \approx 0.9998
$$

$$
A \approx 99.98\,\%
$$

**(ii) Nines representation = 1 mark**

$$
0.9998 \approx 3.98 \text{ nines} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{roughly "four nines"}
$$

**(iii) Annual downtime in minutes = 2 marks**

$$
D_{year} = 525600 \times (1 - 0.9998) = 525600 \times 0.0002 = 105.12 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(iv) Annual cost of downtime = 2 marks**

$$
D_{hours} = \frac{105.12}{60} = 1.752 \text{ hours/year}
$$

$$
C_{loss} = 1.752 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 14{,}01{,}600 \; \approx \; \text{₹ } 14.02 \text{ lakh}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Disaster Recovery lifecycle from detection to cutover. Identify the exact steps where RTO clock and RPO data-loss bound are measured. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** An e-commerce portal targets **99.99 % availability** (4 nines). Currently they achieve **99.9 %** (3 nines). The portal earns **₹ 12 lakh/hour**.
>  (i) Compute the **excess annual downtime** between current and target.
>  (ii) Compute the **annual revenue loss** at the current 3-nines level.
>  (iii) The CTO is considering a 2N multi-region deployment costing ₹ 4 crore/year. Justify with payback whether the investment is financially sound. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**
- Step 1 (1 mark): Detection — health-check probes.
- Step 2 (1 mark): Declaration — consecutive-failure threshold.
- Step 3 (1 mark): Traffic shift — DNS / Anycast reroute. **RTO clock starts here.**
- Step 4 (1 mark): DB promotion — synchronous replicas become primary. **RPO data loss is fixed here.**
- Step 5 (1 mark): App warm-up — auto-scale and health probes.
- Step 6 (1 mark): Validation — synthetic canary transactions.
- Step 7 (1 mark): Cutover complete — RTO clock stops. (1 mark for the block diagram itself; describe mermaid-style sequence as in §4.4)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Excess annual downtime = 2 marks**

$$
D_{3n} = 525600 \cdot 10^{-3} = 525.6 \text{ min}
$$

$$
D_{4n} = 525600 \cdot 10^{-4} = 52.56 \text{ min}
$$

$$
D_{excess} = 525.6 - 52.56 = 473.04 \text{ min} = 7.884 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Annual revenue loss at 3 nines = 2 marks**

$$
C_{loss} = 7.884 \times 12{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 94{,}60{,}800 \approx \text{₹ } 94.6 \text{ lakh/year}
$$

**(iii) Payback justification = 3 marks**

If the 2N deployment eliminates the 94.6 lakh/year loss, then:

$$
\text{Payback period} = \frac{4{,}00{,}00{,}000}{94{,}60{,}800} \approx 4.23 \text{ years}
$$

Adding indirect benefits (customer trust, regulatory compliance, brand value, avoided SLA penalties), the **3-to-4 year payback is highly acceptable** for a tier-1 e-commerce platform. **Decision: Invest.** The 2N strategy is financially sound. *(1 mark for payback calc, 1 mark for indirect benefits, 1 mark for verdict.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing scalability with elasticity.** Scalability is the *capability*; elasticity is the *automated behaviour*. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for interchange.
> 2. **Wrong direction of nines.** Writing "5 nines = 99.9 %" instead of 99.999 % is a classic blunder costing **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Inverting MTBF / MTTR.** The formula is $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$, not $A = MTTR / (MTBF + MTTR)$. Examiners spot this and deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting units in cost-of-downtime.** Always show the unit conversion (minutes → hours) explicitly. Marks are awarded for the *unit* step, not just the final number.
> 5. **RTO/RPO reversed in DR questions.** RTO = *time* the system is down; RPO = *time* of data you can rewind. Reversing them = **full 2-mark loss** in Part A.
> 6. **Active-Active described as "twice the cost".** It is approximately 1.6–2×, not exactly 2×. Saying "double the cost" loses **0.5 mark** for imprecision.
> 7. **Not drawing a diagram in Part B.** KTU mandates diagrams for 14-mark questions. Skipping the block diagram costs at least **2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **30-Second Rapid Revision — Print This Section Before Exam**

- ✅ **Scalability = capability to grow**; **Elasticity = automated mechanism** that does it.
- ✅ **Vertical** = scale-up/down (one node, more power); **Horizontal** = scale-out/in (more nodes, same power).
- ✅ **Statelessness is mandatory** for horizontal scaling.
- ✅ **Capacity formula:** $C = \lambda_{avg} \times P_{factor} \times S_{safety}$.
- ✅ **Redundancy grades:** N < N+1 < N+M < 2N < 2N+1.
- ✅ **Active-Active** doubles throughput; **Active-Passive** has hot standby.
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- ✅ **Nines of availability:** $A_n = 1 - 10^{-n}$.
- ✅ **Annual downtime** for $n$ nines = $525600 \times 10^{-n}$ **minutes**.
- ✅ **RTO** = max downtime allowed; **RPO** = max data loss allowed. **Always RTO ≥ RPO.**
- ✅ **Cold standby** = hours RTO, low cost. **Warm standby** = minutes RTO, medium cost. **Hot standby / A-A** = seconds RTO, high cost.
- ✅ **Cost of downtime** $C = D_{hours} \times R_{hourly}$.
- ✅ **Synchronous replication** → RPO ≈ 0; **Asynchronous** → RPO > 0.
- ✅ **BGP multi-homing + Anycast DNS** are the canonical multi-region DR techniques.
- ✅ **4 nines = 52.6 min/yr**; **5 nines = 5.26 min/yr** — these are the two numbers examiners love.
- ✅ **Always include a block diagram** in any 14-mark answer; never skip the unit step in numericals.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security in Cloud Networks: Cloud-Native Security Controls, Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS), Identity and Access Management (IAM), Encryption Techniques, and Zero-Trust Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT521 - NETWORK FUNDAMENTALS FOR CLOUD
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security in Cloud Networks: Cloud-Native Security Controls, Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS), Identity and Access Management (IAM), Encryption Techniques, and Zero-Trust Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overviews

## 1.1 Cloud-Native Security Controls

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Cloud-Native Security Controls are security mechanisms and services that are **designed, built, and delivered as integral components of a cloud platform** rather than retrofitted from on-premises environments. They leverage cloud properties such as elasticity, API-driven orchestration, micro-segmentation, and shared responsibility models to protect workloads, data, and infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, cloud-native security is taught as a *layered, programmable, and policy-as-code* discipline — students must remember that it is **NOT** the same as traditional perimeter security.

**Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
Think of a modern, smart apartment building. Traditional on-premises security is like having a single guard at the front gate — once past the gate, the attacker is "inside." Cloud-native security is like a building where **every door, every window, and every room has its own smart lock, motion sensor, and camera** that all talk to a central AI system. The security follows the asset, not the perimeter. Even if an attacker gets into the lobby, every individual apartment remains locked and monitored.

**Standard Cloud-Native Security Control Categories (Must-Memorize for KTU):**
- **Identity & Access Management (IAM)** — who can do what
- **Network Security Groups (NSG) / Security Lists** — virtual firewalls at the NIC/subnet level
- **Web Application Firewalls (WAF)** — Layer 7 protection for HTTP/HTTPS
- **DDoS Protection** — e.g., AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection
- **Data Encryption (At-Rest & In-Transit)** — KMS, HSM-backed encryption
- **Key Management Services (KMS)** — centralized key lifecycle
- **Security Information & Event Management (SIEM)** — centralized logging
- **Configuration & Compliance Monitoring** — e.g., AWS Config, Azure Policy

---

## 1.2 Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems (IDPS)

**Formal Definition:**
An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** is a security mechanism that **monitors network or system activities for malicious behavior, policy violations, or unauthorized access** and generates alerts. An **Intrusion Prevention System (IPS)** goes one step further by **automatically blocking** the detected threat in real-time, often by dropping packets, resetting connections, or reconfiguring firewalls.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
An IDS is like a **CCTV camera in a jewelry store** — it sees the thief break the glass, triggers an alarm, and records the footage for the police. An IPS is like a **CCTV camera connected to an automated glass door that slams shut the moment the thief reaches for the jewelry** — it not only detects but also actively intervenes to stop the crime in progress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Distinction:**
> - **IDS = Detect + Alert** (passive monitoring)
> - **IPS = Detect + Alert + Block/Prevent** (inline active response)
> - **IDPS** = umbrella term for systems that can do both

**The Four Detection Methodologies (High-Yield for KTU):**
1. **Signature-Based Detection** — uses known attack patterns (like antivirus signatures). Fast, but useless against zero-days.
2. **Anomaly-Based Detection** — establishes a baseline of "normal" behavior; flags deviations. Catches unknown attacks but prone to false positives.
3. **Stateful Protocol Inspection** — understands the expected state of a protocol (e.g., TCP handshake) and flags violations.
4. **Behavioral / Heuristic Analysis** — uses ML/AI to learn behavior over time.

---

## 1.3 Identity and Access Management (IAM)

**Formal Definition:**
**Identity and Access Management (IAM)** is the framework of **policies, processes, and technologies** that ensures the **right individuals (or services) get the right access to the right resources at the right time, for the right reasons.** It is the cornerstone of cloud security and the foundation of the Zero-Trust model.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
IAM is the **HR + Security Pass Office of a corporate campus**. When a new employee joins:
- **HR verifies identity** (Authentication) — checks the Aadhaar/PAN/ID card
- **Security issues a badge** (Authorization) — the badge has access only to the floors/rooms of their job
- **HR enforces time limits** (Time-bound access) — interns have 6-month badges
- **HR revokes on exit** (Lifecycle Management) — badge is destroyed the day they leave
- **Security logs every door swipe** (Auditing) — forensically traceable

**The Three Pillars of IAM (AAAL — KTU Favourite Mnemonic):**
- **A**uthentication — "Are you who you say you are?" (Passwords, MFA, biometrics)
- **A**uthorization — "What are you allowed to do?" (RBAC, ABAC, policies)
- **A**ccountability — "What did you actually do?" (Logging, auditing)
- **L**ifecycle — Provisioning → Modification → Deprovisioning

---

## 1.4 Encryption Techniques

**Formal Definition:**
**Encryption** is the cryptographic process of transforming readable plaintext into unreadable ciphertext using a mathematical algorithm (cipher) and a key, such that only parties holding the correct key can reverse the process (decryption) and recover the original plaintext. In cloud networks, encryption protects data **at-rest** (stored), **in-transit** (moving), and **in-use** (processing).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Encryption is like sending a **locked steel box through a public courier**. The box (ciphertext) is unreadable to anyone in transit. Only the recipient has the matching key (decryption key) to open it. If the courier is robbed, the thief gets a useless metal box.

**The Two Major Families (High-Yield KTU):**
1. **Symmetric Encryption** — Same key encrypts and decrypts. Fast, used for bulk data.
   - Examples: **AES-128, AES-256, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20**
2. **Asymmetric Encryption** — Mathematically paired public + private key. Slower, used for key exchange and digital signatures.
   - Examples: **RSA-2048, RSA-4096, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), Diffie-Hellman**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modern Cloud Reality (KTU 2024):** Real systems use **hybrid encryption** — e.g., TLS 1.3 uses asymmetric (RSA/ECC) only to securely exchange a symmetric session key (AES), then all bulk data is encrypted with the fast symmetric key. This gives both security AND speed.

---

## 1.5 Zero-Trust Architecture (ZTA)

**Formal Definition:**
**Zero-Trust Architecture** is a security model that assumes **no implicit trust** for any entity — user, device, application, or network — regardless of whether it is inside or outside the traditional network perimeter. Every access request is **continuously verified** using the principle of *"never trust, always verify."* Formally defined in **NIST SP 800-207**.

**The Three Core Principles of Zero-Trust (Must-Memorize):**
1. **Verify Explicitly** — Authenticate and authorize every request using identity, device posture, location, and context.
2. **Least Privilege Access (LPA)** — Grant the minimum permissions required, for the shortest time necessary (Just-In-Time access).
3. **Assume Breach** — Operate as if the attacker is already inside. Segment aggressively. Monitor everything. Encrypt end-to-end.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Zero-Trust is like an **ultra-secure corporate office where even the CEO is frisked at every door**. There is no VIP pass. Every door, even the pantry door, requires badge + fingerprint + purpose-of-visit. Past behavior (e.g., "this person entered yesterday so they can enter today") grants **zero** automatic trust. The system constantly re-checks: *is the badge still valid? is the device patched? is the network still trusted?*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Zero-Trust is the *philosophical* backbone of modern cloud security. AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud have all built Zero-Trust-aligned services (e.g., AWS IAM Identity Center, Azure Entra ID, Google BeyondCorp).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Cloud-Native Security Controls — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 The Shared Responsibility Model (SRM)

This is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU cloud security questions. Cloud security is split between the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **Customer (you)**.

| Layer / Service Model | Provider Secures (CSP) | Customer Secures (You) |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** (e.g., EC2) | Physical DC, Hypervisor, Network fabric | OS, Apps, Data, IAM, Network config, Firewall rules |
| **PaaS** (e.g., RDS) | + OS, Runtime, Middleware | Apps, Data, IAM, Access policies |
| **SaaS** (e.g., Gmail) | + Apps, Runtime, Middleware, OS | Data, User access, Device security |
| **Always Customer** | — | **Data, Devices, Accounts, Identity** |

### 2.1.2 The Defense-in-Depth Layers (Cloud-Native)

Operating from outermost to innermost:
1. **Perimeter:** DDoS protection (Shield, Cloud Armor)
2. **Network:** VPC, Subnets, NSGs, WAF, Private Link
3. **Identity:** IAM roles, MFA, Conditional Access
4. **Application:** WAF rules, API Gateway throttling, input validation
5. **Data:** Encryption (KMS), tokenization, DLP
6. **Host/Compute:** OS patching, EDR (Endpoint Detection & Response)
7. **Monitoring:** CloudTrail, GuardDuty, SIEM, SOAR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Every major cloud breach (Capital One 2019, SolarWinds 2020, Microsoft 2023) exploited a **failure of one of these layers**, often misconfigured IAM or unencrypted data. Cloud-native controls are designed to **fail safely** when one layer is breached.

---

## 2.2 IDPS — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Four Operational Modes of IDPS

| Mode | Placement | Action | Latency | Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Network-Based IDS (NIDS)** | Promiscuous tap on a switch/SPAN port | Passive monitoring, alerts | **Zero added latency** | Attacker traffic is allowed to reach target before being blocked |
| **Network-Based IPS (NIPS)** | Inline in the data path | Real-time packet drop/block | **Adds ~1-5 ms** | Misconfiguration can drop legitimate traffic |
| **Host-Based IDS (HIDS)** | Installed on a single endpoint (server) | Monitors logs, file integrity, syscalls | Negligible | Per-host resource cost; bypassed if host is compromised |
| **Wireless IDS (WIDS)** | Monitors RF spectrum | Detects rogue APs, evil twins | Low | Coverage gaps in dense RF environments |

### 2.2.2 Detection Methodology Trade-off (High-Yield KTU Table)

| Method | Known Attacks | Unknown/Zero-Day | False Positives | Speed | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Signature-Based | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Useless | Very Low | Very Fast | Low |
| Anomaly-Based | ⚠️ Moderate | ✅ Good | High | Moderate | High (baseline training) |
| Stateful Inspection | ✅ Good for protocol attacks | ⚠️ Moderate | Low | Fast | Moderate |
| Behavioral/ML-Based | ✅ Good | ✅ Very Good | Medium | Slower at training, fast at inference | Very High |

### 2.2.3 The IDPS Workflow (5-Stage Pipeline)

1. **Collection** — Capture packets / logs / flow records (NetFlow, sFlow)
2. **Normalization** — Convert heterogeneous data into a common format
3. **Detection Engine** — Apply signatures, anomaly models, state machines
4. **Alerting/Logging** — Generate alert, write to SIEM
5. **Response** — For IPS: block, drop, reset, quarantine. For IDS: notify, ticket.

---

## 2.3 IAM — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 The Three Authorization Models (Comparison)

| Model | Full Form | Logic | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Access based on **role** assigned to user | "All doctors can read patient records" |
| **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Access based on **attributes** (user, resource, environment, action) | "Doctors in Cardiology dept. can read records of patients in their ward during shift hours" |
| **PBAC** | Policy-Based Access Control | Access defined by **declarative policy** files (Rego, Cedar) | AWS IAM policies written in JSON |

### 2.3.2 The AAA + Lifecycle Framework (Memorize for KTU)

- **Authentication (AuthN)** → **"Prove identity"** → Factors: Knowledge (password), Possession (OTP token), Inherence (fingerprint). MFA = combining ≥ 2.
- **Authorization (AuthZ)** → **"Define allowed actions"** → Enforced via policies, roles, scopes.
- **Accountability (Auditing)** → **"Trace what happened"** → CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log.
- **Lifecycle** → Joiner (provision) → Mover (modify) → Leaver (deprovision) workflows.

### 2.3.3 Federated Identity & SSO (Critical for Multi-Cloud)

- **SSO (Single Sign-On):** One login → access many apps (e.g., Okta, Azure Entra ID, Google Workspace).
- **Federation:** Trust established between an **Identity Provider (IdP)** and a **Service Provider (SP)** using standards like **SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect (OIDC)**.

---

## 2.4 Encryption Techniques — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.4.1 The Hybrid Encryption Flow (Used in TLS 1.3)

Modern cloud security **always** uses hybrid encryption. The KTU-favoured flow:

1. Client opens TLS connection.
2. Server presents **digital certificate** containing its **public key** (asymmetric).
3. Client verifies certificate against a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.
4. Client generates a random **symmetric session key**.
5. Client encrypts the session key with server's public key (asymmetric).
6. Server decrypts using its **private key** to recover the session key.
7. **All further communication uses symmetric encryption (AES-256-GCM)** for speed.

### 2.4.2 Encryption Types by State of Data (KTU Must-Know)

| State | Description | Cloud Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **At-Rest** | Data stored on disk/database/storage | S3 server-side encryption, RDS TDE, EBS encryption with KMS |
| **In-Transit** | Data moving over network | TLS 1.3, IPsec VPN, MACsec |
| **In-Use** | Data being processed in CPU/RAM | Confidential Computing (Intel SGX, AWS Nitro Enclaves, Azure Confidential VMs) |

### 2.4.3 Common Algorithms — KTU Reference Table

| Algorithm | Type | Key Size | Speed | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AES-256** | Symmetric (block) | 256 bits | Very fast | Bulk data encryption (S3, EBS) |
| **ChaCha20** | Symmetric (stream) | 256 bits | Fast on mobile/no AES-NI | TLS, mobile apps |
| **RSA-2048** | Asymmetric | 2048 bits | Slow | Digital signatures, small data |
| **ECC (P-256)** | Asymmetric | 256 bits (equivalent to RSA-3072) | Faster than RSA | Mobile, IoT, TLS |
| **SHA-256** | Hash (not encryption!) | 256-bit output | Very fast | Integrity, digital signatures |
| **Bcrypt / Argon2** | Password hashing | Variable | Intentionally slow | Storing user passwords |

### 2.4.4 Key Formulas & Metrics (Engineering Utility)

$$ \text{Key Space} = 2^n \quad \text{where } n = \text{key length in bits} $$

$$\text{AES-256 key space} = 2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77} \text{ possible keys}$$

$$\text{Work Factor} = \frac{\text{Time to break key}}{\text{Time to generate key}} \quad \text{(higher is better security)}$$

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** AES-256 is used by the U.S. NSA for **TOP SECRET** data. RSA-2048 is the de-facto standard for digital certificates on the web. The "bits of security" is the metric used to compare algorithms (e.g., ECC-256 ≈ RSA-3072 in security strength).

---

## 2.5 Zero-Trust Architecture — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.5.1 The Zero-Trust Logical Components (NIST SP 800-207)

1. **Policy Engine (PE)** — The brain. Decides whether to allow/deny a request based on policies.
2. **Policy Administrator (PA)** — The executor. Carries out PE's decision (issues session token or denies).
3. **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** — The muscle. Sits in the data path and gates the request (e.g., a NextGen firewall, API gateway).

### 2.5.2 The Trust Algorithm (Conceptual Formula)

A Zero-Trust decision is a **continuous, weighted, contextual evaluation**. Formally:

$$ T_{request} = f(I, D, L, R, B, T, C) $$

Where:
- $I$ = Identity assertion (user/service principal)
- $D$ = Device posture (patch level, EDR status, jailbreak flag)
- $L$ = Location / network (geo, ASN, IP reputation)
- $R$ = Resource sensitivity (data classification)
- $B$ = Behavioral baseline (anomaly score)
- $T$ = Time / session age
- $C$ = Compliance / regulatory context

If $T_{request}$ ≥ policy threshold → **Allow with scoped access**.
If $T_{request}$ < threshold → **Deny / Step-up Auth / Quarantine**.

### 2.5.3 Zero-Trust Maturity Levels (KTU Favourite)

| Level | Description | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **L0 — Traditional** | Perimeter-only, VPN-trust | "Inside = trusted" |
| **L1 — Initial Zero-Trust** | MFA on all users, basic segmentation | Identity-aware |
| **L2 — Advanced** | Microsegmentation, device posture checks, conditional access | Identity + device + context |
| **L3 — Optimal** | Continuous adaptive risk-based access, full microsegmentation, SDP | Full NIST ZTA implementation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Questions now ask to *compare* traditional perimeter security with Zero-Trust. Remember: **Perimeter security = "trust but verify inside"**, while **Zero-Trust = "never trust, always verify"** — even after authentication.

### 2.5.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google BeyondCorp (2014)** — Pioneered Zero-Trust for Google's 100,000+ employees. Replaced VPN entirely.
- **AWS Verified Access** — ZTA-style access to private apps without VPN.
- **Microsoft Zero Trust Maturity Model** — Adopted by U.S. Federal Government (Executive Order 14028, May 2021).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrices

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The "When to Use What" Decision Table

This matrix is a **derivation of the engineering trade-offs** a KTU student must internalize:

| Threat / Requirement | Best Cloud-Native Control | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Brute-force login attempts | IAM + WAF rate limiting + MFA | Authentication-level control stops at the door |
| Insider data exfiltration | DLP (Data Loss Prevention) + UEBA + Encryption at-rest | Catches authorized users doing unauthorized actions |
| DDoS volumetric attack | Cloud-native DDoS protection (Shield/Cloud Armor) | Edge scrubbing, absorbs Tbps-scale attacks |
| Lateral movement after breach | Microsegmentation + Zero-Trust PEPs | Stops spread via subnet/user rules |
| Data stolen from stolen disk | Full-disk encryption with KMS-managed keys | Disk is unreadable without KMS key access |
| Zero-day exploit | Anomaly-based NIPS + EDR | Signature systems miss it; behavioral catches deviation |
| Man-in-the-middle on cloud API | TLS 1.3 + mTLS (mutual TLS) | Both client and server authenticate, key is ephemeral |
| Privilege escalation | Least privilege RBAC + JIT + PAM | Removes standing admin access |
| Ransomware on cloud VMs | Immutable backups + IDPS + EDR | Backup cannot be modified by attacker; IDPS detects the encryption behavior |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: How TLS 1.3 Hybrid Encryption Works (Numerically Annotated)

This is a **classic KTU 14-marker territory** topic. Master this derivation.

**Step 1 — TCP Connection Established (Out of TLS scope)**
Client and server have a reliable TCP connection.

**Step 2 — Client Hello (Key Exchange Initiation)**
Client sends: `ClientHello` containing:
- Supported cipher suites (e.g., `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`)
- Client random nonce
- **Key share** — client's ephemeral **ECDHE public key** (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman Ephemeral)

**Step 3 — Server Hello + Certificate**
Server responds with:
- Selected cipher suite
- Server's **ECDHE public key**
- **Digital certificate** (X.509) containing server's long-term public key (RSA-2048 or ECC P-256)
- Server random nonce

**Step 4 — Certificate Verification (AuthN)**
Client verifies certificate against trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)** chain.
Validation checks:
- Certificate not expired (validity dates)
- Domain name matches (SAN — Subject Alternative Name)
- Not revoked (OCSP or CRL check)
- Signature valid (decrypt with CA's public key → recover hash → compare with recomputed hash)

**Step 5 — ECDHE Key Agreement (Forward Secrecy Generation)**
Both client and server combine their private + public ECDHE keys to derive a **shared secret**. Formally:

$$ \text{SharedSecret} = (P_{\text{client}})^{k_{\text{server}}} \pmod{p} = (P_{\text{server}})^{k_{\text{client}}} \pmod{p} $$

Where $P$ is the curve base point, $k$ is each side's ephemeral private key.

This is run through a **Key Derivation Function (HKDF)** to produce the **session key** $K_s$.

**Step 6 — Encrypted Handshake (TLS 1.3 innovation)**
In TLS 1.3, the server's certificate is itself **encrypted with the session key** before transmission — even before the handshake completes. This reduces the visible metadata to eavesdroppers.

**Step 7 — Symmetric Bulk Encryption Begins**
All subsequent application data (HTTP, API calls) is encrypted using:

$$ C_i = E_{K_s}(\text{Nonce}_i \parallel P_i \parallel AAD) $$

Where:
- $E$ = AES-256-GCM authenticated encryption
- $K_s$ = the symmetric session key derived in Step 5
- $P_i$ = i-th plaintext chunk
- $AAD$ = Additional Authenticated Data (header)
- $C_i$ = i-th ciphertext chunk

**Step 8 — Session Closure**
On disconnect, both sides **destroy the session key**. The ECDHE keys are also ephemeral and never reused — this is **Forward Secrecy**: even if the long-term private key is later compromised, past sessions cannot be decrypted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight (Why Hybrid?):** Asymmetric encryption is ~1000x slower than symmetric at equivalent security. By using asymmetric only for the key exchange (Step 5) and symmetric for bulk data (Step 7), TLS achieves the best of both worlds: secure key establishment + high throughput.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Designing a Zero-Trust Access Decision

A KTU 14-marker question often asks: *"A user wants to access a confidential S3 bucket. Walk through the Zero-Trust decision."*

**Step 1 — Request Initiated**
User (Identity: `alice@corp.com`) from device (MacBook, corporate EDR active) tries to `GET s3://finance-prod/q4-report.csv` from IP `203.0.113.42` (Singapore).

**Step 2 — PEP Intercepts**
The PEP (e.g., AWS Verified Access, CloudFront + Lambda@Edge) intercepts the request **before** it reaches S3.

**Step 3 — Context Collection**
PEP collects signals:
- $I$ = Alice's SSO assertion, MFA timestamp = 2 minutes ago
- $D$ = EDR green, OS patched, disk encrypted = true
- $L$ = Singapore (matches user's office geo)
- $R$ = S3 bucket tagged `Confidential:Restricted`
- $B$ = Alice's typical access pattern: reads S3 buckets at ~2 PM UTC; current time = 2:14 PM UTC (within baseline)
- $T$ = Session age = 12 minutes (no timeout)
- $C$ = SOC2 compliance requires MFA + encryption for `Confidential:Restricted`

**Step 4 — Policy Evaluation**
PE evaluates against the rule:
> *Allow access to `Confidential:Restricted` S3 objects IF (MFA in last 15 min) AND (device compliant) AND (geo in {SG, US, IN}) AND (behavioral risk score ≤ 70).*

**Step 5 — Decision**
- MFA recent? ✅ Yes (2 min)
- Device compliant? ✅ Yes (EDR green, patched)
- Geo in allow-list? ✅ Yes (Singapore)
- Behavioral risk score? **68** (computed from: 30 login attempts/hour × 0.5 + 0 anomalies × 1 = 15 base, + 53 contextual) ≤ 70 ✅

**Decision: ALLOW** with **scoped access**:
- Read-only (no `s3:DeleteObject`)
- Time-bound session token (TTL = 1 hour)
- All actions logged to CloudTrail

**Step 6 — Continuous Verification**
Every 60 seconds, PEP re-evaluates. If Alice's EDR goes red (laptop compromised), the session is **immediately terminated**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Derivation: IDPS Placement Math

**Scenario:** A cloud VPC has **N = 5** subnets, each with an average of **T = 200 hosts** running critical workloads. Compute the optimal placement of NIPS sensors.

**Step 1 — Compute the Aggregate Throughput**

$$ R_{total} = N \times T \times \bar{r}_{host} = 5 \times 200 \times 5 \text{ Mbps} = 5000 \text{ Mbps} = 5 \text{ Gbps} $$

Where $\bar{r}_{host}$ = average throughput per host = 5 Mbps.

**Step 2 — Compute Number of NIPS Sensors Required**
Assume each NIPS sensor capacity $C_{sensor} = 2$ Gbps. The minimum sensor count is:

$$ S_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{R_{total}}{C_{sensor}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{5}{2} \right\rceil = 3 \text{ sensors} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Latency Introduced**
Inline NIPS adds a processing delay of $d = 3$ ms per packet (industry standard). For a TCP ACK traveling client → server → client:

$$ \text{RTT}_{new} = \text{RTT}_{base} + 2d = 20 \text{ ms} + 2(3) = 26 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 4 — Engineering Trade-off Decision**
If $R_{total} > 10$ Gbps, switch to **passive IDS + dedicated DDoS scrubbing service** because inline NIPS cannot keep up without packet loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Inline NIPS placement is a **cost vs. latency vs. coverage** trade-off. Many production deployments use **2-3 inline NIPS** for north-south traffic and **distributed HIDS agents** (one per host) for east-west coverage.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: Key Rotation Strategy (KMS)

**Scenario:** A cloud KMS-encrypted EBS volume must be rotated every **90 days** per compliance. Compute the operational cost of rotation.

**Step 1 — Rotation Frequency**

$$ f_{rotation} = \frac{365 \text{ days}}{90 \text{ days}} \approx 4.06 \text{ rotations per year} $$

**Step 2 — Cost per Rotation (AWS KMS reference pricing)**
Let $c_{KMS} = \$1.00$ per 10,000 API calls. A typical rotation involves:
- 1 `GenerateDataKey` call
- 1 `Encrypt` call per GB of data
- 1 `Decrypt` call per GB during re-encryption

For $V = 100$ GB volume:

$$ C_{rotation} = c_{KMS} \times \left(2 + 2V\right) = 1.00 \times (2 + 200) = \$20.20 \text{ per rotation} $$

Wait — that's a rate. Re-deriving the cost per rotation:

$$ C_{rotation} = \$0.20 \times V_{GB} + \text{control plane fees} $$

For a 1 TB volume (1024 GB):

$$ C_{rotation} = 0.20 \times 1024 + 1.00 = \$205.80 \text{ per rotation} $$

**Step 3 — Annual Cost**

$$ C_{annual} = f_{rotation} \times C_{rotation} = 4.06 \times 205.80 = \$835.55 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** More frequent rotation (e.g., daily) increases security against key compromise but multiplies the cost ~90x. KTU-favourite question: *"Why not rotate daily?"* — Answer: cost, operational risk of re-encryption failures, and marginal security gain if the key is KMS-managed (HSM-protected).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** Notice how the PEP sits in the data path BEFORE the resource. This is the architectural difference between Zero-Trust and traditional security — there is **no implicit trust zone**.

## 4.5 Hybrid Encryption (TLS 1.3) Handshake Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server
    C->>S: ClientHello + ECDHE Public Key
    S->>C: ServerHello + ECDHE Public Key + Encrypted Certificate
    Note over C,S: Both Compute Shared Secret via ECDHE
    C->>S: Finished - Encrypted with Session Key
    S->>C: Finished - Encrypted with Session Key
    Note over C,S: Bulk Data Encrypted with AES-256-GCM
    C->>S: Application Data (Encrypted)
    S->>C: Application Data (Encrypted)
    Note over C,S: Session Ends - Keys Destroyed (Forward Secrecy)
```
